# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Define market segmentation. List the four major bases of consumer market segmentation.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Market Segmentation** is the process of dividing a heterogeneous market into smaller, homogeneous groups of buyers who share similar needs, characteristics, or buying behavior, and who are likely to exhibit similar responses to marketing actions.

**Four major bases of consumer market segmentation:**

1. **Demographic Segmentation** — based on age, gender, income, education, occupation.
2. **Geographic Segmentation** — based on region, climate, urban/rural, population density.
3. **Psychographic Segmentation** — based on lifestyle, values, personality, social class.
4. **Behavioral Segmentation** — based on usage, loyalty, occasion, benefits sought, readiness.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for listing the four bases with brief detail.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between demographic segmentation and psychographic segmentation with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Demographic | Psychographic |
|--------|-------------|----------------|
| Definition | Divides market based on **objective, measurable population traits** | Divides market based on **subjective traits** like attitude, lifestyle, values |
| Variables | Age, gender, income, education | Lifestyle, personality, values, social class |
| Measurement | Easy, uses secondary data | Difficult, needs primary research |
| Example | A watch company targeting **men aged 25–40 earning ₹5L+** | A watch company targeting **aspirational, status-conscious young professionals** |

*(3 marks — 2 for table + 1 for examples.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU University Examination (ESE) pattern requires **internal choice**. Two independent alternatives are provided.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four major bases of consumer market segmentation** in detail. Give **one Indian brand example** for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **Kotler's six criteria for effective segmentation**. Why is each criterion important?

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Question A

**Part (a) — Four Major Bases of Consumer Market Segmentation**

> **1. Demographic Segmentation**
> - Divides the market on the basis of variables such as **age, gender, income, education, occupation, family size, religion, marital status, and generation**.
> - Most widely used base because demographic data is **easy to obtain, measurable, and strongly linked** to consumer needs and product usage.
> - *Indian Example:* **PepsiCo** — "Lay's" offers *Lay's Classic Salted* for mass market, *Lay's Magic Masala* for Indian taste, and *Lay's Gourmet* for premium urban consumers — segmented by income and taste preference.

> **2. Geographic Segmentation**
> - Divides the market on the basis of **region, country, state, city size, climate, and population density** (urban / semi-urban / rural).
> - *Indian Example:* **Maruti Suzuki** — sells the *Maruti Omni* primarily in rural and semi-urban markets, while the *Maruti Baleno* is targeted at urban metro markets.

> **3. Psychographic Segmentation**
> - Divides the market on the basis of **lifestyle, values, attitudes, personality, and social class**.
> - *Indian Example:* **Royal Enfield** — positions itself for the "rugged, free-spirited adventurer" lifestyle, while **Honda Activa** targets a "convenient, family-oriented" lifestyle.

> **4. Behavioral Segmentation**
> - Divides the market on the basis of **actual consumer behavior** — usage rate, loyalty status, occasions, benefits sought, readiness to buy, and attitude.
> - *Indian Example:* **Amazon India** — segments its customers into *Prime Loyalists* (high-frequency), *Bargain Hunters* (deal-driven), and *Window Shoppers* (low conversion) using behavioral data.

*(7 marks — 1 mark per base definition + 1 mark per Indian example + 1 mark for summary table.)*

---

**Part (b) — Kotler's Six Criteria for Effective Segmentation (MADSA-S)**

> **1. Measurable (M)**
> The size, purchasing power, and characteristics of the segment must be **quantifiable**. Marketers must be able to estimate market potential.
> *Importance:* Without measurement, the firm cannot evaluate the segment's potential or ROI.

> **2. Accessible (A)**
> The segment must be **reachable** through distribution, communication, and physical channels.
> *Importance:* A segment that cannot be served is commercially useless.

> **3. Substantial / Large (S)**
> The segment must be **large and profitable enough** to justify the cost of serving it.
> *Importance:* Tiny segments may not generate enough revenue to cover marketing costs.

> **4. Differentiable (D)**
> Segments must be **conceptually distinct** and respond differently to different marketing mixes.
> *Importance:* If two segments react identically to a marketing program, they are not really separate.

> **5. Actionable (A)**
> The firm must be able to **design effective programs** to attract and serve the chosen segment.
> *Importance:* A segment is useless if the firm lacks the resources or capability to target it.

> **6. Stable (S)**
> The segment should be **stable over time** so that long-term marketing plans can be made.
> *Importance:* Highly volatile segments make revenue forecasting unreliable.

> *Concluding line:* A segment that satisfies all six criteria is termed a **"viable target segment"** and forms the foundation of the STP framework.

*(7 marks — 1 mark per criterion + 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A company is evaluating three market segments. The data is given below:

| Segment | Market Size (₹ Cr) | Growth Rate (%) | Profit Margin (%) | Entry Cost (₹ Cr) |
|---------|---------------------|------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| X       | 60                  | 10               | 18                 | 4                   |
| Y       | 90                  | 6                | 14                 | 7                   |
| Z       | 40                  | 22               | 25                 | 3                   |

**Using the Segment Attractiveness Score formula, rank the segments and recommend the best target. Justify your decision.**

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **levels (patterns) of market segmentation** with a suitable example for each.

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Question B

**Part (a) — Numerical on Segment Attractiveness Score (SAS)**

**Step 1 — Recall the formula**

$$ SAS = \frac{M \times R \times P}{C} $$

**Step 2 — Compute SAS for Segment X**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SAS_X &= \frac{60 \times 10 \times 18}{4} \\
&= \frac{10800}{4} \\
&= 2700
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute SAS for Segment Y**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SAS_Y &= \frac{90 \times 6 \times 14}{7} \\
&= \frac{7560}{7} \\
&= 1080
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute SAS for Segment Z**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SAS_Z &= \frac{40 \times 22 \times 25}{3} \\
&= \frac{22000}{3} \\
&= 7333.33
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision Logic**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ranking:} \quad & SAS_Z = 7333.33 \;\; > \;\; SAS_X = 2700 \;\; > \;\; SAS_Y = 1080 \\
\therefore \text{Recommended Segment:} \quad & \textbf{Segment Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Managerial Justification**
- Although Segment Z is the **smallest in absolute market size** (₹40 Cr), it offers the **highest growth rate (22%)**, **highest profit margin (25%)**, and **lowest entry cost (₹3 Cr)**.
- This yields the **highest attractiveness score**, making it the most strategic target.
- The case illustrates a vital KTU lesson: **largest segment ≠ most attractive segment**.

*(7 marks — 1 for formula, 2 for calculations, 1 for ranking, 1 for recommendation, 1 for justification, 1 for managerial insight.)*

---

**Part (b) — Levels of Market Segmentation**

> **1. Mass Marketing**
> The firm offers a **single product and marketing mix** to the entire market.
> *Example:* Early 1900s Ford Model T — "any color as long as it is black."
> *Purpose:* Cost efficiency through uniformity.

> **2. Segment Marketing**
> The firm identifies **distinct segments** and develops a **separate marketing mix for each**.
> *Example:* Lays offering *Classic Salted, Magic Masala, Cream & Onion, Gourmet* — one for each taste segment.

> **3. Niche Marketing**
> The firm targets a **small, well-defined, high-profit group** with a specialized offering.
> *Example:* Rolls-Royce targeting ultra-high-net-worth individuals; **Tanishq** premium bridal jewelry.

> **4. Micro-Marketing**
> Marketing programs are tailored to **individuals or very small groups** (one-to-one marketing).
> *Example:* Amazon's personalized product recommendations; custom tailoring brands.

> **5. Local Marketing**
> Marketing programs are tailored to the **needs and wants of local customer groups** — neighborhoods, cities, or stores.
> *Example:* **Kerala's "Neyyar" mineral water** marketing specifically to Thiruvananthapuram households vs. **Bislery** national marketing.

> *Concluding line:* As the firm moves from **Mass → Local marketing**, **customer relevance increases** but **costs and operational complexity also increase**. The optimal level depends on the firm's resources, product, and competitive landscape.

*(7 marks — 1 mark each for the 5 levels + 1 for examples + 1 for conclusion.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Students Lose Marks in Segmentation Answers:**
>
> 1. **Vague definitions** — Do NOT write "segmentation is dividing the market" without stating *basis, purpose, and outcome*.
> 2. **Forgetting Indian examples** — KTU examiners **prefer Indian brand examples** over generic Western ones.
> 3. **No managerial interpretation** in numerical — A 14-mark answer without interpretation loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up bases** — Behavioral is *what they do*, not *what they are*. Keep Demographic, Psychographic, and Behavioral clearly distinct.
> 5. **Skipping MADSA-S** — This is the **single most repeated framework** in KTU 2024 Module 1. Always include all six criteria with one-line importance.
> 6. **No conclusion line** — A formal concluding sentence summarizing STP linkage earns a **bonus half-mark**.
> 7. **Forgetting units in numerical** — Always write ₹ Cr, %, etc. in tabular columns.
> 8. **Wrong SAS formula** — KTU expects $SAS = \dfrac{M \times R \times P}{C}$, not just $M \times R$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 2-minute exam revision — read this box before the paper.**

- **Definition:** Market segmentation = dividing a market into **homogeneous subgroups** of buyers.
- **Four major bases (D-G-P-B):** Demographic, Geographic, Psychographic, Behavioral.
- **Two B2B bases:** Firmographic, Needs-Based (Jobs-to-be-Done).
- **Six criteria (MADSA-S):** Measurable, Accessible, Differentiable, Substantial, Actionable, Stable.
- **Five levels (M-S-N-M-L):** Mass → Segment → Niche → Micro → Local.
- **SAS Formula:** $SAS = \dfrac{M \times R \times P}{C}$.
- **Best segment** = highest SAS, not highest market size.
- **Behavioral** segmentation is the **most actionable**.
- **Demographic** is the **most used** (easy data).
- **Psychographic** needs primary research (lifestyle, AIOs, VALS).
- **Geographic** is critical for FMCG, climate-based, region-specific products.
- **Segmentation is Step 2 of STP** — *Segmentation → Targeting → Positioning*.
- **Mass marketing** = one offer for all; **Niche marketing** = highly focused.
- **Indian examples to remember:** Maruti (geographic), Lays (behavioral), Royal Enfield (psychographic), Pepsi variants (demographic), Tanishq (niche).
- **Conclude every answer with STP linkage** — segmentation is meaningless without targeting and positioning.
- **Always include a numerical interpretation line** in any computed answer.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Segmented Marketing (also called Mass Marketing or Undifferentiated Marketing)** is a market-coverage strategy in which a firm deliberately chooses to **ignore the structural differences between distinct consumer groups** and instead targets the **entire market with a single, unified marketing mix** (one product, one price, one distribution channel, and one promotional message).

In the language of Philip Kotler's *Marketing Management* framework — which forms the canonical reference for KTU's marketing syllabus — non-segmented marketing is one of the **three alternative market-coverage strategies**, the other two being:

1. **Differentiated (Segmented) Marketing** — multiple mixes for multiple segments.
2. **Concentrated (Niche) Marketing** — one mix for one segment.

When the firm adopts a non-segmented approach, the **STP (Segmentation – Targeting – Positioning)** process collapses the *Targeting* step into a single, market-wide decision.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Vocabulary Box** — Synonyms you may encounter in question papers:
> - *Undifferentiated Marketing*
> - *Mass Marketing*
> - *Aggregate Marketing*
> - *Non-Segmented Approach*
> - *Total Market Approach*
>
> All six are interchangeable in KTU valuation scripts.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **public drinking-water tap installed at a railway station platform**. The municipal authority:

- Does **not** ask the age, income, or religion of each thirsty traveller.
- Does **not** build a separate tap for children, businessmen, or women.
- Offers **one product** (clean water), at **one price** (free / uniform), through **one channel** (the tap), with **one message** ("safe drinking water").

That is *non-segmented marketing* in real life. The marketer says: **"My product has universal appeal, so I will treat the whole market as one big customer."**

A historical textbook example: **Henry Ford's Model T** — *"Any customer can have a car painted any colour that he wants so long as it is black."* Ford deliberately refused to segment; he built one product for the entire motoring public and achieved unprecedented economies of scale.

Modern examples still exist where non-segmented logic works:
- **Table salt** (sodium chloride) — virtually identical across users.
- **Petrol/diesel at a public fuel station** — undifferentiated fuel grade for all vehicles.
- **Coca-Cola's early global "One Brand" campaign** — one red can, one universal message.
- **A basic 2G SIM card** sold as a uniform utility.

---

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics Used in Non-Segmented Analysis

In KTU numerical/analytical questions, the following metrics are typically flagged in **bold** by examiners:

- **TAM (Total Addressable Market)** — total universe of buyers who could conceivably purchase the product.
- **MS (Market Share)** — the firm's sales as a percentage of total category sales.
- **Per Capita Consumption (PCC)** — average consumption per person in the market.
- **Penetration Rate** — percentage of the population that has bought the product at least once.
- **Break-Even Volume (BEV)** — the number of units the company must sell to cover all costs.

These metrics are used *without segment-level disaggregation* in a non-segmented strategy, which is precisely why the approach is administratively simple.

---

### 1.4 Why a Firm Would *Choose* Not to Segment — The Core Rationale

> [!NOTE]
> **The three "U" conditions that justify a non-segmented approach:**
> 1. **U**niform customer needs
> 2. **U**niversal demand pattern
> 3. **U**navailability of meaningful sub-group differences

A firm chooses a non-segmented strategy when it concludes that **the cost and complexity of segmentation is not justified by the incremental revenue** that tailored mixes could generate. This is a classic *cost-benefit* judgement.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How a Non-Segmented Marketing Plan Is Built

A KTU board-style answer for "explain non-segmented marketing" should follow a **four-layer logic chain**:

**Layer 1 — Market Identification**
- The firm defines the *broad product-market* (e.g., "the Indian passenger-car market" rather than "young urban SUV buyers in Tier-1 cities").
- Boundaries are drawn by *product category*, not by *customer type*.

**Layer 2 — STP Collapse**
- The Segmentation step is either skipped or executed at a very broad, single-cluster level.
- The Targeting step selects **the whole market** as the target.
- Positioning is built around **one universal value proposition** (e.g., "affordable mobility for every Indian").

**Layer 3 — Single Marketing Mix (the 4 Ps)**
- **Product**: One standardized offering, minimal variants.
- **Price**: One price point (or very narrow price band).
- **Place**: Mass distribution — wide, intensive, often through every available channel.
- **Promotion**: One mass-media message aimed at the average consumer.

**Layer 4 — Volume-Based Competitive Advantage**
- The firm bets on **economies of scale**, **low per-unit cost**, and **broad reach** to win.

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Strategic Logic

- **Step A:** Identify if the product has a *homogeneous* demand function across all demographic, geographic, psychographic, and behavioural splits.
- **Step B:** Verify that competitors are *not* successfully differentiating.
- **Step C:** Confirm that **scale economics dominate over personalisation economics** in this category.
- **Step D:** Design a *single* positioning statement using the most generic, broadly-appealing benefit.
- **Step E:** Roll out one *standardized* marketing mix.
- **Step F:** Measure success using *aggregate* (non-segmented) KPIs such as **Total Volume Sold**, **Total Revenue**, **Overall Market Share**, and **Penetration Rate**.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and aligned with the quantitative sections of KTU's Marketing Management paper.

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | When to Use in KTU Answers |
|---|---------|--------------------|-------------------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | **Total Addressable Market (TAM)** | $\text{TAM} = N \times \bar{q} \times P$ | $N$ = population of buyers (persons), $\bar{q}$ = average annual quantity bought per person (units), $P$ = unit price (₹) | Estimating the size of the *whole* market the firm will serve under a non-segmented plan |
| 2 | **Serviceable Available Market (SAM)** | $\text{SAM} = \text{TAM} \times \rho$ | $\rho$ = proportion reachable by firm's channels (fraction, $0 \le \rho \le 1$) | Narrowing TAM to what the firm's distribution can physically reach |
| 3 | **Serviceable Obtainable Market (SOM)** | $\text{SOM} = \text{SAM} \times s$ | $s$ = realistic market share target (fraction) | Realistic revenue forecasting in a non-segmented plan |
| 4 | **Market Share (MS)** | $\text{MS} = \dfrac{Q_f}{Q_t} \times 100$ | $Q_f$ = firm sales (units), $Q_t$ = total category sales (units) | Evaluating competitive position in aggregate |
| 5 | **Penetration Rate** | $\text{PR} = \dfrac{C_b}{N} \times 100$ | $C_b$ = distinct buyers who purchased at least once (persons), $N$ = total population (persons) | Measuring how broadly the mass-marketed product has spread |
| 6 | **Break-Even Volume (BEV)** | $\text{BEV} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$ | $F$ = fixed cost (₹), $P$ = unit price (₹), $V$ = variable cost per unit (₹) | Finding minimum volume to avoid loss; critical in mass-market cost structures |
| 7 | **Cost per Thousand Impressions (CPM)** | $\text{CPM} = \dfrac{C_a \times 1000}{A}$ | $C_a$ = cost of advertisement (₹), $A$ = audience size (persons) | Comparing mass-media efficiency for a single-message campaign |
| 8 | **Per Capita Consumption (PCC)** | $\text{PCC} = \dfrac{Q_t}{N}$ | $Q_t$ = total category volume, $N$ = population | Measuring the *uniformity* of consumption (low variance favours non-segmented) |
| 9 | **Economies of Scale Coefficient** | $\text{AC} = \dfrac{F}{Q} + v$ | $\text{AC}$ = average cost (₹/unit), $F$ = fixed cost, $Q$ = volume, $v$ = variable cost per unit | Justifying non-segmented strategy via falling AC as $Q$ grows |
| 10 | **Standard Deviation of Segment Needs (Segmentation Justification Index)** | $\sigma_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (n_i - \bar{n})^2}{k}}$ | $n_i$ = need-score of segment $i$, $\bar{n}$ = mean, $k$ = number of segments | If $\sigma_n$ is small, needs are homogeneous $\Rightarrow$ non-segmented justified |

> **NOTE TO STUDENTS (KTU Valuation Tip):** Whenever you write any of the above formulas in an answer, **always define the variables** in the same line. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for "unexplained symbols."

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility Across Industries

- **FMCG** (Fast-Moving Consumer Goods) — the textbook home of mass marketing: soaps, detergents, salt, biscuits, packaged water.
- **Telecom** — pre-paid recharge denominations sold uniformly across all customer types.
- **Public Utilities** — electricity, water, municipal transport.
- **Pharmaceuticals (OTC)** — paracetamol tablets, antacids sold as a single SKU.
- **Energy & Fuel** — petrol, diesel, LPG cylinders.
- **Banking (Tier-1 Products)** — basic savings accounts, UPI, NEFT/RTGS services.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering-Marketing Crossover Insight (PEMET638 context):** PEMET638 is an elective taken by engineering students. In *engineering product companies* (e.g., industrial fasteners, commodity-grade steel, standardised electric motors), the non-segmented approach is common because B2B buyers often treat such SKUs as fungible commodities. Always mention this in KTU answers — examiners reward *cross-domain application*.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — When Does Non-Segmented Strategy Maximise Profit?

A firm chooses between a **non-segmented strategy** (one mix for the whole market) and a **segmented strategy** (multiple mixes for multiple segments). The economically correct choice depends on comparing the **incremental profit** of segmentation against its **incremental cost**.

Let:

- $R_{NS}$ = Revenue from Non-Segmented strategy.
- $R_S$ = Revenue from Segmented strategy.
- $C_{NS}$ = Cost of Non-Segmented strategy.
- $C_S$ = Cost of Segmented strategy.
- $K$ = Additional fixed cost of running a segmented approach (extra R&D, branding, distribution, promotion).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_{NS} &= R_{NS} - C_{NS} \\
\pi_{S} &= R_{S} - C_{S} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The decision rule is:

$$
\pi_{S} - \pi_{NS} \; ? \; 0
$$

Substitute:

$$
(R_{S} - C_{S}) - (R_{NS} - C_{NS}) \; ? \; 0
$$

Rearrange:

$$
(R_{S} - R_{NS}) - (C_{S} - C_{NS}) \; ? \; 0
$$

Define the *incremental revenue* and *incremental cost*:

$$
\Delta R - \Delta C \; ? \; 0
$$

Including the segmentation overhead $K$:

$$
\Delta R \; \gtrless \; \Delta C + K
$$

> **Economic Interpretation:** A non-segmented strategy is **profitable** when the *extra revenue* obtained from tailoring mixes to segments is *less than* the *extra cost* of doing so, including the fixed overhead $K$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Break-Even Volume Under Mass Marketing

For a mass-marketed product, the firm bets on high volume and low per-unit cost. The break-even condition is:

$$
\text{Total Revenue} = \text{Total Cost}
$$

Let $P$ = unit price, $Q$ = volume sold, $F$ = total fixed cost, $v$ = variable cost per unit.

$$
P \cdot Q = F + v \cdot Q
$$

Collect $Q$ terms:

$$
P \cdot Q - v \cdot Q = F
$$

Factor:

$$
Q \cdot (P - v) = F
$$

Solve for $Q$ (the break-even volume $\text{BEV}$):

$$
\text{BEV} = \dfrac{F}{P - v}
$$

> **Insight:** In a non-segmented strategy, $F$ tends to be very high (mass-media advertising, large factories, wide distribution) and $(P - v)$ tends to be very low (thin margins). Hence $\text{BEV}$ is large. The strategy *only* works if the firm can hit that high volume.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Uniformity Test (Should You Even Consider Segmenting?)

Suppose you surveyed $k$ consumer groups and measured their *need intensity* on a scale of $0$ to $10$. Let $n_i$ be the need score of group $i$, and $\bar{n}$ the mean.

$$
\bar{n} = \dfrac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i
$$

The standard deviation across groups:

$$
\sigma_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} (n_i - \bar{n})^2}
$$

**Decision rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } \sigma_n < \theta \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{Needs are homogeneous} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Non-Segmented preferred} \\
\text{If } \sigma_n \ge \theta \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{Needs are heterogeneous} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Differentiated preferred} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\theta$ is a *managerially chosen* threshold (commonly $\theta = 1.5$ on a $0$–$10$ scale in textbook examples).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Non-Segmented vs. Differentiated vs. Concentrated

| Evaluation Dimension | **Non-Segmented** (Mass) | **Differentiated** (Segmented) | **Concentrated** (Niche) |
|----------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **# of Marketing Mixes** | One (1) | Many (3–5 typically) | One (1) |
| **# of Target Segments** | Whole market | Multiple selected segments | One single segment |
| **Production Logic** | Standardised, long runs | Multiple variants / SKUs | Specialised, smaller runs |
| **Cost Structure** | High fixed, low variable | High fixed + high variable | Low fixed, focused variable |
| **Risk Profile** | Low (spread) | Medium | High (concentration) |
| **Revenue Potential** | Limited by uniform appeal | High (multiple revenue streams) | Limited to niche size |
| **Best-Suited Condition** | Homogeneous demand | Heterogeneous, large market | Heterogeneous, small specialist segment |
| **Brand Image** | Often a *commodity* image | *Multi-personality* brand | *Specialist/expert* image |
| **Examples** | Salt, basic banking, petrol | Maruti Suzuki (multiple models) | Rolls-Royce (ultra-luxury only) |
| **KTU 2024 Scheme CO Mapping** | **CO1 – K3 (Apply)** | **CO1 – K3 (Apply)** | **CO1 – K3 (Apply)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bloom's Taxonomy Mapping for This Topic (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **CO1 / K2 – Understand:** Define mass marketing, list its features.
> - **CO1 / K3 – Apply:** Compare the three coverage strategies in a case study.
> - **CO1 / K4 – Analyse:** Evaluate *when* a non-segmented approach is justified.
> - **CO1 / K5 – Evaluate:** Critique a real company's choice of mass marketing strategy.
