- [x] It is a type of **conclusive research**, positioned between exploratory and causal research in the marketing research hierarchy.
- [x] The **six-step process** is essential: Define problem → Specify info → Choose design → Plan sampling → Collect data → Analyze & report.
- [x] Two main **types**: **Cross-sectional** (snapshot) and **Longitudinal** (over time).
- [x] Longitudinal further splits into **Panel Study** (same respondents) and **Trend Study** (same population, different respondents).
- [x] Four key **methods**: **Surveys**, **Observation**, **Secondary Data**, **Case Studies**.
- [x] The most common method is the **Survey Method** — pre-planned, structured, quantitative.
- [x] **Cochran's Formula** $n_0 = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}$ is the foundation for sample size determination.
- [x] When $p$ is unknown, use **$p = 0.5$** to maximize sample size conservatively.
- [x] The **Finite Population Correction** is applied when $n_0 > 5\%$ of $N$.
- [x] The **Response Rate** formula $RR = \frac{\text{Completed}}{\text{Total Distributed}} \times 100\%$ is a critical quality metric.
- [x] Descriptive research **cannot establish causality** — only associations and descriptions.
- [x] For KTU exams, always **combine definition + process + types + methods + Indian example** for full marks.
- [x] **Diagrams fetch 1–2 marks** — always draw at least one flowchart or comparison chart.
- [x] For numericals, **show all substitution steps** — partial credit is awarded for working.

> [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. Writing only the definition without the **process or types** → loses 5+ marks.
> 2. Confusing **panel study** (same respondents) with **trend study** (same population).
> 3. Using $p = 0.5$ without explaining why (maximum variance assumption).
> 4. Forgetting the **finite population correction** when the population is small.
> 5. Claiming descriptive research establishes causality — it does **NOT**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experimental
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET638 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Experimental

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Marketing Management** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, the term **"Experimental"** refers to the family of **Experimental Research Designs** used in marketing research, broadly classified under the **Conclusive Research** methodology. Experimental research is the most scientifically rigorous form of causal research where the investigator manipulates one or more **Independent Variables (IVs)** and measures the effect on one or more **Dependent Variables (DVs)**, while **controlling for extraneous variables** to isolate cause-and-effect relationships.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved Wording):**
> "Experimental research is a type of causal research in which the researcher deliberately manipulates one or more independent variables, observes and measures the effect on the dependent variable(s), and controls for all other influencing factors to establish a true cause-and-effect relationship."

The mathematical representation of an experimental effect is:

$$E = M_{\text{treatment}} - M_{\text{control}}$$

Where:
- $E$ = Experimental Effect (the causal impact)
- $M_{\text{treatment}}$ = Mean of the Dependent Variable in the experimental/treatment group
- $M_{\text{control}}$ = Mean of the Dependent Variable in the control group

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like a Medical Drug Trial...**
> Imagine a pharmaceutical company testing a new headache pill. They give the **real pill** to one group of patients (Treatment Group) and a **sugar pill (placebo)** to another identical group (Control Group). If the Treatment Group shows significantly more relief than the Control Group, we can confidently say the **pill caused** the relief — not placebo, not coincidence, not random variation.
> 
> **Marketing Parallel:** Replace "pill" with "new packaging" or "₹50 discount coupon," and "patients" with "consumers." That is exactly what experimental marketing research does.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Mandate (KTU Board Vocabulary)

The following **high-frequency terminological set** is mandatory for any KTU answer:

| Term | Board-Examiner Definition |
|---|---|
| **Independent Variable** | The variable manipulated by the researcher (e.g., price, ad copy, packaging). |
| **Dependent Variable** | The outcome measured (e.g., sales volume, brand recall, purchase intent). |
| **Extraneous Variables** | Unwanted variables that may influence the dependent variable. |
| **Control Variable** | A variable deliberately held constant to neutralize its effect. |
| **Treatment** | The specific experimental condition applied (e.g., discount level A, discount level B). |
| **Control Group** | The group NOT exposed to the treatment (baseline). |
| **Test Group** | The group EXPOSED to the treatment. |
| **Effect** | The measurable difference between treatment and control outcomes. |
| **Internal Validity** | The degree to which the observed effect is truly caused by the IV. |
| **External Validity** | The degree to which results can be generalized to the real market. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:**
> The syllabus explicitly requires students to differentiate between **Experimental, Descriptive, and Exploratory** research designs, and to identify the appropriate experimental design for a given marketing scenario (e.g., pre-test/post-test, after-only with control group, factorial design).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Framework

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to explain the **operational mechanics** of experimental research using the following six-stage logical chain:

### Step 1 — Identify the Research Problem & Hypothesis
The researcher starts with a managerial problem (e.g., "Will a 20% discount increase sales volume?"). This is converted into a testable **Hypothesis**:
- **$H_0$ (Null Hypothesis):** The discount has no effect on sales volume.
- **$H_1$ (Alternative Hypothesis):** The discount significantly increases sales volume.

### Step 2 — Identify the Variables
- **Independent Variable (IV):** Discount level (manipulated).
- **Dependent Variable (DV):** Sales volume (measured).
- **Extraneous Variables:** Seasonality, competitor pricing, store location, brand loyalty — these must be **controlled**.

### Step 3 — Select the Experimental Design
KTU syllabus mandates familiarity with three core designs:
1. **Pre-Test / Post-Test Control Group Design** (classic randomized design).
2. **After-Only Control Group Design** (no pre-test).
3. **Factorial Design (2×2, 3×3)** (multiple IVs).

### Step 4 — Randomize & Assign Units
**Random Assignment** to control vs. treatment groups ensures that pre-existing differences among subjects are evenly distributed. This is the **single most important validity safeguard**.

### Step 5 — Apply Treatment & Measure
Administer the experimental stimulus (e.g., the new ad) and measure the DV (e.g., purchase intent on a 1–7 scale).

### Step 6 — Statistical Analysis & Inference
Compute the experimental effect, then use a **t-test** or **ANOVA** to confirm that the observed difference is statistically significant.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exact set of equations** a student must memorize for any 14-mark derivation question on experimental research in KTU ESE.

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Purpose / Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Experimental Effect (E)** | $E = \bar{X}_{\text{test}} - \bar{X}_{\text{control}}$ | Quantifies the causal impact of the treatment on the DV. |
| **Relative Effect** | $E_{\text{rel}} = \dfrac{\bar{X}_{\text{test}} - \bar{X}_{\text{control}}}{\bar{X}_{\text{control}}} \times 100$ | Expresses effect as a percentage — common in marketing ROI analysis. |
| **Sample Size (t-test)** | $n = \dfrac{2 \sigma^2 (Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^2}{E^2}$ | Determines minimum sample size for a valid test (where $\sigma$ = population std. dev., $Z_{\alpha/2}$ = confidence level critical value, $Z_{\beta}$ = power critical value). |
| **ANOVA F-Ratio** | $F = \dfrac{\text{Between-Group Mean Square (MS}_{\text{between}}\text{)}}{\text{Within-Group Mean Square (MS}_{\text{within}}\text{)}}$ | Used in factorial designs with 3+ treatment groups. |
| **Statistical Power (1 − β)** | $\beta$ = Probability of Type II Error | Target power in marketing research is typically **0.80** (i.e., $\beta = 0.20$). |
| **Confidence Level** | $1 - \alpha$ | KTU board convention: use **95% confidence** ($\alpha = 0.05$). |
| **Effect Size (Cohen's d)** | $d = \dfrac{\bar{X}_{\text{test}} - \bar{X}_{\text{control}}}{\sigma_{\text{pooled}}}$ | Standardizes the experimental effect for cross-study comparison. |
| **Threat to Internal Validity** | History, Maturation, Testing, Instrumentation, Regression, Mortality, Selection Bias | The classic Campbell & Stanley 7-threat framework — **KTU-favorite 14-mark question**. |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Marketing Engineering

Experimental designs are the **gold standard** for marketing decisions where millions of rupees are at stake:
- **A/B Testing in Digital Marketing:** Google, Amazon, and Flipkart run millions of randomized experiments daily on button color, pricing, and recommendation algorithms.
- **Test Markets:** Companies like Hindustan Unilever (HUL) and Nestlé launch new products in geographically isolated test markets (e.g., one city as test, an adjacent city as control).
- **Price Elasticity Measurement:** Airlines and e-commerce platforms use field experiments to model price-demand curves.
- **Advertising Copy Testing:** TV commercials are tested in matched-market designs before national rollout.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Experimental Effect Size (Cohen's d)

This is the **most frequently asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU ESE for the experimental research unit.

**Given:**
- Test Group mean: $\bar{X}_{\text{test}}$
- Control Group mean: $\bar{X}_{\text{control}}$
- Pooled standard deviation: $\sigma_{\text{pooled}}$

**Step 1:** Start with the raw experimental effect.

$$\Delta \mu = \bar{X}_{\text{test}} - \bar{X}_{\text{control}}$$

*Explanation:* This raw difference represents the absolute change caused by the treatment.

**Step 2:** Compute the pooled standard deviation.

$$\sigma_{\text{pooled}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(n_{\text{test}} - 1) s_{\text{test}}^{2} + (n_{\text{control}} - 1) s_{\text{control}}^{2}}{n_{\text{test}} + n_{\text{control}} - 2}}$$

*Explanation:* The pooled SD combines within-group variability from both samples. The denominator uses Bessel's correction ($n-1$) per group.

**Step 3:** Standardize the effect.

$$d = \dfrac{\Delta \mu}{\sigma_{\text{pooled}}} = \dfrac{\bar{X}_{\text{test}} - \bar{X}_{\text{control}}}{\sigma_{\text{pooled}}}$$

*Explanation:* Dividing by the pooled SD converts the raw effect into a unit-free standardized score (Cohen's d).

**Step 4:** Interpret the magnitude using Cohen's benchmarks.

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= 0.20 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Small Effect} \\
d &= 0.50 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Medium Effect} \\
d &= 0.80 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Large Effect}
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* These conventions apply to marketing research as well — a $d \ge 0.50$ is generally considered managerially meaningful.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Minimum Sample Size for a Two-Group Experiment

**Given:** We want to detect an effect of size $E$ with confidence level $1 - \alpha$ and power $1 - \beta$.

**Step 1:** Start with the Z-statistic for the difference between two means.

$$Z = \dfrac{\bar{X}_{\text{test}} - \bar{X}_{\text{control}}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma^{2}}{n} + \dfrac{\sigma^{2}}{n}}} = \dfrac{E}{\sigma \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{n}}}$$

*Explanation:* We set the difference equal to the desired effect $E$ and solve for the sample size $n$ per group, assuming equal variance $\sigma^2$ and equal group sizes.

**Step 2:** Set $|Z| = Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta}$ to account for both Type I and Type II error simultaneously.

$$\dfrac{E}{\sigma \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{n}}} = Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta}$$

*Explanation:* This is the "two-tailed" criterion — we must budget for both false-positive ($Z_{\alpha/2}$) and false-negative ($Z_{\beta}$) error.

**Step 3:** Square both sides.

$$\dfrac{E^{2}}{\sigma^{2} \cdot \dfrac{2}{n}} = (Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2}$$

*Explanation:* Squaring eliminates the square root and prepares the equation for algebraic isolation of $n$.

**Step 4:** Cross-multiply to isolate $n$.

$$\dfrac{n \cdot E^{2}}{2 \sigma^{2}} = (Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2}$$

*Explanation:* Move $\sigma^2 \cdot 2/n$ to the denominator's other side.

**Step 5:** Solve the final sample size equation.

$$n = \dfrac{2 \sigma^{2} (Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2}}{E^{2}}$$

*Explanation:* This is the **final, board-ready formula** for minimum sample size per group. KTU expects this exact form.

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):**
A retailer expects that a new in-store display will increase average daily sales by ₹500. Past data shows $\sigma = 1000$. With 95% confidence ($Z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96$) and 80% power ($Z_{\beta} = 0.84$):

$$n = \dfrac{2 \times (1000)^{2} \times (1.96 + 0.84)^{2}}{(500)^{2}} = \dfrac{2 \times 1{,}000{,}000 \times 7.84}{250{,}000} = \dfrac{15{,}680{,}000}{250{,}000} \approx 62.72$$

Therefore, the retailer needs **63 stores per group** (test and control).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Three Core Experimental Designs

| Design Feature | Pre-Test / Post-Test Control Group | After-Only Control Group | 2×2 Factorial Design |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-Test Required?** | Yes | No | Yes (recommended) |
| **Control Group Used?** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Number of Independent Variables** | 1 | 1 | 2 (or more) |
| **Tests Interaction Effects?** | No | No | **Yes** (main strength) |
| **Susceptible to History Threat?** | Yes (pre-test to post-test interval) | Lower | Yes |
| **Susceptible to Maturation?** | Yes | Lower | Yes |
| **Statistical Test Used** | ANCOVA / Difference-in-Differences | Independent t-test | Two-Way ANOVA |
| **Typical Marketing Application** | Brand pre-launch tracking | Email subject-line A/B test | Price × Promotion conjoint study |
| **KTU Examiner Preference** | Most frequently tested design | Common in digital marketing questions | Asked when "interaction" is in the question |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Experimental Research Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Identify Managerial Problem"] --> B["Formulate Hypothesis H0 and H1"]
    B --> C["Identify IV, DV and Extraneous Variables"]
    C --> D["Choose Experimental Design Type"]
    D --> E["Random Assignment to Test and Control Groups"]
    E --> F["Apply Treatment Stimulus"]
    F --> G["Measure Dependent Variable"]
    G --> H["Compute Experimental Effect E"]
    H --> I{"Statistically Significant?"}
    I -- Yes --> J["Reject H0 Conclude Causal Effect"]
    I -- No --> K["Fail to Reject H0 No Proven Effect"]
    J --> L["Managerial Decision Implementation"]
    K --> L
    L --> M["Post Experiment Audit"]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Pre-Test / Post-Test Control Group Design (X Notation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GroupA["Random Group 1: Test Group"]
        A1["Pre-Test Measurement R1"] --> A2["Apply Treatment X"] --> A3["Post-Test Measurement R2"]
    end
    subgraph GroupB["Random Group 2: Control Group"]
        B1["Pre-Test Measurement R3"] --> B2["No Treatment"] --> B3["Post-Test Measurement R4"]
    end
    A3 --> C["Compute Effect E = R2 minus R1 minus R4 minus R3"]
    B3 --> C
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — 2×2 Factorial Design Cell Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PriceHigh["Price: HIGH"]
        PH1["Ad LOW Sales A"]
        PH2["Ad HIGH Sales B"]
    end
    subgraph PriceLow["Price: LOW"]
        PL1["Ad LOW Sales C"]
        PL2["Ad HIGH Sales D"]
    end
    PriceHigh --> Result["Compute Main Effects and Interaction Effect I = A plus D minus B minus C divided by 2"]
    PriceLow --> Result
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Campbell & Stanley Threats to Internal Validity

```mermaid
mindmap
    root((Threats to Internal Validity))
        History
            External event occurs between pre-test and post-test
        Maturation
            Subjects naturally change over time
        Testing
            Pre-test sensitizes subjects to post-test
        Instrumentation
            Change in measurement instrument or observer
        Statistical Regression
            Extreme scores naturally move toward mean
        Selection Bias
            Non-random assignment to groups
        Mortality
            Differential dropout between groups
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define experimental research. List any four threats to internal validity.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Experimental research is a type of causal research design in which the researcher deliberately manipulates one or more independent variables and observes the effect on the dependent variable while controlling for extraneous factors, to establish a true cause-and-effect relationship. (2 marks)

Four threats to internal validity (any four, ¼ mark each):
1. **History** – external events during the experiment.
2. **Maturation** – natural changes in subjects over time.
3. **Testing** – effect of pre-test on post-test performance.
4. **Instrumentation** – changes in the measurement instrument.
5. **Statistical Regression** – extreme scores reverting to the mean.
6. **Selection Bias** – non-random group assignment.
7. **Mortality** – differential dropout of subjects.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Distinguish between internal validity and external validity in experimental research.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Dimension | Internal Validity | External Validity |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The extent to which the experiment proves that the IV actually caused the change in the DV. | The extent to which experimental results can be generalized to the real market. |
| **Focus** | Accuracy of causal inference within the study. | Applicability of results to other settings, populations, and times. |
| **Threats** | History, Maturation, Testing, etc. | Interaction of selection and treatment, reactive effects. |
| **Relationship** | Necessary precondition for external validity. | Cannot be achieved without internal validity. |

(2 marks for definitions, 1 mark for the relationship statement.)

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **DO NOT confuse "Experimental Design" with "Research Design in general."** The examiner deducts up to 2 marks if the answer drifts into descriptive or exploratory research.
> 2. **Always label variables explicitly as IV and DV** in any worked example. Failing to do so costs 1 mark.
> 3. **In derivation questions, every algebraic step carries a mark.** Skipping steps from the start to the final equation leads to a maximum of only 4 out of 7 marks on the derivation sub-part.
> 4. **Mention "Random Assignment" explicitly** whenever discussing how groups are formed — this single phrase can fetch 1 mark that students routinely miss.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**(a)** Explain the **Pre-Test / Post-Test Control Group Design** with the help of a suitable marketing example. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A FMCG company wants to test the effect of a new shelf display on average daily sales. Past data shows the population standard deviation is **σ = ₹800**. The company expects a minimum detectable effect of **E = ₹400**. Using a 95% confidence level (**Z = 1.96**) and 80% power (**Z = 0.84**), calculate the minimum sample size per group using the standard formula. Interpret the result. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
The Pre-Test / Post-Test Control Group Design is a classic randomized experimental design where both the test group and the control group are measured before (pre-test) and after (post-test) the treatment is applied to the test group only. The treatment effect is isolated by comparing the change in the test group to the change in the control group.

**Notation (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
R \quad & \rightarrow \quad \text{Random Assignment} \\
O_{1}, O_{3} \quad & \rightarrow \quad \text{Pre-Test Observations} \\
X \quad & \rightarrow \quad \text{Treatment (Stimulus)} \\
O_{2}, O_{4} \quad & \rightarrow \quad \text{Post-Test Observations}
\end{aligned}$$

**Test Group:** $R \rightarrow O_{1} \rightarrow X \rightarrow O_{2}$
**Control Group:** $R \rightarrow O_{3} \rightarrow \rightarrow O_{4}$

**Effect Formula (1 mark):**
$$E = (O_{2} - O_{1}) - (O_{4} - O_{3})$$

**Marketing Example (1 mark):**
A detergent brand wants to test a new TV commercial. They select 20 matched retail stores, randomly assign 10 as test stores (pre-test sales, show ad, post-test sales) and 10 as control stores (pre-test sales, no ad, post-test sales).

**Advantages (1 mark):** Controls for history and maturation because both groups experience the same external events; allows direct measurement of change.
**Limitations (1 mark):** Pre-testing may sensitize respondents (testing effect); requires a true control group, which may not be feasible in all field situations.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Identify given values (1 mark).**
- $\sigma = 800$
- $E = 400$
- $Z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96$
- $Z_{\beta} = 0.84$

**Step 2: State the formula (2 marks).**

$$n = \dfrac{2 \sigma^{2} (Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2}}{E^{2}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute and compute (3 marks).**

$$n = \dfrac{2 \times (800)^{2} \times (1.96 + 0.84)^{2}}{(400)^{2}}$$

$$n = \dfrac{2 \times 640{,}000 \times (2.80)^{2}}{160{,}000}$$

$$n = \dfrac{1{,}280{,}000 \times 7.84}{160{,}000}$$

$$n = \dfrac{10{,}035{,}200}{160{,}000}$$

$$n = 62.72$$

**Step 4: Round up and interpret (1 mark).**
Since $n$ must be a whole number, we round up to **$n = 63$ stores per group** (test and control). The company needs **126 stores in total** to detect the ₹400 effect with 95% confidence and 80% statistical power.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative Module Choice]
**(a)** What is a **2×2 Factorial Design**? Explain with a marketing example how it is used to study the **main effects and interaction effect** of two independent variables. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute and interpret **Cohen's d** for the following marketing experiment: a brand tested two versions of a digital ad. The **Test Group** ($n_1 = 50$) had a mean purchase-intent score of $\bar{X}_{\text{test}} = 6.2$ with $s_1 = 1.4$, while the **Control Group** ($n_2 = 50$) had $\bar{X}_{\text{control}} = 5.0$ with $s_2 = 1.2$. Comment on the practical significance for management. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
A 2×2 Factorial Design is an experimental design that simultaneously manipulates **two independent variables**, each at two levels, resulting in four experimental cells. It allows the researcher to study not only the **main effect** of each variable but also the **interaction effect** between them — which is impossible in single-factor designs.

**Structure (2 marks):**

|  | **Level 1 of IV-B** | **Level 2 of IV-B** |
|---|---|---|
| **Level 1 of IV-A** | Cell 1 | Cell 2 |
| **Level 2 of IV-A** | Cell 3 | Cell 4 |

**Marketing Example (2 marks):**
A beverage company wants to study the effect of **Price (High/Low)** and **Promotion (Coupon / No Coupon)** on sales. They run the experiment in four matched store groups:
- Cell 1: High Price + No Coupon
- Cell 2: High Price + Coupon
- Cell 3: Low Price + No Coupon
- Cell 4: Low Price + Coupon

The **interaction effect** tests whether the impact of coupons on sales differs between high-price and low-price stores — a critical insight for promotional budgeting.

**Statistical Test (1 mark):** Two-Way ANOVA is used to test significance of both main effects and the interaction.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1: State the formula (2 marks).**

$$d = \dfrac{\bar{X}_{\text{test}} - \bar{X}_{\text{control}}}{\sigma_{\text{pooled}}}$$

**Step 2: Compute the pooled standard deviation (3 marks).**

$$\sigma_{\text{pooled}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(50-1)(1.4)^{2} + (50-1)(1.2)^{2}}{50 + 50 - 2}}$$

$$\sigma_{\text{pooled}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{49 \times 1.96 + 49 \times 1.44}{98}}$$

$$\sigma_{\text{pooled}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{96.04 + 70.56}{98}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{166.60}{98}}$$

$$\sigma_{\text{pooled}} = \sqrt{1.70} \approx 1.304$$

**Step 3: Compute Cohen's d (1 mark).**

$$d = \dfrac{6.2 - 5.0}{1.304} = \dfrac{1.2}{1.304} \approx 0.92$$

**Step 4: Interpret (1 mark).**
Since $d \approx 0.92 > 0.80$, this represents a **large effect**. The new ad has a substantial managerially meaningful impact on purchase intent. The brand should consider rolling out the new ad across all digital channels.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 1 (Experimental Research):**

- **Definition:** Experimental research is the most rigorous form of causal research, manipulating IVs to measure effects on DVs while controlling extraneous variables.
- **Core Equation:** $E = \bar{X}_{\text{test}} - \bar{X}_{\text{control}}$ (raw effect) and $d = \dfrac{E}{\sigma_{\text{pooled}}}$ (standardized Cohen's d).
- **Sample Size Formula:** $n = \dfrac{2\sigma^{2}(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2}}{E^{2}}$ — memorize this verbatim.
- **Three Designs:** Pre-Test/Post-Test Control Group, After-Only Control Group, and 2×2 Factorial Design.
- **Seven Threats to Internal Validity:** History, Maturation, Testing, Instrumentation, Regression, Selection Bias, Mortality — write all seven in any 7-mark question.
- **Validity Pair:** Internal Validity = causal accuracy; External Validity = generalizability.
- **Statistical Tests:** t-test (2 groups), ANOVA (3+ groups), Two-Way ANOVA (factorial designs).
- **Standard Conventions:** 95% confidence level, 80% statistical power, $Z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96$, $Z_{\beta} = 0.84$.
- **Always Randomize:** Mention "random assignment" in every experimental design answer — KTU awards marks for this term.
- **Cohen's d Benchmarks:** 0.20 (small), 0.50 (medium), 0.80 (large) — used for interpreting practical significance.
- **Marketing Applications:** A/B testing, test markets, price elasticity studies, advertising copy testing, conjoint analysis.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Survey Methods: Marketing Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET638 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Survey Methods: Marketing Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Survey Method (Marketing Research Definition)**
> A *survey method* is a **systematic, structured, and standardized technique of gathering primary data** from a defined sample of respondents (customers, dealers, suppliers, competitors, or any stakeholder) by asking them a predetermined set of questions — directly, by telephone, by mail, or online — in order to understand, monitor, and respond to the **Marketing Environment** of an organization.

The **Marketing Environment** is the sum total of all **internal and external forces** that influence the organization's ability to build and maintain successful relationships with its target customers. It is conventionally divided into two concentric layers:

- **Micro (Internal/Task) Environment** – forces close to the company that directly affect its ability to serve its customers.
- **Macro (External/Broad) Environment** – larger societal forces that shape opportunities and pose threats.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Doctor's Stethoscope" Analogy**
> Think of a company as a **patient** and the marketing environment as the **patient's body**. A doctor does not guess what is wrong — she uses instruments (a stethoscope, blood test, MRI) to *measure* vital signs. **Survey methods are the marketing stethoscope** of a company. They measure the *pulse of customers*, the *blood pressure of competitors*, the *temperature of regulations*, and the *oxygen level of new technology*. Without these structured measurements, a company is "marketing blind" — it operates on hunches, not evidence.

### 1.3 Why Surveys are the Dominant Method for Studying the Marketing Environment

| Feature | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Structured** | Each respondent answers the *same* questions, enabling comparison. |
| **Scalable** | Can reach **hundreds to thousands** of respondents in a single wave. |
| **Quantifiable** | Data can be statistically analysed (percentages, means, regression). |
| **Direct** | Captures **first-hand** opinions, attitudes, and intentions of stakeholders. |
| **Verifiable** | Auditable methodology → high credibility in strategic decision-making. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly links **Survey Methods → Marketing Environment**, meaning the question is *not* "what is a survey?" but **"how do we deploy surveys to study the internal and external marketing environment?"** — keep this link alive in every answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Layers of the Marketing Environment — A Structured Breakdown

**A. The Micro (Internal / Task) Environment**

These are forces *inside* the company's immediate sphere of operation:

1. **The Company itself** – internal departments (Finance, R&D, HR, Operations). Surveyed through *internal employee satisfaction surveys* and *internal capability audits*.
2. **Suppliers** – those who provide raw materials, components, services. *Supplier evaluation surveys* track reliability, quality, on-time delivery.
3. **Marketing Intermediaries** – resellers, distributors, logistics firms, agencies. *Trade satisfaction surveys* measure channel partner morale.
4. **Customers** – the *most-surveyed* group (B2B and B2C). *Customer satisfaction (CSAT)*, *Net Promoter Score (NPS)*, and *Voice of Customer (VoC)* surveys.
5. **Competitors** – tracked through *competitor benchmarking surveys* and mystery shopping.
6. **Publics** – media, government, local community, financial groups. *Public opinion surveys* gauge brand image.

**B. The Macro (External / Broad) Environment** — The classic **PESTEL / PESTLE** framework

| Force | What a Survey Measures | Example Survey Instrument |
|---|---|---|
| **P**olitical | Government stability, policy support, FDI climate | Industry-expert Delphi survey |
| **E**conomic | Consumer confidence, purchasing power, inflation expectations | Household Income & Expenditure Survey |
| **S**ocial | Lifestyle, values, demographic shifts, cultural attitudes | Omnibus attitude survey |
| **T**echnological | Tech adoption rates, innovation acceptance | Product-feature preference survey |
| **E**nvironmental | Green concerns, sustainability willingness | Eco-awareness survey |
| **L**egal | Consumer-rights awareness, regulatory compliance feedback | Compliance audit survey |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic:** "**PESTEL** = **P**owerful **E**ngine **S**canning **T**he **E**arth's **L**aws" — use this in exams to recall the six forces.

### 2.2 Classification of Survey Methods (The KTU High-Yield Table)

| # | Method | Medium | Strengths | Weaknesses | Best Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Personal (Face-to-Face) Interview** | Door-to-door / in-store | High response rate, deep probing | Expensive, interviewer bias | Industrial buyer surveys, B2B |
| 2 | **Telephone Interview** | Phone call | Fast, geographically flexible | Declining response, short attention | Quick pulse of customer satisfaction |
| 3 | **Mail Survey** | Postal questionnaire | Low cost per respondent, anonymity | Very slow, low response rate | Industrial customers with known addresses |
| 4 | **Online / E-survey** | Email, web form, app | Cheap, fast, global reach, auto-tabulation | Sample bias (digital divide) | Consumer attitude tracking, NPS |
| 5 | **In-Depth / Qualitative Survey** | One-on-one long interview | Rich, deep insight | Non-replicable, requires trained interviewer | Exploratory research on competitor moves |
| 6 | **Group (Focus Group) Survey** | Small facilitated group | Synergistic idea generation | Groupthink, dominance effect | Concept testing for new product |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are routinely used in survey-design problems and quantitative KTU questions.

| Concept | Formula | Meaning of Terms |
|---|---|---|
| Sample size (finite population) | $$n = \dfrac{N \cdot z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2 \cdot (N-1) + z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}$$ | $N$ = population, $z$ = z-score (1.96 for 95% confidence), $p$ = estimated proportion, $e$ = margin of error |
| Response rate | $$RR = \dfrac{\text{Completed questionnaires}}{\text{Total questionnaires distributed}} \times 100$$ | Expressed as a percentage |
| Net Promoter Score | $$NPS = \%P - \%D$$ | $\%P$ = Promoters (9–10 rating), $\%D$ = Detractors (0–6) |
| Margin of error | $$e = z \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}}$$ | Maximum expected deviation from true value |
| Confidence interval | $$CI = \bar{x} \pm z \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$ | Range containing the true mean with stated confidence |
| Customer Satisfaction Score | $$CSAT = \dfrac{\text{Positive responses}}{\text{Total responses}} \times 100$$ | Typically captured via a 5-point Likert survey |

**Real-world utility:** These formulas allow marketing managers to convert *qualitative environmental noise* into *quantitative strategic input* — for instance, sizing a market, predicting election outcomes (political environment), or estimating a product's adoption curve (technological environment).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Survey Research Process — Exhaustive 7-Step Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Expectation:** In Part B (14-mark) questions, examiners expect a *process diagram* + *brief explanation of each step*. Memorize these seven stages in order.

**Step 1 — Define the Problem and Research Objectives**
- Clearly articulate *what* decision the survey will inform (e.g., "Should we launch Product X in South India?").
- Specify the *information needed* (e.g., customer preferences, price sensitivity, competitor positioning).
- Formulate the **research question** in operational terms.

**Step 2 — Develop the Research Approach**
- Choose between **exploratory** (qualitative, open-ended), **descriptive** (structured surveys), or **causal** (experimental) research.
- For *marketing environment scanning*, **descriptive surveys** are most common.

**Step 3 — Design the Data Collection Form (Questionnaire)**
- Draft **closed-ended** (multiple choice, Likert scale) and **open-ended** questions.
- Apply the funnel sequence: *Easy → Sensitive → Demographics* at the end.
- Pre-test the questionnaire on a small pilot sample (typically 5%–10% of the planned main sample).

**Step 4 — Design the Sample and Sampling Procedure**
- Define the **target population**, **sampling frame**, **sample size** (using the formula in Section 2.3), and **sampling method** (random, stratified, cluster, convenience, judgemental).

**Step 5 — Collect the Data (Field Work)**
- Train interviewers, supervise the field, and log response rates.
- Apply **quality checks** to detect fabricated or incomplete responses.

**Step 6 — Prepare and Analyse the Data**
- Edit, code, tabulate, and statistically analyse.
- Use cross-tabulation, regression, factor analysis, or simple percentages depending on the objective.

**Step 7 — Present the Findings**
- Prepare a written report with executive summary, methodology, findings, conclusions, and recommendations.
- Decision-makers use this to **modify marketing mix** in response to environmental shifts.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Sample Size Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-style question:** *A company wants to survey customer satisfaction in a city of 8,000 households. They expect 50% to be satisfied, with a 95% confidence level and ±5% margin of error. Calculate the required sample size.*

**Given:**
- $N = 8000$
- $p = 0.50 \Rightarrow p(1-p) = 0.25$
- $z = 1.96$ (for 95% confidence)
- $e = 0.05$

**Substitute into the formula:**

$$n = \dfrac{N \cdot z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2 \cdot (N-1) + z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the numerator:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 8000 \times (1.96)^2 \times 0.25$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 8000 \times 3.8416 \times 0.25$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 8000 \times 0.9604$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 7683.2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator:**

$$\text{Denominator} = (0.05)^2 \times (8000 - 1) + (1.96)^2 \times 0.25$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 0.0025 \times 7999 + 3.8416 \times 0.25$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 19.9975 + 0.9604$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 20.9579$$

**Step 3 — Divide:**

$$n = \dfrac{7683.2}{20.9579} \approx 366.61$$

**Step 4 — Round up (always):**

$$\boxed{n \approx 367 \text{ households}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why round up?** A sample of 366.6 is statistically invalid; you cannot interview 0.6 of a person. The convention is to **always round up** to ensure the desired margin of error is met.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Survey Method vs. Marketing Environment Component

| Marketing Environment Component | Most Suitable Survey Method | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Customers (micro) | Online + Personal interviews | Reach volume + deep attitude probing |
| Competitors (micro) | In-depth interviews with industry experts | Competitors will not respond to your survey; surrogate experts do |
| Suppliers (micro) | Mail/Email structured questionnaire | Known, finite, B2B audience |
| Political (macro) | Delphi method (expert panels) | Forward-looking, qualitative |
| Economic (macro) | Large-scale household survey | Needs large $n$ for representativeness |
| Social (macro) | Telephone + Online omnibus surveys | Speed, cost, broad demographic coverage |
| Technological (macro) | Focus groups + Online survey | Captures aspirational and behavioural intent |
| Legal (macro) | Mail questionnaire to legal experts + secondary data | Niche, professional respondents |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — The Survey-Based Marketing Environment Scanning System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Company Strategic Goal] --> stepB[Define Research Problem]
    stepB --> stepC[Choose Research Approach]
    stepC --> stepD[Design Questionnaire]
    stepD --> stepE[Determine Sample Size and Method]
    stepE --> stepF[Collect Data via Surveys]
    stepF --> stepG[Analyse Data]
    stepG --> stepH[Report to Management]
    stepH --> stepI[Modify Marketing Mix 4Ps]
    stepI --> endZ[Improved Customer Response]

    subgraph microEnv[Micro Environment Survey Targets]
        m1[Customers] 
        m2[Suppliers]
        m3[Intermediaries]
        m4[Competitors]
        m5[Publics]
    end

    subgraph macroEnv[Macro Environment Survey Targets]
        p1[Political]
        p2[Economic]
        p3[Social]
        p4[Technological]
        p5[Environmental]
        p6[Legal]
    end

    stepF --> microEnv
    stepF --> macroEnv
    microEnv --> stepG
    macroEnv --> stepG
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Classification of Survey Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a0[Survey Methods] --> a1[Personal Interview]
    a0 --> a2[Telephone Interview]
    a0 --> a3[Mail Survey]
    a0 --> a4[Online E Survey]
    a0 --> a5[Focus Group]

    a1 --> a11[Door to Door]
    a1 --> a12[In Store Mall Intercept]
    a1 --> a13[Executive B2B]

    a2 --> a21[CATI Computer Assisted]
    a2 --> a22[Mobile Telephony]

    a3 --> a31[Postal Self Completion]
    a3 --> a32[Drop and Collect]

    a4 --> a41[Email Weblink]
    a4 --> a42[Mobile App Survey]
    a4 --> a43[Social Media Poll]

    a5 --> a51[Traditional Focus Group]
    a5 --> a52[Online Focus Group Bulletin Board]
```

### 4.3 Process Diagram — Linking Survey Output to Environmental Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    env1[Environmental Signal Identified via Survey] --> env2{Survey Result}
    env2 -- Positive --> env3[Seize Opportunity]
    env2 -- Negative --> env4[Counter Threat]
    env3 --> env5[Adjust Marketing Mix]
    env4 --> env5
    env5 --> env6[Monitor Continuously via Periodic Surveys]
    env6 --> env1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define the term "Marketing Environment". List its two broad components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Marketing Environment** consists of all internal and external forces that affect the organization's ability to create and sustain successful customer relationships. It has two broad components:
> 1. **Micro (Internal/Task) Environment** — includes the company, suppliers, marketing intermediaries, customers, competitors, and publics.
> 2. **Macro (External/Broad) Environment** — includes Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, and Legal forces (PESTEL framework).
>
> *Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for micro + 1 mark for macro.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between a "Mail Survey" and an "Online Survey". State any two advantages of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Mail Survey | Online Survey |
|---|---|---|
| Medium | Postal questionnaire | Email, web-form, app |
| Cost | Postage + printing | Near-zero per respondent |
| Speed | Weeks to months | Hours to days |
| Advantage 1 | Reaches non-internet population | Easy global distribution |
| Advantage 2 | Perceived as formal, high legitimacy | Auto-tabulates data, allows multimedia |

*2 marks for table + 1 mark for crisp conclusion.*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT** define the Marketing Environment in generic terms — the examiner wants **the PESTEL/micro-micro split** with at least one example for each layer.
> 2. **Do NOT** list survey methods without stating **when** each is best used — the *application link* to the marketing environment is the mark-earning phrase.
> 3. **Do NOT** skip the *sampling formula* if the question contains numerical data — students frequently lose 2–3 marks for skipping the substitution step.
> 4. Always draw a **labelled block diagram** of the survey process in the answer — it carries **2 marks** even if your text is brief.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**A. (a) [7 marks]** Explain the **PESTEL framework** for analysing the macro marketing environment. For each force, give one example of a **survey instrument** that can capture data on that force.

**Model Answer:**

**PESTEL** is a six-letter mnemonic for the six macro-environmental forces:

1. **P — Political:** Government policies, political stability, taxation, trade restrictions. *Survey instrument:* A **Delphi survey** of industry experts forecasting the impact of the next Union Budget on consumer durables.

2. **E — Economic:** GDP growth, inflation, disposable income, interest rates. *Survey instrument:* A **large-scale household income & expenditure survey** to estimate purchasing power across regions.

3. **S — Social:** Demographics, culture, lifestyle, education levels. *Survey instrument:* An **attitude-and-lifestyle omnibus survey** using stratified random sampling to map generational preferences.

4. **T — Technological:** Rate of innovation, R&D activity, automation, adoption of digital tools. *Survey instrument:* An **online feature-preference survey** asking customers which smart features they would pay a premium for.

5. **E — Environmental:** Climate change awareness, sustainability, waste, carbon footprint. *Survey instrument:* An **eco-awareness and willingness-to-pay survey** for green packaging.

6. **L — Legal:** Consumer protection laws, advertising standards, data privacy (e.g., DPDP Act 2023 in India). *Survey instrument:* A **compliance-feedback survey** sent to dealers regarding new labelling rules.

*Conclusion (1 mark):* Together, these six survey streams constitute the **macro-environmental intelligence system** of the firm. *(Total: 7 marks — 1 mark per force + 1 mark for the conclusion.)*

**A. (b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **advantages and limitations of Online Surveys** as a tool for studying the marketing environment. How can firms mitigate the limitations?

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages (4 marks — any 4):**
1. **Low cost per response** — no printing, postage, or travel.
2. **Speed** — global responses in hours; ideal for time-sensitive market signals.
3. **Wide reach** — can access dispersed urban and semi-urban consumers.
4. **Automatic data capture** — direct entry into Excel/SPSS, fewer manual errors.
5. **Multimedia capability** — videos, images, and skip-logic enhance engagement.
6. **Easy follow-up** — reminder emails can be scheduled automatically.

**Limitations (2 marks — any 2):**
1. **Sample bias / digital divide** — excludes elderly and rural non-internet users.
2. **Low response representativeness** — voluntary respondents may be more opinionated.
3. **Privacy and security concerns** — risk of data breach under DPDP Act 2023.
4. **Spam fatigue** — consumers ignore survey invitations.

**Mitigation Strategies (1 mark — any 1):**
- Use **mixed-mode surveys** (online + telephone + face-to-face) to cover offline populations.
- Apply **screening questions** to filter serious respondents.
- Offer incentives (discounts, gift vouchers) to lift response rates.
- Partner with rural field agencies to reach non-internet respondents.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**B. (a) [7 marks]** Describe the **seven steps of the marketing survey research process**. Illustrate with a suitable example of a company surveying the **customer component of the micro-environment**.

**Model Answer (7 marks — 1 per step):**

| Step | Description | Example: A mobile-phone company surveying its customers |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Define the problem | Identify the information need | "Why are we losing customers to Brand X?" |
| 2. Develop research approach | Choose exploratory, descriptive, or causal | Descriptive survey using structured questionnaire |
| 3. Design data collection form | Draft and pre-test questionnaire | Likert items on price, design, service, network quality |
| 4. Design sample | Define population, frame, size, method | Stratified random sample of 1,000 existing customers |
| 5. Collect data | Train interviewers, supervise fieldwork | Mixed-mode: online + telephone + mall-intercept |
| 6. Analyse data | Edit, code, tabulate, apply statistics | Cross-tabulate satisfaction vs. age group; compute NPS |
| 7. Present findings | Report with recommendations | Recommend service-quality training for underperforming regions |

**B. (b) [7 marks]** A company plans to conduct a customer satisfaction survey in a market of **10,000 customers**. The estimated proportion of satisfied customers is **60%**, with a **95% confidence level** and a **±4% margin of error**. Calculate the required sample size and interpret the result.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $N = 10000$
- $p = 0.60 \Rightarrow p(1-p) = 0.24$
- $z = 1.96$
- $e = 0.04$

**Apply the formula:**

$$n = \dfrac{N \cdot z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2 \cdot (N-1) + z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}$$

**Numerator:**

$$\text{Num} = 10000 \times (1.96)^2 \times 0.24$$

$$= 10000 \times 3.8416 \times 0.24$$

$$= 10000 \times 0.92198 = 9219.84$$

**Denominator:**

$$\text{Den} = (0.04)^2 \times (9999) + 3.8416 \times 0.24$$

$$= 0.0016 \times 9999 + 0.92198$$

$$= 15.9984 + 0.92198 = 16.92038$$

**Divide:**

$$n = \dfrac{9219.84}{16.92038} \approx 544.9$$

**Round up:**

$$\boxed{n = 545 \text{ customers}}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** The company must survey at least **545 customers** out of 10,000 to be 95% confident that the true satisfaction proportion lies within ±4% of the survey result.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision list — read this before the exam:**

- [ ] **Marketing Environment = Micro + Macro.** Micro = company, suppliers, intermediaries, customers, competitors, publics. Macro = **PESTEL**.
- [ ] **PESTEL** stands for **Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, Legal** — always list all six in order; partial listing loses marks.
- [ ] **Survey methods** = personal, telephone, mail, online, focus group. Know **at least two strengths and two weaknesses** of each.
- [ ] **Online surveys** are the *dominant modern method* — know advantages (low cost, speed) and the *digital-divide limitation*.
- [ ] **Survey research process** = 7 steps: Define problem → Approach → Questionnaire → Sample → Collect → Analyse → Report. **Memorize the order.**
- [ ] **Sample size formula** for finite population must be **substituted step-by-step** in the exam. Always **round up** the final value.
- [ ] **NPS = %Promoters − %Detractors**; **CSAT = positive responses / total responses × 100** — both are favourite 3-mark definitional questions.
- [ ] **Link every survey method to a specific environment component** in your answer — this is what makes the answer "KTU-grade".
- [ ] **Cite real-world examples** (e.g., Amazon NPS tracking, Apple competitor benchmarking) to gain the examiner's respect.
- [ ] **Draw at least one labelled diagram** in any 14-mark answer — it earns easy 2 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Pitfall:** Students often describe surveys generically *without* tying them to the marketing environment. The question is **NOT** "describe survey methods" alone — it is **"survey methods AS APPLIED TO the marketing environment."** Always end each section with one sentence showing the *application link*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro & Macro Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET638 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Micro & Macro Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Marketing Environment — Formal KTU Definition

The **Marketing Environment** refers to the external and internal forces, actors, and conditions that surround and influence an organization's ability to build and maintain successful relationships with its target customers. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the marketing environment is broadly classified into two concentric layers:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The marketing environment comprises **actors and forces** outside marketing that affect marketing management's ability to develop and maintain successful transactions with its target customers. It consists of the **Microenvironment** (immediate environment — task environment) and the **Macroenvironment** (broader environment — external environment / uncontrollable forces).

### 1.2 Microenvironment vs. Macroenvironment — Quick Distinction

| Layer | Also Called | Scope | Controllability |
|---|---|---|---|
| Micro Environment | Task Environment / Operating Environment | Forces close to the firm that directly affect its ability to serve customers | Partially controllable |
| Macro Environment | External Environment / Controllable Forces? No — **Uncontrollable Forces** | Larger societal forces that shape opportunities and pose threats | Largely uncontrollable |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Restaurant Story

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Running a Restaurant in Kochi**
> Imagine you open a small restaurant in Kochi.
> - **Micro Environment** = your head chef, your vegetable supplier, the food delivery apps (Swiggy/Zomato), your regular customers, your immediate rival restaurant next door, and food bloggers. You can choose your chef, negotiate with the supplier, and respond to customer feedback. These are the people and forces *immediately* around your business.
> - **Macro Environment** = Kerala's monsoon season, GST rules imposed by the central government, inflation in vegetable prices, Instagram food trends, the Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) regulations, and the green-movement (avoiding plastic). You *cannot* stop the monsoon or change inflation — these are the broad forces you must **adapt to**.
>
> This is exactly how Philip Kotler frames the difference — **Micro = forces you can influence**, **Macro = forces you must adapt to**.

### 1.4 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Module 1 of PEMET638 (Marketing Management) typically allocates **10–15% weightage** of the entire End Semester Examination (ESE) to environment scanning concepts. The University Examiner frequently frames Module 1 Part B questions as: *"Explain the micro and macro environment of marketing"* (14 marks) or *"Discuss PESTEL analysis with reference to an Indian company"* (14 marks). Mastering this topic is essential for scoring a high GPA in the marketing paper.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Micro Environment — The Six Forces (Philip Kotler's Framework)

The microenvironment consists of forces **close to the company** that affect its ability to serve its customers. It comprises **six key actors**:

1. **The Company** (Internal Environment)
   - All departments inside the organization — top management, finance, R&D, purchasing, operations, accounting.
   - Marketing decisions must align with the company's overall mission, resources, and capabilities.

2. **Suppliers**
   - Firms and individuals that provide the resources needed to produce goods and services.
   - **Critical Issue**: Shortages, delays, or quality issues in supplier inputs directly impact marketing operations.
   - Example: Apple sourcing semiconductors from TSMC.

3. **Marketing Intermediaries (Marketing Middlemen)**
   - Firms that help the company promote, sell, and distribute its products to final buyers.
   - Types: *Resellers* (retailers, wholesalers), *Physical distribution firms* (warehouses, transporters), *Marketing service agencies* (advertising, research), *Financial intermediaries* (banks, insurance).

4. **Customers**
   - The **most important** actor in the microenvironment.
   - Five types of customer markets:
     - **Consumer Markets** (B2C — individuals buying for personal use)
     - **Business Markets** (B2B — firms buying to use in production)
     - **Reseller Markets** (wholesalers, retailers buying to resell)
     - **Government Markets** (government bodies buying for public services)
     - **International Markets** (foreign buyers)

5. **Competitors**
   - Firms offering similar products/services competing for the same customer's rupee.
   - Types: *Brand competitors* (same product category), *Generic competitors* (substitutes), *Total budget competitors* (anything the consumer could buy with their budget).

6. **Publics**
   - Any group that has an actual or potential interest in or impact on the company's ability to achieve its objectives.
   - Types: *Financial publics*, *Media publics*, *Government publics*, *Citizen-action publics*, *Local publics*, *General public*, *Internal publics* (employees).

### 2.2 Macro Environment — The PESTEL Framework (Six Forces)

The macro environment consists of broader societal forces that shape opportunities and pose threats. The industry-standard framework is **PESTEL** (sometimes expanded from PESTLE):

1. **Political Factors**
   - Government policies, political stability, taxation policy, trade restrictions, tariff regulations.
   - Example: Make in India initiative, GST reforms, FDI limits in retail.

2. **Economic Factors**
   - Macroeconomic conditions — GDP growth, inflation, exchange rates, interest rates, unemployment, disposable income.
   - Example: RBI repo rate hikes affecting consumer credit demand.

3. **Social Factors (Sociocultural)**
   - Demographic shifts, cultural values, lifestyle changes, education levels, religion, attitudes toward work/leisure.
   - Example: Kerala's high literacy, rising nuclear families, veganism trend.

4. **Technological Factors**
   - Innovations in technology, R&D activity, automation, digitalization, rate of technological change.
   - Example: AI in marketing, UPI revolution, AR/VR shopping experiences.

5. **Environmental Factors**
   - Ecological and environmental aspects — climate change, sustainability, pollution control, renewable energy adoption.
   - Example: Plastic ban in Kerala, EV push by the Indian government.

6. **Legal Factors**
   - Laws, regulations, and legal frameworks affecting business — consumer protection, labor laws, antitrust, IP rights.
   - Example: Consumer Protection Act 2019, Competition Act 2002, IT Act 2000.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Micro vs. Macro Environment

| Force | Micro / Macro | Influence Level | KTU-Standard Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Company | Micro | Direct, Internal | **C** |
| Suppliers | Micro | Direct, External | **S** |
| Marketing Intermediaries | Micro | Direct, External | **M** |
| Customers | Micro | Direct, External | **C** |
| Competitors | Micro | Direct, External | **C** |
| Publics | Micro | Direct, External | **P** |
| Political | Macro | Indirect | **P** |
| Economic | Macro | Indirect | **E** |
| Social | Macro | Indirect | **S** |
| Technological | Macro | Indirect | **T** |
| Environmental | Macro | Indirect | **E** |
| Legal | Macro | Indirect | **L** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic (Recall Trick for Exam Hall):**
> **Micro** → **"My Successful Company Competes Publicly"** (Company, Suppliers, Marketing Intermediaries, Customers, Competitors, Publics).
> **Macro** → **"PESTEL"** (Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, Legal).

### 2.4 The "Why & How" — Practical Significance

- **Why study Micro Environment?** Because these are forces marketing managers can **directly engage with** — negotiate with suppliers, conduct customer research, beat competitors through positioning.
- **Why study Macro Environment?** Because these are forces that are **uncontrollable but inescapable**. A marketer who ignores PESTEL is like a sailor who ignores the weather forecast — disaster is inevitable.
- **How to use both?** The marketer conducts **Environmental Scanning** (collecting information about forces), **Environmental Monitoring** (tracking changes), and **Environmental Forecasting** (predicting future trends). This entire process is called **STEEP / PESTEL analysis**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Impact Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Logic Flow — How a Marketer Interacts with the Environment

To demonstrate the depth expected in a KTU 14-mark answer, here is the structured analytical logic that examiners reward:

**Step 1: Identify the Firm's Boundary**
Determine which actors lie *inside* the company (internal departments — top management, finance, R&D, operations) versus those *outside* (suppliers, customers, etc.).

**Step 2: Map the Microenvironment Forces**
Map each microenvironmental actor to its specific impact on marketing decision-making:
- A supplier delay → stockouts → lost sales.
- A competitor's price cut → need for repricing or value-add.
- A customer shift to online buying → need for digital channel investment.

**Step 3: Scan the Macroenvironment (PESTEL)**
For each of the six PESTEL factors, ask three questions:
1. *What is the current state of this force?*
2. *How is it changing (trend)?*
3. *How does this trend create an opportunity or threat for the firm?*

**Step 4: Synthesize an Environmental Analysis**
Combine micro and macro insights into a coherent strategic picture — typically expressed via **SWOT Analysis** (Strengths, Weaknesses = internal/micro; Opportunities, Threats = external/macro).

**Step 5: Adapt the Marketing Mix (4Ps / 7Ps)**
Adjust **Product**, **Price**, **Place**, **Promotion** (and People, Process, Physical Evidence for services) in response to environmental forces.

### 3.2 Detailed Impact Matrix — PESTEL Applied to an Indian Company

For a 14-mark KTU answer, examiners expect you to discuss PESTEL **with reference to a real company** (commonly tested: Nestlé India, Apple, IKEA, Maruti Suzuki, or any Kerala-based enterprise like Kitex Garments). Below is a worked analytical framework using **Nestlé India Limited** as the reference case.

**Step 1 — Political Factors:**
- Nestlé India must comply with FSSAI standards and the Government of India's food labeling laws.
- Political decisions like GST on packaged foods (currently 12%–18%) directly affect pricing strategy.
- Recent government push for local sourcing under "Make in India" influenced Nestlé's Maggi sourcing from Indian wheat.

**Step 2 — Economic Factors:**
- India is the **world's 5th largest economy** with GDP growth ~7%, providing Nestlé with growing middle-class demand.
- High inflation in milk prices (2023–2024) pressured Nestlé's input costs — forcing premium pricing on Munch and KitKat.
- Rupee depreciation against the Swiss Franc impacted royalty payments to the parent company.

**Step 3 — Social Factors:**
- India has a young median age (~28 years), favoring confectionery and snack categories.
- Rising health consciousness has boosted demand for Nestlé's "Maggi Atta Noodles" and low-sugar variants.
- Cultural festivals (Diwali, Onam, Christmas) drive seasonal SKU rollouts.

**Step 4 — Technological Factors:**
- Nestlé deployed AI-driven demand forecasting in 2022.
- E-commerce boom (Amazon, BigBasket) created direct-to-consumer channels.
- Smart packaging and QR-code traceability align with tech-savvy urban consumers.

**Step 5 — Environmental Factors:**
- Plastic Waste Management Rules (India) forced Nestlé to redesign Maggi packaging.
- Kerala floods (2018, 2019) disrupted milk collection from Wayanad — prompting supply chain diversification.
- Sustainability push → Nestlé's commitment to 100% recyclable packaging by 2025.

**Step 6 — Legal Factors:**
- Consumer Protection Act 2019 (replaced 1986 Act) heightened liability for misleading ads — Maggi's 2015 ban was a landmark case.
- Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 governs product recalls.
- Competition Commission of India scrutinizes anti-competitive practices.

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Equations — Market Opportunity Index

Although Marketing Management is qualitative, KTU 2024 Scheme values quantitative reasoning. Marketers often calculate an **Environmental Opportunity Index (EOI)** to size opportunities:

$$EOI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (O_i \times W_i) - \sum_{j=1}^{m} (T_j \times W_j)$$

Where:
- $O_i$ = magnitude of opportunity $i$ (on a 1–10 scale).
- $W_i$ = weight assigned to opportunity $i$ (probability of capture, 0.0 to 1.0).
- $T_j$ = magnitude of threat $j$ (on a 1–10 scale).
- $W_j$ = weight assigned to threat $j$ (probability of occurrence, 0.0 to 1.0).

**Interpretation Rule (KTU Standard):**

$$EOI > 0 \implies \text{Favorable Environmental Position (Proceed aggressively)}$$

$$EOI = 0 \implies \text{Neutral Environmental Position (Hold strategy)}$$

$$EOI < 0 \implies \text{Unfavorable Environmental Position (Retreat/Divest)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example for KTU:**
> Nestlé identifies two macro opportunities:
> - Health-conscious consumers (magnitude $O_1 = 8$, weight $W_1 = 0.7$).
> - Rural India demand (magnitude $O_2 = 7$, weight $W_2 = 0.6$).
> And two macro threats:
> - Sugar tax (magnitude $T_1 = 6$, weight $W_1 = 0.8$).
> - Plastic ban compliance cost (magnitude $T_2 = 5$, weight $W_2 = 0.5$).
>
> Computing the EOI:

$$EOI = (8 \times 0.7) + (7 \times 0.6) - (6 \times 0.8) - (5 \times 0.5)$$

$$EOI = 5.6 + 4.2 - 4.8 - 2.5$$

$$EOI = 9.8 - 7.3 = 2.5$$

Since $EOI = 2.5 > 0$, the environmental position is favorable — proceed with growth strategy.

### 3.4 The 5Cs Framework (Modern Synthesis)

Modern KTU textbooks (Philip Kotler & Keller, 16th edition onwards) merge micro and macro analysis using the **5Cs Framework**:

1. **Company** — internal capabilities.
2. **Customers** — needs, segments, behavior.
3. **Competitors** — direct, indirect, future.
4. **Collaborators** — suppliers, distributors, partners.
5. **Context** — PESTEL (macro environment).

This is essentially a **bridge between micro and macro analysis**, and KTU examiners frequently award bonus marks for mentioning it.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
