The Green Premium quantifies the price consumers are willing to pay for a sustainable product relative to its conventional counterpart.

**Given:**
- $P_{green}$ = Price of the green product (₹/unit)
- $P_{conv}$ = Price of the conventional product (₹/unit)

**Step 1:** Compute the absolute price difference.

$$
\Delta P = P_{green} - P_{conv}
$$

**Step 2:** Normalize by the conventional baseline price to get a relative measure.

$$
GP_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{\Delta P}{P_{conv}}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to a percentage for board reporting.

$$
GP \ (\%) = \frac{P_{green} - P_{conv}}{P_{conv}} \times 100
$$

**Numerical Illustration (KTU-Style):**
> A conventional detergent costs ₹200. The same brand's "eco-friendly" version costs ₹260. Calculate the Green Premium.

$$
GP = \frac{260 - 200}{200} \times 100 = \frac{60}{200} \times 100 = 30 \ \%
$$

**Interpretation:** Consumers pay a **30% premium** for the green attribute. KTU marks are awarded only if you state the *managerial implication* — e.g., "A 30% premium is sustainable if the perceived environmental value exceeds the price differential."

### 3.2 Derivation: Eco-Efficiency Ratio (EER)

The World Business Council for Sustainable Development (WBCSD) popularized EER as a strategic metric.

**Given:**
- $V$ = Value delivered to the customer (functional + emotional worth, ₹)
- $E$ = Environmental influence across the lifecycle (CO₂e kg)

**Step 1:** The WBCSD model states that eco-efficiency is achieved by *maximizing value* and *minimizing environmental influence* simultaneously.

$$
EER = \frac{V}{E}
$$

**Step 2:** For cross-product comparison, normalize using a baseline.

$$
EER_{\text{index}} = \frac{EER_{product}}{EER_{baseline}} \times 100
$$

**Step 3:** An $EER_{index} > 100$ implies the product is **more eco-efficient** than the baseline; $< 100$ implies it is **less eco-efficient**.

**Illustration:**
- Product A delivers ₹500 value with 10 kg CO₂e footprint → $EER_A = 50$
- Product B (baseline) delivers ₹400 value with 10 kg CO₂e footprint → $EER_B = 40$

$$
EER_{index} = \frac{50}{40} \times 100 = 125
$$

**Managerial Insight:** Product A is **25% more eco-efficient** than Product B and can justify a premium.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Opportunities vs. Challenges in Green Marketing

| Dimension | Opportunities (Strengths) | Challenges (Weaknesses) |
|---|---|---|
| **Consumer** | Rising eco-consciousness, LOHAS segment growth, willingness-to-pay premium. | Green fatigue, skepticism, lack of awareness in Tier-2/3 markets. |
| **Firm** | Cost savings from efficiency, brand differentiation, access to ESG capital. | Higher R&D costs, supply-chain complexity, greenwashing risk. |
| **Regulatory** | Government incentives, carbon credits, tax rebates. | Compliance burden, dynamic regulations, certification costs. |
| **Competitive** | First-mover advantage, strategic moats. | Imitation by rivals, undercutting by non-green competitors. |
| **Technology** | Renewable energy, AI-driven waste optimization. | Capital-intensive, technology obsolescence. |

### 3.4 The Green Consumer Adoption Curve (Rogers' Diffusion Applied)

The KTU syllabus often asks students to map green product adoption to **Rogers' Diffusion of Innovation Theory**.

| Stage | % of Adopters | Green-Consumer Profile |
|---|---|---|
| **Innovators (2.5%)** | First buyers of EVs, organic-only diets. |
| **Early Adopters (13.5%)** | LOHAS consumers, brand-loyal eco-buyers. |
| **Early Majority (34%)** | Price-sensitive but eco-aware mass market. |
| **Late Majority (34%)** | Adopt only when green is the default, not premium. |
| **Laggards (16%)** | Resistant to change; require subsidies/mandates. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Mass-market green marketing campaigns should target the *Early Majority* — they require **proof points, social validation, and affordability**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Green Marketing Strategic Flow

The following Mermaid block maps the **end-to-end strategic flow** of green marketing — from environmental audit to consumer communication.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1: Environmental Audit] --> A2[Step 2: Green Market Research]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Green Positioning Strategy]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Green Product Design and Development]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Sustainable Sourcing and Production]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Green Pricing and Eco-labeling]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7: Green Distribution and Reverse Logistics]
    A7 --> A8[Step 8: Green Promotion and CSR Communication]
    A8 --> A9[Step 9: Consumer Feedback and Continuous Improvement]
    A9 --> A1

    A4 -.->|Parallel Track| B1[Lifecycle Assessment LCA]
    A5 -.->|Parallel Track| B2[Carbon Footprint Analysis]
    A6 -.->|Parallel Track| B3[ISO 14001 Certification]
    A7 -.->|Parallel Track| B4[Reverse Supply Chain]
    A8 -.->|Parallel Track| B5[Sustainability Reporting GRI]
```

### 4.2 The Three Pillars of Green Marketing

```mermaid
graph TD
    Pillar1[Planet: Environmental Stewardship]
    Pillar2[People: Social Responsibility]
    Pillar3[Profit: Economic Viability]

    Pillar1 --- Detail1[Carbon reduction, waste management, biodiversity]
    Pillar2 --- Detail2[Fair trade, community welfare, employee safety]
    Pillar3 --- Detail3[Long-term profitability, cost efficiency, shareholder value]

    Pillar1 --> Outcome[True Sustainability and Brand Trust]
    Pillar2 --> Outcome
    Pillar3 --> Outcome
```

### 4.3 The 4Ps of Green Marketing Mix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Mix["Green Marketing Mix Framework"]
        P1[Green Product] --> M1[Biodegradable, energy-efficient, recyclable]
        P2[Green Price] --> M2[Reflects environmental cost, includes green premium]
        P3[Green Place] --> M3[Eco-logistics, reverse supply chain]
        P4[Green Promotion] --> M4[Transparent claims, eco-labels, sustainability reports]
    end

    P1 --> Center[Sustainable Value Proposition]
    P2 --> Center
    P3 --> Center
    P4 --> Center
    Center --> Result[Customer Satisfaction plus Environmental Benefit]
```

### 4.4 Greenwashing vs. Authentic Green Marketing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Claim1[Company makes green claim]
    Claim1 --> Q1{Is it verifiable and substantive?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Path1[Authentic Green Marketing]
    Q1 -->|No| Path2[Greenwashing Risk]

    Path1 --> Out1[Long-term trust, brand equity, regulatory safety]
    Path2 --> Out2[Penalties, brand damage, consumer backlash]

    Path2 --> Action[Correct via third-party audit, transparent reporting]
    Action --> Path1
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in KTU exams:** When asked to "illustrate the green marketing framework," sketch a *flowchart* showing the audit → positioning → 4Ps loop. Mermaid-rendered equivalents receive full marks when labeled clearly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Green Marketing. List any four reasons why firms adopt it.*

**Model Answer:**

Green Marketing is the marketing of products and services based on their **environmental benefits**. It includes a wide range of activities such as product modification, eco-friendly packaging, sustainable production, and changes in advertising, designed to reduce the negative environmental impact of consumption.

**Four reasons firms adopt it:**

1. **Competitive advantage** through differentiation in eco-conscious segments.
2. **Cost reduction** via energy and resource efficiency.
3. **Compliance** with environmental regulations (e.g., Plastic Waste Management Rules 2022, India).
4. **Enhanced brand image and consumer trust**, particularly among the LOHAS demographic.

*(Word limit: ~80–100 words. Marks awarded for definition + 4 distinct reasons.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is greenwashing? Why is it dangerous for a firm's long-term brand equity?*

**Model Answer:**

**Greenwashing** is the practice of making **misleading or unsubstantiated environmental claims** to project an eco-friendly image without substantive sustainability actions.

**Why it is dangerous:**

- **Erodes consumer trust** – Once exposed, the brand's credibility collapses.
- **Regulatory penalties** – Bodies like the Advertising Standards Council of India (ASCI) and the Competition Commission of India (CCI) impose fines.
- **Litigation risk** – Class-action lawsuits from misled consumers.
- **Long-term brand equity destruction** – The brand is permanently associated with deception.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for explanation of dangers.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **4Ps of the Green Marketing Mix** in detail with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **major challenges and opportunities** that Indian companies face while implementing green marketing strategies. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The Green Marketing Mix adapts the traditional 4Ps to embed environmental sustainability at every stage of the value chain.

**1. Green Product:**
- A product designed, manufactured, operated, and disposed of with **reduced environmental impact**.
- Attributes: biodegradable, energy-efficient, recyclable, non-toxic.
- *Example:* Tata Nexon EV (zero tailpipe emissions), PaperBoat beverages (tetra-pack with low carbon footprint).

**2. Green Price:**
- Reflects the **true environmental cost** of production, including externality pricing.
- Consumers often pay a **green premium** for verified eco-benefits.
- *Example:* Organic mangoes cost 30–50% more than conventionally grown mangoes due to lower yields and certification costs.

**3. Green Place (Distribution):**
- Uses **eco-friendly logistics** — electric vehicles, consolidated shipments, optimized routes.
- Implements **reverse logistics** — product take-back for recycling.
- *Example:* IKEA's furniture buy-back program; Amazon's "frustration-free packaging."

**4. Green Promotion:**
- Communicates environmental claims **honestly and transparently** to avoid greenwashing.
- Tools: eco-labels (e.g., BEE Star Rating, India Organic Logo), sustainability reports (GRI framework), digital storytelling.
- *Example:**The Body Shop's "Forever Against Animal Testing" campaign.*

*(7 marks: ~1.5 marks per P, plus 1 mark for coherent integration.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Challenges in Green Marketing in India:**

1. **High Cost of Production** – Sustainable inputs, R&D, and certification are capital-intensive.
2. **Consumer Skepticism** – Greenwashing history has made Indian consumers wary of unverified claims.
3. **Lack of Standardization** – Multiple certification bodies create confusion (e.g., India Organic vs. Jaivik Bharat).
4. **Supply Chain Complexity** – Reverse logistics is underdeveloped outside metro cities.
5. **Regulatory Inconsistency** – State-level variation in plastic bans and waste-management rules.

**Opportunities in Green Marketing in India:**

1. **Rising LOHAS Segment** – Urban millennials willing to pay premiums for sustainable brands.
2. **Government Push** – Swachh Bharat Mission, FAME-II (EV subsidies), Perform-Achieve-Trade (PAT) scheme.
3. **Digital Enablement** – Apps like EcoBiz connect green businesses with conscious consumers.
4. **Export Potential** – Global markets demand ISO 14001-certified suppliers; Indian organic exports to EU are growing.
5. **First-Mover Advantage** – Indian firms entering now can build long-term brand moats in the sustainability space.

*(7 marks: 4 marks for challenges with examples, 3 marks for opportunities, or proportional allocation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** "Green Marketing is not just about products; it is a strategic philosophy." Critically analyze this statement with reference to the **three pillars of sustainability**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step process** of implementing a green marketing strategy in a consumer-electronics firm. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The statement is **valid and supported** by contemporary management thought. Green Marketing is fundamentally a **strategic philosophy** that requires firms to align their mission, processes, and offerings with the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** — Planet, People, Profit.

**Analysis using the Three Pillars:**

**1. Planet (Environmental Pillar):**
- A green firm embeds **ecological thinking** into product design, sourcing, and disposal.
- *Example:* Apple's commitment to be 100% carbon-neutral across its supply chain by 2030 reflects a philosophical, not just product-level, change.

**2. People (Social Pillar):**
- Green marketing philosophy extends to **fair labor, community development, and ethical sourcing**.
- *Example:* H&M's "Conscious Collection" supports garment workers with living wages.

**3. Profit (Economic Pillar):**
- True green marketing is **financially sustainable** — it should not be a CSR afterthought.
- *Example:* Unilever's Sustainable Living Plan generated €2 billion in cost savings by 2020 while reducing environmental impact.

**Critical View:** Critics argue that green marketing without substantive change is **performative activism (greenwashing)**. Hence, the philosophy must be backed by **measurable KPIs**, third-party audits, and transparent reporting.

*(7 marks: 3 for stating pillars with examples, 2 for synthesis, 2 for critical counter-perspective.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step-by-Step Green Marketing Implementation — Consumer Electronics Firm:**

**Step 1: Environmental Audit**
- Measure current carbon footprint across manufacturing, packaging, logistics, and product use-phase.
- *Tool:* ISO 14064-1 GHG inventory.

**Step 2: Green Market Segmentation**
- Identify the **LOHAS consumer** segment — typically urban, 25–45 years, willing to pay 15–25% premium.

**Step 3: Green Product Re-engineering**
- Replace hazardous materials (lead, mercury, cadmium) with RoHS-compliant alternatives.
- Design for **energy efficiency** (e.g., 5-star BEE-rated appliances).
- Plan for **modular design** enabling easy repair and recycling.

**Step 4: Sustainable Packaging**
- Use recycled paper, soy-based inks, and biodegradable plastic alternatives.
- *Example:* Dell's bamboo packaging for laptops.

**Step 5: Green Pricing Strategy**
- Set the **green premium** based on lifecycle cost savings communicated to consumers.
- Offer **trade-in discounts** for old electronics to incentivize circularity.

**Step 6: Green Distribution & Reverse Logistics**
- Set up **e-waste collection centers** in retail outlets.
- Partner with certified e-waste recyclers.

**Step 7: Green Promotion**
- Run campaigns highlighting **measurable environmental impact** ("Your TV saved 200 kWh/year").
- Use **eco-labels** (BEE, EPEAT, Energy Star) prominently.
- Publish an annual **Sustainability Report** following GRI Standards.

**Step 8: Continuous Improvement**
- Conduct **lifecycle assessments (LCA)** at every stage.
- Engage in **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycles to iteratively improve.

*(7 marks: ~1 mark per step with a clear example, plus 1 mark for integration.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Green Marketing" with "Greenwashing"** — Marks are deducted when students equate the two; one is genuine, the other is deceptive.
> 2. **Mere definition in 14-mark answers** — KTU requires *application, examples, and critical evaluation*. A definition-only answer fetches ≤ 3 marks.
> 3. **No Indian examples** — Kerala/Karnataka/regional case studies (e.g., KSPCB, NIPUN Kerala) score higher than generic global ones.
> 4. **Skipping the "Why"** — Always link green marketing to **consumer value + business value + societal value**. The Triple Bottom Line is the KTU examiner's favorite phrase.
> 5. **Ignoring 3Ps extension** — Many students miss the **People, Process, Physical Evidence** extension in service-based green marketing answers.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Green Marketing** = Marketing with minimal negative environmental impact; rooted in the **Triple Bottom Line** (Planet, People, Profit).
- **3 Key Drivers:** Consumer demand, regulation, competitive advantage.
- **Green Marketing Mix:** Green Product + Green Price + Green Place + Green Promotion (+ People, Process, Physical Evidence).
- **Greenwashing** is the biggest reputational risk — always verify claims with **third-party certification (ISO 14001, GRI, BEE, EPEAT)**.
- **6-Step Process:** Audit → Research → Positioning → Product Design → Pricing → Distribution → Promotion → Feedback.
- **Green Premium Formula:** $GP = \frac{P_{green} - P_{conv}}{P_{conv}} \times 100$ — must be justified by perceived environmental value.
- **Eco-Efficiency** = $\frac{Value}{Environmental\ Influence}$ — higher is better.
- **Adoption Curve:** Target the **Early Majority** for mass-market green campaigns.
- **Indian Context:** FAME-II, Swachh Bharat, Plastic Waste Management Rules 2022, KSPCB regulations are exam-relevant.
- **Avoid** equating green marketing *only* with products — it is a **strategic philosophy** spanning the entire value chain.
- **Quote for KTU answers:** *"Sustainability is no longer about doing less harm. It's about doing more good."* — Unilever Sustainable Living Plan.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Consumer Behaviour: Consumer Psychology - Choice Criteria - Order Management Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET638 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Consumer Behaviour: Consumer Psychology - Choice Criteria - Order Management Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Consumer Behaviour

**Definition:** Consumer Behaviour is the systematic study of how individuals, groups, or organizations select, buy, use, and dispose of goods, services, ideas, or experiences to satisfy needs and wants. According to the **American Marketing Association (AMA)**, it is the dynamic interaction of affect and cognition, behaviour, and environmental events by which human beings conduct the exchange aspects of their lives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Consumer Behaviour is not just the act of buying; it includes the *entire process* from problem recognition to post-purchase evaluation. The 2024 scheme emphasizes the **psychological and cognitive dimensions** of the consumer, not just the transactional outcome.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Think of consumer behaviour like a **scripted movie shoot**. The consumer is the actor, the product is the prop, the store/website is the set, and the marketing mix (4Ps) is the director. Before the camera rolls (the actual purchase), there is:
- A *storyboard* (problem recognition),
- A *rehearsal* (information search),
- A *casting call* (evaluation of alternatives),
- The *final take* (purchase decision),
- And a *review after release* (post-purchase behaviour).

The actor's *psychology* — what motivates them, what they perceive, what they remember, and what they learn — determines how the scene plays out.

### 1.2 Consumer Psychology

**Definition:** Consumer Psychology is the branch of behavioural science that studies the **mental processes** — perception, motivation, learning, attitude formation, and memory — that influence how consumers interpret marketing stimuli and make purchase decisions.

> [!NOTE]
> The four pillars of consumer psychology (often called the "**Four Pillar Model of Buyer Psyche**") are:
> 1. **Motivation** — the driving force (needs/wants)
> 2. **Perception** — how stimuli are received and interpreted
> 3. **Learning** — changes in behaviour arising from experience
> 4. **Attitude** — lasting evaluations that predispose responses

### 1.3 Choice Criteria

**Definition:** Choice Criteria are the **specific attributes or dimensions** that a consumer uses to evaluate competing brands or products in the consideration set. They form the cognitive yardstick against which alternatives are measured.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Common Choice Criteria in KTU-marketed case studies include: **Price, Quality, Brand Reputation, Features, After-Sales Service, Warranty, Availability, and Aesthetics.** The relative weightage assigned to each criterion differs by individual, product category, and purchase context.

### 1.4 Order Management Cycle

**Definition:** The Order Management Cycle (OMC) is the **end-to-end operational process** that tracks an order from initial placement through fulfillment, delivery, and post-delivery service. It bridges the consumer's purchase decision (demand side) with the firm's logistics capability (supply side).

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine ordering a **birthday cake from a bakery**:
- You *place the order* (flavour, size, message).
- The bakery *acknowledges* and *processes* it (mixing, baking).
- It is *packed* and *dispatched* (delivery).
- You *receive* it and provide *feedback* (was it fresh? on time?).
- If needed, the cake is *returned/exchanged*.

This entire journey — from the call/click to the bite and the feedback — is the Order Management Cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric in Bold:** A well-optimized OMC achieves a **Perfect Order Rate (POR) ≥ 95%**, where an order is "perfect" if it is delivered on time, complete, undamaged, and with accurate documentation. **OTIF (On-Time-In-Full)** is the standard KTU-aligned operational KPI.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

### 2.1 The Consumer Decision-Making Process (Engel-Blackwell-Miniard Model)

The EBM Model is the **canonical KTU-recommended framework** and proceeds through five sequential stages:

1. **Need Recognition** — The consumer identifies a gap between the desired state and the actual state. Triggered by internal cues (hunger, thirst) or external cues (an advertisement, peer influence).
2. **Information Search** — The consumer seeks internal memory (past experience) and external sources (web reviews, friends, retail visits). Classified as *internal search* or *external search*.
3. **Evaluation of Alternatives** — Each option is judged against a set of **Choice Criteria**. The consumer forms an *evoked set* (brands considered) and uses compensatory or non-compensatory decision rules.
4. **Purchase Decision** — The consumer selects the brand that maximizes perceived value. This stage is vulnerable to **situational factors** (store ambience, sales pressure) and **anticipated regret**.
5. **Post-Purchase Behaviour** — The consumer experiences either **satisfaction** (when performance ≥ expectation) or **dissonance** (when performance < expectation). This drives repurchase, word-of-mouth, and complaints.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage — The Psychological Drivers

| Stage | Psychological Driver | Marketing Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Need Recognition | **Motivation** (Maslow's hierarchy, drives) | Stimulate latent needs through advertising |
| Information Search | **Perception** (selective exposure, attention) | Ensure brand is salient at point of search |
| Evaluation of Alternatives | **Learning & Attitude** (multi-attribute model) | Position brand on most-valued attributes |
| Purchase Decision | **Perceived Risk & Trust** | Provide guarantees, testimonials, easy returns |
| Post-Purchase Behaviour | **Cognitive Dissonance & Memory** | Follow-up communication, loyalty programs |

### 2.3 Multi-Attribute Choice Model — The KTU Analytical Engine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For examination problems involving consumer evaluation of alternatives, KTU frequently tests the **Multi-Attribute Attitude Model (Fishbein Model)**.

The consumer's overall attitude toward a brand is computed as:

$$
A_0 \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot b_i
$$

Where:
- $A_0$ = overall attitude score toward the brand
- $w_i$ = importance weight (salience) of attribute $i$
- $b_i$ = belief strength that the brand possesses attribute $i$
- $n$ = total number of attributes

**Variants of the Multi-Attribute Model:**

- **Compensatory Rule (Weighted Sum):** A strong score on one attribute can offset a weak score on another. Used when $A_0 = \sum w_i b_i$.
- **Non-Compensatory Rules:**
  - *Conjunctive Rule:* Reject brand if it falls below a minimum threshold on **any single** critical attribute.
  - *Disjunctive Rule:* Accept brand if it exceeds threshold on **at least one** attribute.
  - *Lexicographic Rule:* Rank attributes by importance; choose brand that scores highest on the **most important** attribute.
  - *Elimination-by-Aspects Rule:* Apply minimum cutoff on the most important attribute, then proceed to the next, eliminating brands that fail.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Framework / Concept | Key Components | Exam Use |
|---|---|---|
| **EBM 5-Stage Model** | Need → Search → Evaluate → Purchase → Post | Full-mark descriptive answers |
| **Four Pillars of Psychology** | Motivation, Perception, Learning, Attitude | 3-mark definitions |
| **Multi-Attribute Formula** | $A_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i b_i$ | Numerical problems |
| **Compensatory Rule** | Weighted-sum trade-off allowed | Decision logic questions |
| **Non-Compensatory Rules** | Conjunctive / Disjunctive / Lexicographic | Comparative analysis |
| **Cognitive Dissonance** | Post-purchase mental tension | Post-purchase case questions |
| **Order Management Cycle** | Order Entry → Processing → Fulfillment → Delivery → Returns | Process-flow questions |
| **Perfect Order Rate (POR)** | $POR = \frac{\text{Perfect Orders}}{\text{Total Orders}} \times 100$ | 14-mark logistics case |
| **OTIF (On-Time-In-Full)** | Delivered correctly + on time | Quality-of-service metrics |

### 2.5 The Order Management Cycle — Stage-Wise Theoretical Breakdown

1. **Order Generation & Capture** — Order received via POS, EDI, e-commerce portal, or call centre. Data integrity is critical: the *single source of truth* (SKU, quantity, price) must be captured.
2. **Order Acknowledgement** — Confirmation sent to the customer; reduces anxiety and *perceived risk*.
3. **Order Processing & Inventory Allocation** — Cross-functional coordination with warehouse; available-to-promise (ATP) check.
4. **Picking, Packing & Dispatch** — Fulfillment at warehouse; this is where *perceived quality* is sealed.
5. **Shipment & In-Transit Visibility** — Logistics tracking; meets the consumer's *expectation of timeliness*.
6. **Delivery & Proof of Delivery (POD)** — Last-mile interaction; influences *post-purchase satisfaction*.
7. **Post-Delivery Service & Returns (Reverse Logistics)** — Handles damaged/unsatisfactory goods; critical for *customer lifetime value (CLV)*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The OMC is the operational backbone of e-commerce giants (Amazon, Flipkart). A robust OMC directly reduces cart-abandonment rates (industry average **70.19%**, Baymard Institute benchmark) and improves customer retention.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Computations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Multi-Attribute Attitude Score

**Problem Setup (typical KTU-style problem):**
A consumer is evaluating three smartphone brands — **Alpha, Beta, and Gamma** — on four attributes: Price, Camera, Battery, and Brand. The importance weights $w_i$ and belief scores $b_i$ (on a 1–10 scale) are tabulated below.

| Attribute | Weight $w_i$ | $b_i$ (Alpha) | $b_i$ (Beta) | $b_i$ (Gamma) |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Price | 0.30 | 8 | 6 | 9 |
| Camera | 0.25 | 7 | 9 | 6 |
| Battery | 0.25 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
| Brand | 0.20 | 9 | 8 | 7 |

**Step 1 — Formalize the formula.**

$$
A_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot b_i
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $A_0$ for Alpha.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0^{Alpha} &= w_{Price} \cdot b_{Price} + w_{Camera} \cdot b_{Camera} + w_{Battery} \cdot b_{Battery} + w_{Brand} \cdot b_{Brand} \\
&= (0.30 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 9) \\
&= 2.40 + 1.75 + 1.50 + 1.80 \\
&= 7.45
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $A_0$ for Beta.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0^{Beta} &= (0.30 \times 6) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 8) \\
&= 1.80 + 2.25 + 1.75 + 1.60 \\
&= 7.40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $A_0$ for Gamma.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0^{Gamma} &= (0.30 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 6) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 7) \\
&= 2.70 + 1.50 + 2.00 + 1.40 \\
&= 7.60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Decision Rule.**

Since $A_0^{Gamma} = 7.60 > A_0^{Alpha} = 7.45 > A_0^{Beta} = 7.40$, the consumer will **choose Gamma** under the compensatory rule.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does Gamma win despite Beta having the best Camera (9)?** Because the compensatory rule allows Gamma's strong Price score (9) to compensate for its weak Camera score (6). Under a *Lexicographic Rule*, the answer would have differed — Brand would have been the deciding factor.

### 3.2 Computation of Perfect Order Rate (POR)

**Setup:** A regional e-commerce firm processes **12,000 orders** in a quarter. The audit reveals the following defects:
- 180 delivered late
- 90 delivered incomplete
- 60 delivered with damaged goods
- 45 with incorrect invoice
- *Assume these defects are mutually exclusive for simplicity in a KTU exam setting.*

**Step 1 — Total Defective Orders.**

$$
D_{total} = 180 + 90 + 60 + 45 = 375
$$

**Step 2 — Number of Perfect Orders.**

$$
O_{perfect} = 12000 - 375 = 11625
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the POR formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
POR &= \frac{O_{perfect}}{O_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{11625}{12000} \times 100 \\
&= 96.875\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

$$
POR \approx 96.88\% \;\geq\; 95\% \text{ (industry benchmark)}
$$

The firm's OMC is **healthy and above industry benchmark**. However, the *Late Delivery* defect is the largest contributor (180 out of 375 = 48% of all defects), so the firm should invest in **logistics and last-mile optimization** first.

### 3.3 Lexicographic Rule Application (Contrast with Compensatory)

Using the *same* belief and weight table, apply the Lexicographic Rule:

1. Rank attributes by weight: Price (0.30) > Camera (0.25) = Battery (0.25) > Brand (0.20).
2. On **Price**, the highest belief is **Gamma (9)**.
3. Therefore, Gamma is chosen — same as compensatory here, but the *reasoning path* is different.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners value **explicit articulation of the decision rule** in any evaluation problem. Always state which rule is being applied and why.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Consumer Decision-Making Process (EBM Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need Recognition] --> B[Information Search]
    B --> C[Evaluation of Alternatives]
    C --> D[Purchase Decision]
    D --> E[Post-Purchase Behaviour]
    E --> F{Satisfied?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Brand Loyalty + Word of Mouth]
    F -- No --> H[Cognitive Dissonance / Complaint]
    H --> A
    G --> A

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 The Four Pillars of Consumer Psychology (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Psyche[Consumer Psyche]
        M[Motivation]
        P[Perception]
        L[Learning]
        At[Attitude]
    end

    M --> Decision[Purchase Decision]
    P --> Decision
    L --> Decision
    At --> Decision

    Decision --> Behavior[Consumer Behaviour]
    Behavior --> Feedback[Feedback to Marketer]
    Feedback --> Psyche

    style M fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style L fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style At fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.3 Order Management Cycle (End-to-End Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O1[Order Capture - POS, EDI, Web] --> O2[Order Acknowledgement]
    O2 --> O3[Inventory Check - ATP]
    O3 -->|Available| O4[Order Processing]
    O3 -->|Not Available| O3A[Backorder / Substitute]
    O3A --> O4
    O4 --> O5[Picking and Packing]
    O5 --> O6[Dispatch and In-Transit]
    O6 --> O7[Last-Mile Delivery]
    O7 --> O8[Proof of Delivery POD]
    O8 --> O9{Return or Issue?}
    O9 -- No --> O10[Post-Delivery Engagement]
    O9 -- Yes --> O11[Reverse Logistics and Refund]
    O10 --> O12[Customer Feedback Loop]
    O11 --> O12
    O12 --> O1

    style O1 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style O7 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style O12 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.4 Multi-Attribute Decision Engine (Compensatory vs. Non-Compensatory)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Consumer Inputs Weights and Beliefs] --> Rule{Decision Rule?}
    Rule -- Compensatory --> Sum[Weighted Sum A0]
    Rule -- Conjunctive --> Conj[Reject if any attribute below threshold]
    Rule -- Disjunctive --> Disj[Accept if any attribute above threshold]
    Rule -- Lexicographic --> Lex[Rank by most important attribute first]
    Sum --> Choice[Final Brand Choice]
    Conj --> Choice
    Disj --> Choice
    Lex --> Choice

    style Sum fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style Choice fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Consumer Behaviour. Briefly explain the four pillars of Consumer Psychology.**

**Model Answer:**

Consumer Behaviour is the study of how individuals, groups, or organizations select, buy, use, and dispose of goods, services, ideas, or experiences to satisfy needs and wants. It encompasses the **entire decision journey** — from problem recognition to post-purchase evaluation — and is influenced by psychological, personal, social, and cultural factors.

The four pillars of Consumer Psychology are:

1. **Motivation:** The internal drive that activates behaviour. Maslow's Hierarchy (physiological → safety → social → esteem → self-actualization) is the classical model.
2. **Perception:** The process by which a consumer selects, organizes, and interprets sensory information. It includes *selective exposure, selective attention,* and *selective distortion*.
3. **Learning:** Changes in behaviour arising from experience. Includes classical conditioning, operant conditioning, and cognitive learning.
4. **Attitude:** A learned predisposition to respond consistently toward a brand or product. Tri-component model: cognitive (belief), affective (feeling), conative (intent).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked to "briefly explain," restrict each pillar to 2–3 lines. Do not exceed 100 words per pillar in a 3-mark question.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What are Choice Criteria? List any four common choice criteria used by consumers while evaluating a laptop.**

**Model Answer:**

Choice Criteria are the **specific attributes or dimensions** that a consumer uses to evaluate and compare competing brands or alternatives in the consideration set. They represent the cognitive yardstick against which products are judged.

Four common choice criteria for a laptop are:
1. **Price** — affordability and value-for-money.
2. **Performance** — processor speed, RAM, storage.
3. **Brand Reputation** — reliability and trust in manufacturer.
4. **After-Sales Service** — warranty, service-centre availability, customer support.

> [!NOTE]
> Other valid criteria include battery life, display quality, weight, design aesthetics, and operating system.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Engel-Blackwell-Miniard (EBM) Model of Consumer Decision Making** in detail. Illustrate each stage with a real-world example from the smartphone industry.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A consumer is choosing between two washing-machine brands, **AquaClean** and **TurboWash**, using three attributes. The importance weights and belief scores (on a 1–10 scale) are:

| Attribute | Weight | AquaClean | TurboWash |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Energy Efficiency | 0.40 | 9 | 7 |
| Wash Quality | 0.35 | 7 | 9 |
| Price | 0.25 | 6 | 8 |

Using the **Multi-Attribute Attitude Model**, determine which brand the consumer will choose. State the rule applied and justify.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) EBM Model Explanation:**

The Engel-Blackwell-Miniard (EBM) Model, developed by James Engel, David Kollat, and Roger Blackwell, is one of the most comprehensive frameworks for understanding consumer decision-making. It has **five sequential stages**:

1. **Need Recognition:** The consumer perceives a gap between the actual state and desired state. *Example:* A user's current phone's battery lasts only 4 hours, prompting the recognition of a need for a phone with a 5000+ mAh battery.

2. **Information Search:** The consumer seeks information from internal (memory) and external (websites, reviews, friends) sources. *Example:* The user reads GSMArena reviews, watches YouTube comparisons, and asks peers about iPhone 15 vs. Samsung S24.

3. **Evaluation of Alternatives:** Each alternative is evaluated against a set of **Choice Criteria** (camera, battery, OS, price). The consumer forms an *evoked set* and applies decision rules. *Example:* The user compares iPhone 15 (A16 chip, iOS) with S24 (Snapdragon 8 Gen 3, Android).

4. **Purchase Decision:** The brand with the highest perceived value is selected. *Example:* The user purchases the iPhone 15 from Amazon during the Diwali sale due to perceived risk reduction through Amazon's return policy.

5. **Post-Purchase Behaviour:** The consumer evaluates satisfaction. If performance ≥ expectation → satisfaction → loyalty. If performance < expectation → **cognitive dissonance** → returns, complaints, or negative WOM. *Example:* The user posts a positive review on YouTube about the iPhone's camera but complains about battery life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Feature of EBM:** The model is **iterative**, not strictly linear. Feedback loops exist (e.g., dissatisfaction can re-trigger the need recognition stage for the next purchase).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the formula.**

$$
A_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot b_i
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $A_0$ for AquaClean.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0^{AquaClean} &= (0.40 \times 9) + (0.35 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 6) \\
&= 3.60 + 2.45 + 1.50 \\
&= 7.55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $A_0$ for TurboWash.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0^{TurboWash} &= (0.40 \times 7) + (0.35 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 8) \\
&= 2.80 + 3.15 + 2.00 \\
&= 7.95
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Compensatory Decision Rule.**

Since $A_0^{TurboWash} = 7.95 > A_0^{AquaClean} = 7.55$, the consumer will **choose TurboWash**.

**Justification:** Although AquaClean has a higher Energy Efficiency score (9), TurboWash compensates with superior Wash Quality (9 vs. 7) and Price attractiveness (8 vs. 6). The compensatory rule allows this trade-off.

---

**Question B (Alternative Choice).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **concept of the Order Management Cycle (OMC)**. Describe each stage of the cycle with reference to an e-commerce company such as Amazon or Flipkart.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A B2B industrial supplier processes **8,500 orders** in a month. Quality audit reveals the following defects:
- 95 delivered late
- 60 delivered incomplete
- 40 delivered damaged
- 25 with incorrect invoice

*Assume defects are mutually exclusive.* Calculate the **Perfect Order Rate (POR)**. Is the OMC performance above the **95% industry benchmark**? Suggest **two improvements** the supplier should implement.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Order Management Cycle — Detailed Explanation:**

The **Order Management Cycle (OMC)** is the end-to-end operational process that bridges the consumer's purchase decision with the firm's logistics and fulfillment capability. For an e-commerce company like Amazon, the cycle has **seven stages**:

1. **Order Capture:** The customer places the order on the app/website. Amazon captures SKU, quantity, address, payment mode in real time. *Single source of truth* is essential.

2. **Order Acknowledgement:** Amazon sends an instant confirmation (email + SMS + in-app notification) with the estimated delivery date. This reduces **perceived risk** and pre-purchase anxiety.

3. **Inventory Check & Allocation:** The nearest fulfillment centre performs an **Available-to-Promise (ATP)** check. If the SKU is unavailable, the system sources from a secondary warehouse or offers a substitute.

4. **Picking, Packing & Dispatch:** Warehouse robots and staff pick the item, pack it in branded packaging, and dispatch via Amazon Logistics or a 3PL partner. This stage is critical for **perceived quality**.

5. **Shipment & In-Transit Visibility:** Real-time tracking via GPS; the customer receives progressive updates. This is the **information-search** phase made tangible for the buyer.

6. **Last-Mile Delivery & POD:** The package is delivered to the customer's door. **Proof of Delivery (POD)** is captured via OTP/photo/signature. This is the moment of truth for **post-purchase satisfaction**.

7. **Post-Delivery Service & Reverse Logistics:** Handles returns, refunds, replacements. A 10-day return policy and no-questions-asked refunds increase **customer lifetime value (CLV)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategic Insight:** Amazon's OMC is world-class because of *vertical integration* (own logistics, own warehouses) and *data analytics* (predictive stocking at regional level).

**(b) POR Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Total Defective Orders.**

$$
D_{total} = 95 + 60 + 40 + 25 = 220
$$

**Step 2 — Number of Perfect Orders.**

$$
O_{perfect} = 8500 - 220 = 8280
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the POR Formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
POR &= \frac{O_{perfect}}{O_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{8280}{8500} \times 100 \\
&= 97.41\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Benchmark Comparison.**

Since $POR = 97.41\% > 95\%$, the supplier is **above the industry benchmark**. However, the *late-delivery* defect accounts for **95/220 ≈ 43.2%** of all defects, indicating a logistics bottleneck.

**Two Recommended Improvements:**

1. **Deploy a Transportation Management System (TMS) with predictive ETA analytics.** Use historical traffic and weather data to dynamically reroute shipments and reduce late deliveries.

2. **Implement an automated Order Verification System using barcode/RFID scanning** at the warehouse exit to eliminate incorrect-invoice and damaged-goods defects.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Formula Misapplication:** In multi-attribute problems, students often forget to *multiply weights by belief scores* and instead simply average the beliefs. This loses **3–4 marks**.
> 2. **OMC Stage Omission:** When describing the Order Management Cycle, students frequently skip **post-delivery service and reverse logistics**, citing only up to delivery. This is a major deduction under KTU 2024 marking — full 14-mark questions expect **all 7 stages** in part (a).
> 3. **Compensatory vs. Non-Compensatory Confusion:** Always *state explicitly* which decision rule you are using. If a question does not specify, the default is the **Compensatory (Weighted Sum) Rule**.
> 4. **No Mention of "Why":** Pure numerical computation without conceptual justification is penalized. Always **interpret** the result (e.g., "the firm is above benchmark, but late delivery is the biggest contributor to defects").

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Consumer Behaviour** = entire decision journey (need → search → evaluate → purchase → post-purchase). **Not** just the act of buying.
- **Four Pillars of Consumer Psychology:** Motivation, Perception, Learning, Attitude. These are the *internal variables* in the EBM model.
- **EBM Model** is **iterative**, not strictly linear. Feedback loops are essential.
- **Choice Criteria** are weighted attributes. Different consumers assign different weights.
- **Multi-Attribute Formula:** $A_0 = \sum w_i b_i$ — must be applied with **compensatory** rule unless specified otherwise.
- **Non-Compensatory Rules:** Conjunctive, Disjunctive, Lexicographic, Elimination-by-Aspects — all exist, each with distinct logic.
- **Cognitive Dissonance** occurs in the post-purchase stage; it is the *driver of complaints* and *negative word-of-mouth*.
- **Order Management Cycle has 7 stages:** Capture → Acknowledgement → Allocation → Picking/Packing → Shipment → Delivery → Post-Delivery/Returns.
- **Perfect Order Rate (POR) formula:** $POR = \frac{\text{Perfect Orders}}{\text{Total Orders}} \times 100$. Industry benchmark ≥ **95%**.
- **OTIF (On-Time-In-Full)** is the standard operational KPI.
- **Linkage Rule:** Consumer psychology (demand side) and OMC (supply side) are *interconnected* — psychological satisfaction is influenced by OMC performance.
- **Avoid these keywords misuse:** "Decision-making" ≠ "Purchase decision" (it is the *entire 5-stage* process).
- **KTU Favourite Topics for 14-mark questions:** Numerical on Multi-Attribute Model, EBM model description, OMC stages in a real-world e-commerce case.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buying Situation - Personal and Social Influence: Product Decision - Concept of a Product
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET638 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Buying Situation - Personal and Social Influence: Product Decision - Concept of a Product

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Buying Situations — The Foundational Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A *Buying Situation* (also called the **Buyclass Framework** or **Buygrid Framework**) describes the degree of effort and complexity a buyer invests when making a purchasing decision. It was formalized by **Robinson, Faris, and Wind** in their 1967 work *Industrial Buying and Creative Marketing*.

The three canonical buying situations are:

| Buying Situation | Decision Effort | Information Search | Alternative Evaluation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Straight Rebuy** | Routine, automatic | None | None — habitual purchase |
| **Modified Rebuy** | Moderate | Limited | Some comparison among existing suppliers |
| **New Task** | High, complex | Extensive | Full-blown evaluation of all alternatives |

### 🔍 Intuitive Analogy
Think of buying situations like the way you shop on a college campus:

- **Straight Rebuy** → You grab your usual masala chai from the same canteen stall every morning without even looking at the menu. Your brain is on autopilot.
- **Modified Rebuy** → Your usual chai stall changes its supplier, so you now compare two nearby stalls before deciding.
- **New Task** → You are buying a new laptop for the first time. You research specs, read reviews, ask seniors, compare prices, and agonize over the choice for days.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters for marketers:** The *buying situation* determines the *selling situation*. A new task purchase requires a consultative, high-involvement sales approach, while a straight rebuy is handled by automated reorder systems.

---

## 1.2 Personal and Social Influences on Buyer Behavior

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:**
> Buyer behavior is shaped by a **three-layer cultural model** (Cultural → Social → Personal → Psychological), but this module focuses on the **Personal** and **Social** layers that directly affect how individuals make purchase decisions.

### Personal Influences
These are characteristics unique to the individual buyer:

1. **Age and Stage in the Life Cycle** — A 20-year-old engineering student buys differently from a 45-year-old IT professional.
2. **Occupation** — A civil engineer buys safety boots; a software developer buys ergonomic chairs.
3. **Economic Situation** — Disposable income, savings, and debt capacity.
4. **Lifestyle** — A pattern of living expressed through activities, interests, and opinions (AIOs).
5. **Personality and Self-Concept** — A confident buyer may prefer premium brands; a cautious buyer prefers value-for-money.

### Social Influences
These originate from the buyer's environment:

1. **Reference Groups** — Groups that influence attitudes and behaviors (aspirational, membership, dissociative).
2. **Family** — The most influential primary reference group; family decision-making roles exist (initiator, influencer, decider, buyer, user).
3. **Roles and Status** — A person occupies multiple positions in groups (e.g., a student is also a son, a class representative, a team captain), and each role influences purchase.

### 🔍 Intuitive Analogy
Imagine choosing a new pair of running shoes (a personal decision) — but what you actually buy is shaped by:
- **Personal:** Your foot type, your budget, your fitness goals.
- **Social:** What your peer group wears, whether your family thinks the brand is worth it, and how you want to be perceived (role/status).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked about influences on buyer behavior, structure your answer as **Cultural → Social → Personal → Psychological**, but expand deeply on the layers asked. This shows examiner-relevant breadth.

---

## 1.3 Product Decision — Concept of a Product

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Kotler):**
> A *product* is **anything that can be offered to a market for attention, acquisition, use, or consumption that might satisfy a want or need.** It includes physical objects, services, persons, places, organizations, and ideas.

### The Three Levels of a Product (Kotler's Core Model)

$$
\text{Product} = \text{Core Benefit} + \text{Actual Product} + \text{Augmented Product}
$$

1. **Core Benefit / Core Product** — The fundamental need being satisfied. (e.g., A car's core benefit is *transportation*, a lipstick's is *beauty enhancement*.)
2. **Actual Product** — The physical features, design, brand name, quality, and packaging that deliver the core benefit.
3. **Augmented Product** — Additional services and benefits that exceed customer expectations (warranty, delivery, installation, after-sales service).

### 🔍 Intuitive Analogy
A **smartphone**:
- **Core:** Communication + connectivity.
- **Actual:** The physical phone with its screen, camera, brand (Apple/Samsung), color, and storage.
- **Augmented:** Free cloud storage, one-year warranty, free screen replacement, 24/7 customer support.

### Product Classification (KTU High-Yield)

| Basis | Types | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Durability & Tangibility** | Non-durable, Durable, Services | Soap, Fridge, Haircut |
| **Consumer vs Industrial** | Convenience, Shopping, Specialty, Unsought (Consumer); Materials, Capital, Supplies, Services (Industrial) | Salt, Laptop, Ferrari, Insurance, Steel, Machine |
| **Market Position** | Leader, Challenger, Follower, Nicher | Apple, Samsung, Vivo, Dyson |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners love Kotler's three-level model.** Memorize the smartphone/camera/pen analogy — it guarantees easy 3 marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Buygrid Framework — Full Analytical Breakdown

The **Buygrid Model** is an 8-cell matrix combining **buying situations** (rows) with **buying process stages** (columns):

| Buying Situation | Recognition of Need | Determination of Characteristics | Search for Suppliers | Request for Proposals | Supplier Selection | Routine Order | Performance Review |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Straight Rebuy** | Auto | Auto | Reorder only | None | None | Auto | Periodic |
| **Modified Rebuy** | Easy | Moderate | Limited | Limited | Some | Updated | Frequent |
| **New Task** | Hard | Complex | Extensive | Full RFP | Detailed | One-time | Intense |

### Why Each Stage Matters:
- **Recognition of Need:** Triggered by internal (machine breakdown) or external (salesperson visit) stimuli.
- **Determination of Characteristics:** Buyer specifies *what* they want (specifications).
- **Search for Suppliers:** Identifying qualified vendors.
- **RFP (Request for Proposal):** Invitation for vendors to submit bids.
- **Supplier Selection:** Multiple criteria applied — price, quality, delivery, reputation.
- **Routine Order:** The actual purchase order.
- **Performance Review:** Post-purchase evaluation, which feeds back into the next buyclass.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Memory Trick:** The 7 stages can be remembered as **"R-D-S-R-S-R-P"** → **"Ram Does Some Reading, Studies, Repeats Patterns."**

---

## 2.2 Personal & Social Influence — Decision Roles in Family Buying

In a family purchase decision, **five roles** are typically observed (Kotler):

| Role | Definition | Example (Family buying a car) |
|---|---|---|
| **Initiator** | First suggests the idea | The teenage daughter |
| **Influencer** | Shapes opinion | A friend who owns a similar car |
| **Decider** | Final authority on the decision | The father |
| **Buyer** | Makes the actual transaction | The mother who visits the showroom |
| **User** | Consumes/uses the product | All family members |

### Personal Influence — Lifestyle Analysis (AIO Framework)

Lifestyle is measured through **Activities (A), Interests (I), and Opinions (O)**:

- **Activities:** Work, hobbies, shopping, sports, social events.
- **Interests:** Family, food, fashion, recreation, community.
- **Opinions:** Self-image, politics, business, education, future.

> [!NOTE]
> This AIO framework is a popular KTU short-answer topic. Use it whenever asked about measuring lifestyle.

---

## 2.3 Concept of a Product — Kotler's Five Product Levels (Extended)

While the three-level model is most commonly tested, the **five-level model** (extended by Kotler and Keller) gives a more detailed view:

| Level | Description | Example (Hotel Stay) |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Core Benefit | Fundamental need | Rest & sleep |
| 2. Basic Product | Basic version of the product | A standard room with bed & bathroom |
| 3. Expected Product | Attributes buyers normally expect | Clean sheets, towels, working AC |
| 4. Augmented Product | Exceeds expectations | Complimentary breakfast, free WiFi, spa access |
| 5. Potential Product | Future enhancements | Smart-room AI, personalized climate control |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Term | Definition | KTU Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|
| Buyclass | Buying Situation | Degree of effort in a purchase | Straight / Modified / New Task |
| Buygrid | Robinson-Faris-Wind | 3×7 matrix of buying situations vs stages | 8-cell matrix |
| Reference Group | Social Influence | Group that affects buyer attitudes | Aspirational, Membership, Dissociative |
| Family Roles | Social Influence | 5 decision-making roles | Initiator → User |
| AIO | Personal Influence | Activities, Interests, Opinions | Lifestyle measurement |
| Product (Kotler) | Marketing | Anything offered to satisfy a need/ want | Tangible + Intangible |
| 3 Product Levels | Kotler | Core + Actual + Augmented | Value hierarchy |
| 5 Product Levels | Kotler-Keller | Core + Basic + Expected + Augmented + Potential | Extended model |
| Product Mix | Portfolio | All products a company offers | Width, Length, Depth, Consistency |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing **Reference Group** with **Social Class**. A reference group is a *group you interact with*; a social class is a *stratified societal layer*. Both are social influences but conceptually distinct.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework

## 3.1 Identifying the Buying Situation — A Logical Decision Tree

Since there are no mathematical derivations in this management topic, this section presents **analytical logic flows** that are the management equivalent of step-by-step derivations.

### Step-by-Step Logic: Classifying a Buyer's Decision Type

**Step 1 — Identify if the product is already being purchased.**
- **YES** → Go to Step 2.
- **NO** → It is a **New Task** situation. → *Stop.*

**Step 2 — Check if the specifications, supplier, or quantity have changed.**
- **YES (changed)** → It is a **Modified Rebuy** situation. → *Stop.*
- **NO (unchanged)** → It is a **Straight Rebuy** situation. → *Stop.*

$$
\text{Buying Situation} = \begin{cases} \text{New Task} & \text{if product is newly required} \\ \text{Modified Rebuy} & \text{if existing product, but specs/supplier changed} \\ \text{Straight Rebuy} & \text{if product re-ordered identically} \end{cases}
$$

### Worked Example:
> A KTU engineering hostel kitchen has been buying 50 kg of rice from the same local supplier every month for the past 2 years. This month, the price increased, and the manager is considering two alternative suppliers.
> - Step 1: Product is being purchased. ✓
> - Step 2: Specifications (50 kg, rice) unchanged, but **supplier** is being reconsidered.
> - **Result: Modified Rebuy.**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Personal & Social Influences to a Purchase

Using the **smartphone purchase** case as our controlled variable:

| Influence Layer | Specific Factor | How It Affects the Decision |
|---|---|---|
| **Personal — Age** | Buyer is 20 years old | Prefers latest model, color, camera quality |
| **Personal — Economic** | Limited pocket money | Price-sensitive, EMI options matter |
| **Personal — Lifestyle** | Tech-savvy gamer | Prioritizes processor and refresh rate |
| **Social — Reference Group** | College friend group | Peer pressure on brand (Apple vs Android) |
| **Social — Family** | Parents funding the purchase | Trust in brand reputation, durability |
| **Social — Roles/Status** | Wants to be seen as "cool" | Premium brand carries social currency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Chain for KTU Answer Writing:**
> 1. Start with **Cultural** influences (broadest).
> 2. Move to **Social** (reference groups, family, roles).
> 3. Drill down to **Personal** (age, lifestyle, personality).
> 4. End with **Psychological** (motivation, perception, learning — for higher marks).

---

## 3.3 Product Decision Logic — Selecting the Right Product Level

Marketers use the following decision logic to position their product:

**Step 1 — Identify the Core Benefit** (the *why* behind the purchase).
- A watch's core benefit is timekeeping; a luxury watch's core benefit is *status signaling*.

**Step 2 — Design the Actual Product** to deliver this core benefit.
- Physical features, brand name, packaging, quality level.

**Step 3 — Build the Augmented Product** to differentiate from competitors.
- Warranty, after-sales service, customization, loyalty programs.

**Step 4 — Evaluate if the augmented features are economically viable.**
- If augmented features cost more than the customer is willing to pay, they may be removed.

**Step 5 — Position the product in the customer's mind relative to competitors.**

$$
\text{Product Value} = \text{Core Benefit} + \text{Actual Features} + \text{Augmented Services} - \text{Total Cost}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** When asked "What is a product?", use the **Kotler definition** first, then immediately follow with the **three levels** to show examiner-relevant depth. This automatically scores 5+ marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Buygrid Framework — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Buyer Faced With a Purchase Need] --> B{Is the Product New?}
    B -- YES --> C[New Task Situation]
    B -- NO --> D{Are Specs or Supplier Changing?}
    D -- YES --> E[Modified Rebuy]
    D -- NO --> F[Straight Rebuy]
    C --> G[Extensive Research, Full RFP, Detailed Evaluation]
    E --> H[Limited Search, Updated Specs, Few New Suppliers]
    F --> I[Automatic Reorder, No New Evaluation Needed]
    G --> J[High Involvement, Long Decision Time]
    H --> K[Moderate Involvement]
    I --> L[Low Involvement, Routine Process]
```

## 4.2 Personal and Social Influences on Buyer Behavior

```mermaid
graph TD
    Buyer[Individual Buyer Decision]
    Buyer --> Personal[Personal Influences]
    Buyer --> Social[Social Influences]
    Personal --> P1[Age and Life Cycle Stage]
    Personal --> P2[Occupation and Economic Situation]
    Personal --> P3[Lifestyle AIO]
    Personal --> P4[Personality and Self Concept]
    Social --> S1[Reference Groups]
    Social --> S2[Family]
    Social --> S3[Roles and Status]
    S1 --> S1a[Aspirational Groups]
    S1 --> S1b[Membership Groups]
    S1 --> S1c[Dissociative Groups]
    S2 --> S2a[Initiator]
    S2 --> S2b[Influencer]
    S2 --> S2c[Decider]
    S2 --> S2d[Buyer]
    S2 --> S2e[User]
```

## 4.3 Kotler's Three Levels of a Product

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Complete Product Offering] --> B[Augmented Product Layer]
    B --> C[Actual Product Layer]
    C --> D[Core Benefit Layer]
    D --> E[Fundamental Customer Need Satisfied]
    C --> F[Features, Design, Brand, Quality, Packaging]
    B --> G[Warranty, Delivery, Installation, After Sales Service]
    E --> H[Example Smartphone Need: Connectivity]
    F --> I[Example Smartphone: Screen Size, Brand, Color]
    G --> J[Example Smartphone: Cloud Storage, Warranty, Support]
```

## 4.4 Product Mix Dimensions (Width, Length, Depth, Consistency)

```mermaid
graph LR
    PM[Product Mix of a Company] --> W[Width: Number of Product Lines]
    PM --> L[Length: Total Number of Items]
    PM --> D[Depth: Variants per Product]
    PM --> C[Consistency: Relationship Between Lines]
    W --> W1[Example Nestle: 5 Lines - Dairy, Beverages, Chocolates, Water, Cereals]
    L --> L1[Example Nestle: 200 Plus Total SKUs]
    D --> D1[Example Maggi Noodles: 4 Sizes, 8 Flavors]
    C --> C1[Example Tata Group: High Consistency Across Steel, Salt, Tea]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Drawing Tip:** If you are asked to draw the 3 product levels in your exam, a simple **3-tier pyramid or staircase diagram** with the smartphone example as labels will fetch full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📚 Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the three types of buying situations and state one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

A buying situation is the degree of decision-making effort required for a purchase, as per the **Buyclass Framework** (Robinson, Faris & Wind).

1. **Straight Rebuy:** A routine, habitual re-purchase with no change in supplier or specifications. *Example: A company reordering the same brand of A4 printing paper every month.*
2. **Modified Rebuy:** A re-purchase where the buyer wants to modify product specifications, prices, or suppliers. *Example: A restaurant switching from one soft drink supplier to another for cost savings.*
3. **New Task:** A first-time purchase requiring extensive research and decision-making. *Example: A startup buying its first fleet of office laptops.*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Explain Kotler's concept of a product with reference to its three levels.**

**Model Answer:**

According to **Philip Kotler**, a product is anything offered to a market to satisfy a need or want. A product has three levels:

1. **Core Benefit:** The fundamental need that the product satisfies. *Example: A college education's core benefit is knowledge and employability.*
2. **Actual Product:** The tangible features, design, brand name, and quality that deliver the core benefit. *Example: A B.Tech degree certificate, syllabus, and faculty quality.*
3. **Augmented Product:** Additional services like placement support, library access, and online learning portals that exceed expectations.

The augmented level is where modern marketers create competitive differentiation.

---

## 📘 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In **buying situation** questions, students often forget to name the **7 stages of the buying process** alongside the 3 buyclass types, costing them 3–4 marks.
> - In **product concept** questions, students define the product but **forget to draw the three-level diagram** — this costs 2 marks.
> - For **personal/social influence** questions, students list factors but **fail to give a unifying example**. Always tie your answer to a single real-world case.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) Explain the Buygrid Framework in detail. Differentiate between the three types of buying situations with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Buygrid Model** (Robinson, Faris & Wind, 1967) is an 8-cell matrix that combines **buying situations** (buyclasses) with the **buying decision process**. It helps marketers design selling strategies that match buyer behavior.

**Three Buying Situations:**

1. **Straight Rebuy:** Routine reorder, no evaluation, low risk. Marketers focus on **maintaining supply reliability**. *Example: A hospital reordering surgical gloves from the same vendor.*

2. **Modified Rebuy:** Some change triggers limited re-evaluation. Marketers compete to be the **new alternative**. *Example: An engineering firm switching to a more energy-efficient AC unit for its server room.*

3. **New Task:** High-involvement purchase requiring full evaluation. Marketers provide **consultative selling** and detailed information. *Example: A manufacturing company buying a new CNC machine for the first time.*

**Marketing Implication:** Strategy intensity scales with decision complexity — straight rebuy needs *logistics*, modified rebuy needs *competitive pricing*, new task needs *technical consultation*.

---

**(b) Discuss the personal and social factors that influence consumer buying behavior. Illustrate with the example of an engineering student purchasing a new laptop. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Personal Influences:**

- **Age & Life Cycle Stage:** A 21-year-old engineering student prioritizes portability, performance, and social appeal.
- **Economic Situation:** Limited disposable income → EMI options, student discounts matter.
- **Lifestyle:** Tech-oriented, gaming, coding → high processor, RAM, and battery requirements.
- **Personality/Self-Concept:** A confident, image-conscious student may prefer a premium brand; a practical one prefers value.

**Social Influences:**

- **Reference Groups:** College friends recommend brands; online tech communities (Reddit, YouTube) influence opinions.
- **Family:** Parents often fund the purchase, so they prefer reputed brands with good after-sales service.
- **Roles & Status:** A class representative may want a laptop that signals leadership and sophistication.

**Conclusion:** The laptop purchase is shaped not just by individual need, but by an interplay of personal characteristics and the social ecosystem the student inhabits.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) "A product is more than a physical good." Discuss Kotler's concept of a product and explain its three levels with the example of a camera. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Kotler's Definition:** A product is *anything that can be offered to a market for attention, acquisition, use, or consumption to satisfy a want or need.* It includes physical goods, services, ideas, places, and organizations.

**Three Levels of a Product (Camera Example):**

| Level | Description | Camera Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Core Benefit** | Fundamental need satisfied | Capturing and preserving memories |
| **Actual Product** | Physical attributes, brand, design | Canon EOS R6, 20MP sensor, ergonomic grip, black matte body |
| **Augmented Product** | Extra services | Free lens cleaning kit, 2-year warranty, cloud photo storage, online photography workshop access |

**Conclusion:** Marketers who focus only on physical features miss the core benefit and fail to create differentiation. The augmented level is increasingly the battleground for customer loyalty.

---

**(b) Explain the concept of Product Mix. Describe its four dimensions with the example of a company of your choice. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A **Product Mix** (or product assortment) is the set of all products and items that a particular seller offers for sale to buyers.

**Four Dimensions of Product Mix (Example: Hindustan Unilever Limited — HUL):**

1. **Width:** Number of distinct product lines.
   - HUL has lines in **personal care, home care, food & beverages, water purifiers, and ice cream** (≈ 5 lines).

2. **Length:** Total number of items in the mix.
   - HUL has **over 50 product lines** with **400+ SKUs** total.

3. **Depth:** Number of variants within each product.
   - *Dove shampoo* alone has 5 variants: dry hair, oily hair, anti-dandruff, color protect, and damage repair.

4. **Consistency:** How closely related the product lines are in terms of usage, distribution, or production.
   - HUL has **high consistency** — most products are fast-moving consumer goods (FMCG) sold through the same retail channels.

**Conclusion:** A well-designed product mix enables cross-selling, brand portfolio synergies, and risk diversification.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ⭐ **Three buying situations** — Straight Rebuy, Modified Rebuy, New Task. Memorize the **8-cell Buygrid matrix**.
- ⭐ **Personal Influences** — Age, Life Cycle, Occupation, Economic Situation, Lifestyle, Personality, Self-Concept. Use the **AIO** framework for lifestyle.
- ⭐ **Social Influences** — Reference Groups (3 types), Family (5 decision roles: Initiator → User), Roles & Status.
- ⭐ **Product Definition (Kotler)** — "Anything offered to satisfy a need/want."
- ⭐ **Three Product Levels** — Core Benefit, Actual Product, Augmented Product. Always illustrate with an example.
- ⭐ **Five Product Levels (Extended)** — Core + Basic + Expected + Augmented + Potential.
- ⭐ **Product Mix Dimensions** — Width, Length, Depth, Consistency. Use Hindustan Unilever or Nestle as your example.
- ⭐ **Product Classification** — Durability (Non-durable, Durable, Services) and Use (Convenience, Shopping, Specialty, Unsought).
- ⭐ **Family Decision Roles** — Initiator, Influencer, Decider, Buyer, User.
- ⭐ **Golden Rule:** Every conceptual answer should be **defined → classified → illustrated → applied**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Last-Minute Exam Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. Do not confuse **Reference Group** (specific group interaction) with **Social Class** (broad societal stratum).
> 2. Do not write "Product = Tangible goods only." Kotler's definition explicitly includes services and ideas.
> 3. In product mix questions, **width ≠ length.** Width = number of lines; Length = total items.
> 4. Always draw a **labeled diagram** in 14-mark answers — examiners explicitly award 2 marks for visual representation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Products - Business, Consumer, Service Product, Commodity, Technology and Customised Product; New Product Development - Product Idea - Product Life Cycle; Brand - Brand Attributes - Building a Brand Name - Strategies of Corporate Branding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET638 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Products - Business, Consumer, Service Product, Commodity, Technology and Customised Product; New Product Development - Product Idea - Product Life Cycle; Brand - Brand Attributes - Building a Brand Name - Strategies of Corporate Branding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Products – The Foundational Marketing Construct

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Philip Kotler Framework):**
> A **Product** is anything that can be offered to a market for attention, acquisition, use, or consumption that might satisfy a want or need. It includes physical objects, services, persons, places, organizations, and ideas.

A product is the **central element** of the marketing mix (the "P" in the 4Ps / 7Ps framework). It is the tangible or intangible offering through which a firm delivers **value** to its chosen customer segment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy – The "Restaurant Meal" Intuition:**
> Imagine a restaurant. The dish you order is the **core product** (the benefit: hunger satisfaction). The recipe, ingredients, presentation, and plating form the **actual product**. The ambience, service, and cleanliness constitute the **augmented product**. Together, they represent the *total product offering*. Marketing managers must design and manage all three layers.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Products – The Six Major Types

Products in the KTU 2024 Marketing Management syllabus are classified into **six distinct categories** based on the buyer, usage pattern, tangibility, and degree of customization.

### 1.2.1 Consumer Products (B2C)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Products bought by **end-consumers** for personal, family, or household use — *not* for business operations or resale.

**Sub-classification (As per KTU Module 2):**

| Type | Buying Behavior | Example |
|------|----------------|---------|
| **Convenience Goods** | Frequent, low-effort, habitual | Salt, bread, newspapers |
| **Shopping Goods** | Comparison-based, planned | Mobile phones, clothing |
| **Specialty Goods** | High brand loyalty, search-intensive | Rolex watches, BMW cars |
| **Unsought Goods** | Purchased with low awareness | Life insurance, fire extinguishers |

### 1.2.2 Business Products (B2B / Industrial Products)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Products purchased by **organizations, industries, or institutions** for further processing, operating a business, or reselling to other customers.

**Categories:** Raw Materials, Major Equipment, Accessory Equipment, Component Parts, Process Materials, Maintenance & Repair Supplies (MRO), and Business Services.

**Real-World Example:** Tata Steel buying iron ore (raw material); a hospital procuring MRI machines (major equipment).

### 1.2.3 Service Products

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** **Intangible, inseparable, variable, and perishable** offerings that provide value without transfer of physical ownership.

The **4Vs Framework of Services:**
- **V**ariability (heterogeneity in quality)
- **V**ariability in demand
- **V**ariability in supply
- **V**ariability in delivery

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Unlike a soap bar you can physically hold, a haircut service cannot be touched, stored, or returned. The service is *produced and consumed simultaneously*. Examples: Banking, Education, Healthcare, Aviation, Consultancy.

### 1.2.4 Commodity Products

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** **Standardized, undifferentiated, fungible** products whose market price is determined by global supply-demand forces rather than brand identity.

Examples: Crude oil, gold, wheat, rice, copper, base metals. Commodities are traded on exchanges (NYMEX, MCX) where **brand differentiation is virtually impossible**.

### 1.2.5 Technology Products

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Products characterized by **rapid innovation cycles, short life spans, network effects, and high R&D intensity**.

Examples: Smartphones (Apple iPhone), Operating Systems (Windows, Android), Cloud platforms (AWS, Azure). The technology sector is governed by Moore's Law and disruptive innovation dynamics.

### 1.2.6 Customised Products

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Products **specifically designed, engineered, or tailored** to meet the unique requirements of a single buyer or niche segment.

Examples: Bespoke tailoring (Savile Row), Custom-built yachts, Enterprise ERP solutions, Made-to-order industrial machinery.

---

## 1.3 New Product Development (NPD) – Product Idea

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Module 2):**
> **New Product Development (NPD)** is the structured, multi-stage process by which organizations conceive, design, test, and commercially launch products that are **new to the firm, new to the market, or new to the world**.

A **Product Idea** is a *conceptualization* of a possible product that the company might offer — it answers the question: *"What could we sell that would solve our customer's problem?"* Idea generation precedes feasibility analysis, design, and launch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The "Idea-to-Market Pipeline":**
> Think of NPD as a **funnel**: 1,000 raw ideas → 100 concepts → 25 prototypes → 5 launched products → 2 commercial successes. The conversion ratios reflect real industry data — most ideas *never* reach the market.

---

## 1.4 Product Life Cycle (PLC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Raymond Vernon, 1966):**
> The **Product Life Cycle (PLC)** is the **S-shaped bell-curve model** describing the sales and profit trajectory of a product across **four sequential stages**: Introduction, Growth, Maturity, and Decline.

It is a **biological analogy** — products, like living organisms, are born, grow, mature, and eventually die. However, unlike biological organisms, products can be **re-launched** through repositioning, reformulation, or reinvention.

---

## 1.5 Brand – The Identity Construct

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 Definition (American Marketing Association):**
> A **Brand** is a *name, term, design, symbol, or any other feature* that identifies one seller's good or service as distinct from those of other sellers. It is essentially a **seller's promise to deliver a specific set of features, benefits, and experiences** consistently to buyers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The "Personality" Metaphor:**
> A brand is to a company what a **personality** is to a human. Just as a person has a name, face, voice, reputation, and character — a brand has a name, logo, tagline, equity, and positioning. Customers don't buy products; they buy **brands they trust**.

---

## 1.6 Module-Wide Conceptual Map

The three core pillars of Module 2 are:
1. **Product Typology** → What is being sold?
2. **PLC & NPD** → How is it created and how does it evolve?
3. **Brand Management** → How is it identified and valued?

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive – Types of Products (Comparative Framework)

Each product type requires a **distinct marketing strategy**, distribution model, pricing approach, and promotional mix. The table below summarizes the strategic differentials:

### 2.1.1 Strategic Comparison Matrix

| Product Type | Buyer | Tangibility | Price Sensitivity | Distribution | Promotion Focus |
|--------------|-------|-------------|-------------------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Consumer** | End-user | Tangible/Intangible | High (varies) | Intensive | Mass advertising |
| **Business** | Firm/Institution | Mostly Tangible | Value-driven | Direct sales | Personal selling |
