| **Service** | End-user | Intangible | Quality-driven | Selective | Trust-building |
| **Commodity** | Trader/Industry | Tangible | Price-driven | Spot/Futures | Minimal |
| **Technology** | Tech-savvy | Tangible+Digital | Feature-driven | Omnichannel | Innovation-led |
| **Customised** | Single buyer | Both | Negotiation-driven | Direct | Relationship-based |

---

## 2.2 New Product Development – The 8-Stage Kotler Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Framework (Philip Kotler, 8-Stage NPD Process):**

1. **Idea Generation** – Brainstorming, customer input, R&D, competitor analysis
2. **Idea Screening** – Eliminate unfit ideas (drop ratio: ~70%)
3. **Concept Development & Testing** – Convert ideas into customer-facing concepts
4. **Marketing Strategy Development** – Define target, value proposition, sales, profit
5. **Business Analysis** – Revenue forecasts, cost projections, break-even
6. **Product Development** – R&D builds prototype
7. **Test Marketing** – Pilot launch in limited geography
8. **Commercialization** – Full market launch

### 2.2.1 KTU Formula Sheet – NPD Strategic Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Application |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| **Drop-off Ratio** | $\text{Drop} = \dfrac{\text{Ideas Screened Out}}{\text{Total Ideas Generated}} \times 100$ | Measures funnel efficiency |
| **Success Rate** | $\text{Success} = \dfrac{\text{Successful Launches}}{\text{Total Launches}} \times 100$ | Industry benchmark: 10–15% |
| **Break-Even Units** | $\text{BEU} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Costs}}{\text{Price} - \text{Variable Cost per Unit}}$ | Go/No-Go decision |
| **NPV of Launch** | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$ | Capital budgeting for NPD |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Companies like **3M** mandate that 30% of revenue must come from products launched within the last 5 years — this is the *Vitality Index* — a direct application of NPD pipeline discipline.

---

## 2.3 Product Life Cycle (PLC) – Stage-Wise Deep Analysis

### 2.3.1 Stage Characteristics Matrix

| PLC Stage | Sales | Profits | Customers | Competitors | Strategy |
|-----------|-------|---------|-----------|-------------|----------|
| **Introduction** | Low | Negative | Innovators (2.5%) | Few | Awareness-building |
| **Growth** | Rapid ↑ | Rising | Early Majority | Increasing | Differentiation |
| **Maturity** | Peak | Maximum | Late Majority | Many | Defending share |
| **Decline** | Falling ↓ | Eroding | Laggards | Exit begins | Harvest / Divest |

### 2.3.2 KTU Formula Sheet – PLC Strategic Calculations

| Metric | Formula | Use |
|--------|---------|-----|
| **Market Growth Rate** | $\text{MGR} = \dfrac{S_t - S_{t-1}}{S_{t-1}} \times 100$ | Identifies PLC stage |
| **Relative Market Share** | $\text{RMS} = \dfrac{\text{Our Sales}}{\text{Largest Competitor Sales}}$ | BCG Matrix input |
| **Stage Duration Index** | $\text{SDI} = \dfrac{\text{Years in Current Stage}}{\text{Industry Average Stage Length}}$ | Maturity detection |
| **Harvesting Index** | $\text{HI} = \dfrac{\text{Cash Flow}}{\text{Marketing Spend}}$ | Decline-phase decision |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The PLC is the foundation of the **BCG Growth-Share Matrix**. A product in *Growth* phase with high RMS is a **Star**; in *Maturity* with high RMS is a **Cash Cow**; in *Growth* with low RMS is a **Question Mark**; in *Decline* with low RMS is a **Dog**.

---

## 2.4 Brand Attributes – The Identity DNA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Brand attributes are the **functional, emotional, and self-expressive characteristics** that consumers associate with a brand.

### 2.4.1 The Three Pillars of Brand Attributes

1. **Functional Attributes** → Performance, features, reliability, durability
   - Example: Volvo = *Safety*; Toyota = *Reliability*
2. **Emotional Attributes** → Feelings evoked, trust, nostalgia, excitement
   - Example: Coca-Cola = *Happiness*; Apple = *Creativity*
3. **Self-Expressive Attributes** → What using the brand *says* about the customer
   - Example: Nike = *Achievement*; BMW = *Status*

---

## 2.5 Building a Brand Name – The Strategic Framework

### 2.5.1 Brand Name Selection Criteria (KTU Module 2)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7 C's of Effective Brand Naming:**
> 1. **C**larity – Easy to pronounce and spell
> 2. **C**atchy – Memorable and distinctive
> 3. **C**ongruity – Aligned with product category
> 4. **C**redibility – Conveys quality and trust
> 5. **C**ompetitiveness – Distinct from competitors
> 6. **C**ompatibility – Works across cultures and markets
> 7. **C**onvertibility – Adaptable to product line extensions

### 2.5.2 KTU Formula Sheet – Brand Equity Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Purpose |
|--------|---------|---------|
| **Brand Awareness** | $\text{BA} = \dfrac{\text{Customers Recognizing Brand}}{\text{Total Sampled Customers}} \times 100$ | Top-of-mind recall |
| **Brand Loyalty Index** | $\text{BLI} = \dfrac{\text{Repeat Buyers}}{\text{Total Buyers}} \times 100$ | Retention measure |
| **Brand Equity (Aaker)** | $\text{BE} = \text{AL} + \text{BL} + \text{PQ} + \text{BA} + \text{PA}$ | Composite equity |
| **Net Promoter Score** | $\text{NPS} = \text{Percent Promoters} - \text{Percent Detractors}$ | Advocacy indicator |

Where:
- **AL** = Awareness; **BL** = Loyalty; **PQ** = Perceived Quality; **BA** = Brand Associations; **PA** = Proprietary Assets

---

## 2.6 Strategies of Corporate Branding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Module 2):**
> **Corporate Branding** is the strategy of positioning the **entire organization** — rather than individual products — as the brand. It uses one master brand identity across all product lines.

### 2.6.1 Five Major Corporate Branding Strategies

1. **Unified / Monolithic Branding** – One brand for all products
   - Example: **Google** (Search, Maps, Drive, Gmail all under one name)
2. **Endorsed Branding** – Sub-brands linked to corporate brand
   - Example: **Marriott** (Courtyard by Marriott, Ritz-Carlton)
3. **Branded House** – Master brand dominates, variants follow
   - Example: **Apple** (iPhone, iPad, MacBook)
4. **House of Brands** – Multiple independent brands
   - Example: **P&G** (Tide, Gillette, Pampers)
5. **Hybrid Branding** – Combination of corporate + product branding
   - Example: **Samsung Galaxy**, **Toyota Fortuner**

### 2.6.2 KTU Formula Sheet – Corporate Brand Valuation

| Method | Formula / Approach | Used By |
|--------|-------------------|---------|
| **Interbrand Method** | $\text{BV} = \sum \left(\text{FCF}_t \times \text{BR} \times \text{WACC}_t\right)$ | Interbrand rankings |
| **BrandZ Method** | $\text{BV} = \text{Earnings} \times \text{BR Multiplier}$ | Millward Brown |
| **Brand Royalty Relief** | $\text{BV} = \sum \text{Royalty Saved}$ | Financial reporting |

Where **BR** = Brand Strength (role, strength, support scores), **WACC** = Weighted Avg Cost of Capital.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In 2024, **Apple** was valued at ~$480B, **Microsoft** at ~$340B, and **Google** at ~$330B on the Interbrand Top 100 list. Corporate brand value directly impacts stock valuation, M&A multiples, and customer acquisition cost (CAC).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix – Product Type Identification Logic

A **decision-tree approach** for KTU students to identify which product type is being discussed in exam scenarios:

```
Step 1: Is the buyer an individual or a firm?
   ├── Individual → Go to Step 2
   └── Firm → Business Product (B2B)

Step 2: Is the offering physical or intangible?
   ├── Tangible → Go to Step 3
   └── Intangible → Service Product

Step 3: Is the product standardized or unique-to-buyer?
   ├── Standardized, fungible, exchange-traded → Commodity
   ├── Tailored to specific buyer → Customised
   └── Mass-produced with brand differentiation → Go to Step 4

Step 4: Does it have rapid innovation & tech-driven features?
   ├── Yes → Technology Product
   └── No → Consumer Product (B2C)
```

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation – Break-Even Analysis for NPD Launch

This is a **quantitative KTU-style worked example** showing how a firm decides whether to launch a new product.

### Problem Statement
A firm plans to launch a new product. Fixed development costs = ₹20,00,000. Variable cost per unit = ₹500. Expected selling price = ₹1,000. What is the minimum units needed to break even?

### Solution

$$\text{Break-Even Units (BEU)} = \frac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Price per Unit} - \text{Variable Cost per Unit}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\text{BEU} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{1000 - 500}$$

$$\text{BEU} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{500}$$

$$\text{BEU} = 4{,}000 \text{ units}$$

### Interpretation
The firm must sell **at least 4,000 units** before it starts generating profit. Any unit sold beyond 4,000 contributes ₹500 (the contribution margin) toward net profit.

### Decision Criterion (KTU Valuation Pattern)

$$\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Launch}, & \text{if Projected Sales} > \text{BEU} \times 1.3 \\ \text{Re-evaluate}, & \text{if } 0.9 \times \text{BEU} \leq \text{Projected Sales} \leq 1.3 \times \text{BEU} \\ \text{Reject}, & \text{if Projected Sales} < 0.9 \times \text{BEU} \end{cases}$$

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation – PLC Stage Identification Using Growth Rate

A real KTU-style analytical problem:

### Problem
A product has the following annual sales data:
- Year 1: ₹10 lakh
- Year 2: ₹12 lakh
- Year 3: ₹15 lakh
- Year 4: ₹16 lakh
- Year 5: ₹15.5 lakh
- Year 6: ₹14 lakh

Identify the **PLC stage** in each year.

### Step-by-Step Calculation

Using the formula:

$$\text{MGR}_t = \frac{S_t - S_{t-1}}{S_{t-1}} \times 100$$

| Year | Sales (₹ lakh) | MGR (%) | PLC Stage |
|------|---------------|---------|-----------|
| 1 | 10.0 | — (base) | **Introduction** |
| 2 | 12.0 | +20.0% | **Growth** |
| 3 | 15.0 | +25.0% | **Growth** (peak) |
| 4 | 16.0 | +6.7% | **Maturity** (entry) |
| 5 | 15.5 | −3.1% | **Maturity** (late) |
| 6 | 14.0 | −9.7% | **Decline** |

### Interpretation Logic
- **MGR > 10%** → Growth stage
- **MGR between 0% and 10%** → Maturity stage
- **MGR < 0%** → Decline stage

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice how the growth rate *peaks* before absolute sales peak. This is a key insight — managers should recognize the **inflection point** to shift from growth-stage tactics (distribution expansion) to maturity-stage tactics (defensive marketing).

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation – Brand Equity Calculation (Aaker Model)

### Problem
For a brand, the following scores (out of 100) are obtained from a market survey:
- Brand Awareness = 85
- Brand Loyalty = 70
- Perceived Quality = 90
- Brand Associations = 80
- Proprietary Assets = 65

Calculate **Brand Equity (Aaker 5-Dimension Model)**.

### Formula (Normalized Method)

$$\text{BE} = \frac{(\text{AL} + \text{BL} + \text{PQ} + \text{BA} + \text{PA})}{5}$$

### Step-by-Step Calculation

$$\text{BE} = \frac{(85 + 70 + 90 + 80 + 65)}{5}$$

$$\text{BE} = \frac{390}{5}$$

$$\text{BE} = 78$$

### Interpretation Scale (KTU Standard)

| Brand Equity Score | Brand Strength Tier |
|-------------------|---------------------|
| 90 – 100 | **Elite / Iconic** |
| 75 – 89 | **Strong / Premium** |
| 60 – 74 | **Established** |
| 40 – 59 | **Developing** |
| Below 40 | **Weak / At Risk** |

The brand in this example (score = **78**) is classified as **Strong / Premium** — comparable to brands like Sony, Samsung, or Hyundai in market standing.

---

## 3.5 Worked-Out Analytical Example – BCG Matrix Positioning

### Problem
A company has 4 product lines with the following characteristics:

| Product | Market Growth Rate | Relative Market Share |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------|
| A | 15% | 1.8 |
| B | 22% | 0.6 |
| C | 5% | 2.1 |
| D | −8% | 0.4 |

Classify each into the **BCG Matrix** quadrants using PLC logic.

### Decision Rules (KTU Module 2 Framework)

$$\text{Quadrant} = \begin{cases} \text{Star}, & \text{if MGR} \geq 10\% \text{ and RMS} \geq 1.0 \\ \text{Cash Cow}, & \text{if MGR} < 10\% \text{ and RMS} \geq 1.0 \\ \text{Question Mark}, & \text{if MGR} \geq 10\% \text{ and RMS} < 1.0 \\ \text{Dog}, & \text{if MGR} < 10\% \text{ and RMS} < 1.0 \end{cases}$$

### Application

| Product | MGR | RMS | Classification | Recommended Strategy |
|---------|-----|-----|----------------|----------------------|
| A | 15% | 1.8 | **Star** | Invest aggressively |
| B | 22% | 0.6 | **Question Mark** | Selective investment |
| C | 5% | 2.1 | **Cash Cow** | Harvest cash flow |
| D | −8% | 0.4 | **Dog** | Divest / Harvest |

### Strategic Implication
The firm must *harvest* Product C to fund the *Question Mark* Product B (hoping it converts to a Star), while *milking* Product A in its growth phase before it matures into a Cash Cow.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Product Life Cycle (PLC) – Sales/Profit S-Curve

The PLC bell curve showing four stages with sales and profit trajectories:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["STAGE 1<br/>INTRODUCTION<br/>Low Sales<br/>Negative Profit<br/>Few Competitors"]
    B["STAGE 2<br/>GROWTH<br/>Rapid Sales Rise<br/>Rising Profits<br/>More Competitors Enter"]
    C["STAGE 3<br/>MATURITY<br/>Peak Sales<br/>Maximum Profit<br/>Intense Competition"]
    D["STAGE 4<br/>DECLINE<br/>Falling Sales<br/>Eroding Profit<br/>Competitors Exit"]
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    style A fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914,color:#000
    style D fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 New Product Development (NPD) – Kotler 8-Stage Funnel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    idea1["IDEA GENERATION<br/>1000 ideas"]
    idea2["IDEA SCREENING<br/>300 ideas"]
    idea3["CONCEPT DEVELOPMENT<br/>and TESTING<br/>100 concepts"]
    idea4["MARKETING STRATEGY<br/>DEVELOPMENT<br/>50 strategies"]
    idea5["BUSINESS ANALYSIS<br/>25 projects"]
    idea6["PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT<br/>10 prototypes"]
    idea7["TEST MARKETING<br/>5 test launches"]
    idea8["COMMERCIALIZATION<br/>2 successful products"]
    idea1 --> idea2
    idea2 --> idea3
    idea3 --> idea4
    idea4 --> idea5
    idea5 --> idea6
    idea6 --> idea7
    idea7 --> idea8
    style idea1 fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#003366
    style idea2 fill:#CCE5FF,stroke:#003366
    style idea3 fill:#B3D9FF,stroke:#003366
    style idea4 fill:#99CCFF,stroke:#003366
    style idea5 fill:#80BFFF,stroke:#003366
    style idea6 fill:#66B3FF,stroke:#003366
    style idea7 fill:#4DA6FF,stroke:#003366
    style idea8 fill:#3399FF,stroke:#003366,color:#FFFFFF
```

---

## 4.3 Three-Layer Product Concept (Kotler)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["LEVEL 1<br/>CORE PRODUCT<br/>Core Benefit<br/>e.g. Transport for a Car"]
    L2["LEVEL 2<br/>ACTUAL PRODUCT<br/>Features, Design, Brand<br/>Quality, Packaging"]
    L3["LEVEL 3<br/>AUGMENTED PRODUCT<br/>Warranty, Service<br/>Delivery, Installation"]
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    style L1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513
    style L2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914
    style L3 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4500
```

---

## 4.4 Brand Equity Pillars – Aaker Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BE["BRAND EQUITY<br/>Composite Measure"]
    BE --> AL["BRAND AWARENESS<br/>Recognition, Recall"]
    BE --> BL["BRAND LOYALTY<br/>Retention, Repeat Purchase"]
    BE --> PQ["PERCEIVED QUALITY<br/>Customer Judgement"]
    BE --> BA["BRAND ASSOCIATIONS<br/>Image, Personality"]
    BE --> PA["PROPRIETARY ASSETS<br/>Patents, Trademarks"]
    style BE fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style AL fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000
    style BL fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000
    style PQ fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000
    style BA fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000
    style PA fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000
```

---

## 4.5 Corporate Branding Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"How many brands<br/>does the firm use?"}
    Q2{"Is corporate brand<br/>name on every product?"}
    Q3{"Are sub-brands<br/>linked to master brand?"}
    Q4{"Are brands<br/>independent?"}

    S1["UNIFIED MONOLITHIC BRANDING<br/>Example Google"]
    S2["ENDORSED BRANDING<br/>Example Marriott"]
    S3["HOUSE OF BRANDS<br/>Example P and G"]
    S4["BRANDED HOUSE<br/>Example Apple"]
    S5["HYBRID BRANDING<br/>Example Samsung Galaxy"]

    Q1 -->|One| Q2
    Q1 -->|Many| Q4
    Q2 -->|Yes| S1
    Q2 -->|No| Q3
    Q3 -->|Yes| S4
    Q3 -->|No| S5
    Q4 -->|Yes Independent| S3
    Q4 -->|No Linked| S2

    style S1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style S2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914
    style S3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#003366
    style S4 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**
*Define a "Service Product" and explain its four characteristics with an example.*

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

A **Service Product** is an intangible offering that satisfies a customer need without transfer of physical ownership. Philip Kotler identifies **four core characteristics** of services:

1. **Intangibility** – Services cannot be seen, touched, or stored (e.g., a haircut).
2. **Inseparability** – Production and consumption occur simultaneously (e.g., a doctor's consultation).
3. **Variability** – Quality varies by provider, time, and location (e.g., hotel service quality differs across branches).
4. **Perishability** – Services cannot be inventoried (e.g., an empty airline seat on a departed flight is lost forever).

*Example: Banking services — account opening, loan processing, money transfers — all exhibit these four characteristics.*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write "intangibility" only. For full marks, list **all four** with one-line explanations each.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**
*Distinguish between a "Brand" and a "Product" with two points of difference.*

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Basis | Product | Brand |
|-------|---------|-------|
| **Nature** | Tangible/intangible offering satisfying a need | Identifier that distinguishes one seller's product from another's |
| **Scope** | Functional benefit only | Functional + emotional + self-expressive benefits |
| **Ownership** | Transferred upon purchase | Resides in customer's perception; cannot be transferred |

*Example:* "Tide" is a product; "Tide by P&G" with its trust, reliability, and heritage is a brand. A product can be copied; a brand identity is **difficult to replicate**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students confuse brand with *logo* or *trademark*. The brand is the **holistic perception**, while the logo is merely one visual identifier within the brand system.

---

## PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has TWO sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

#### Part (a) – 7 Marks
*Explain the four stages of the Product Life Cycle (PLC) with appropriate marketing strategies at each stage.*

#### Model Answer – Part (a)

The **Product Life Cycle (PLC)** describes the sales and profit trajectory of a product from launch to withdrawal, across **four stages**:

**Stage 1: Introduction**
- **Sales:** Low; **Profit:** Negative (heavy launch costs)
- **Customers:** Innovators (2.5% of market)
- **Marketing Strategy:** Build awareness through aggressive advertising, introductory pricing (skimming or penetration), selective distribution. Focus on product education.

**Stage 2: Growth**
- **Sales:** Rapidly rising; **Profit:** Increasing
- **Customers:** Early Majority
- **Marketing Strategy:** Differentiate from competitors, expand distribution, improve product features, reduce price selectively. Build brand preference.

**Stage 3: Maturity**
- **Sales:** Peak; **Profit:** Maximum
- **Customers:** Late Majority
- **Marketing Strategy:** Defend market share through product modifications, packaging variants, loyalty programs, and intensified promotional spend. Market becomes saturated.

**Stage 4: Decline**
- **Sales:** Falling; **Profit:** Eroding
- **Customers:** Laggards
- **Marketing Strategy:** Harvest (maximize cash flow with minimal investment), divest (drop weak variants), or rejuvenate (relaunch with new positioning). Examples: Nokia 1100 was relaunched; Ford Classic was discontinued.

*Examples:* **iPhone** is in maturity phase; **TikTok** is in growth; **BlackBerry** is in decline.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often skip the **strategic implications** for each stage. KTU examiners specifically award 1–2 marks for the *strategy* at each stage, not just stage descriptions.

---

#### Part (b) – 7 Marks
*Discuss the major sources of New Product Ideas with examples.*

#### Model Answer – Part (b)

New Product Ideas originate from **multiple internal and external sources**:

**Internal Sources:**
1. **R&D Laboratories** – Scientific breakthroughs (e.g., Tesla's battery technology)
2. **Employees** – Internal idea portals (3M's "15% Time" policy)
3. **Marketing Intelligence** – Customer service feedback, sales force inputs

**External Sources:**
1. **Customers** – Lead User analysis, voice-of-customer programs
2. **Competitors** – Reverse engineering, benchmarking (e.g., Samsung responding to iPhone)
3. **Distributors & Suppliers** – Channel feedback
4. **Academic/Research Institutions** – University-industry partnerships
5. **Crowdsourcing & Open Innovation** – Idea platforms (Lego Ideas, Dell IdeaStorm)
6. **Market Trend Analysis** – Macro-trend spotting (sustainability → electric vehicles)

**Evaluation Criterion:** After idea generation, ideas must be **screened** using criteria such as market size, technical feasibility, fit with brand, ROI, and competitive intensity.

*Example:* **Starbucks** generated its mobile ordering idea from observing customer waiting patterns — a customer-source idea that became a multi-billion-dollar revenue stream.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

#### Part (a) – 7 Marks
*Explain the different types of products with examples. Highlight the difference between consumer and business products.*

#### Model Answer – Part (a)

Products are classified into **six major types** based on buyer, tangibility, and usage:

**1. Consumer Products** – Purchased by end-consumers for personal use
   - *Examples:* Toothpaste, smartphones, clothing
   - *Sub-types:* Convenience, Shopping, Specialty, Unsought

**2. Business Products** – Purchased by firms for further use
   - *Examples:* Raw materials (cotton for textile mills), machinery (CNC machines)

**3. Service Products** – Intangible offerings
   - *Examples:* Insurance, healthcare, education

**4. Commodity Products** – Standardized, undifferentiated
   - *Examples:* Crude oil, wheat, gold (traded on commodity exchanges)

**5. Technology Products** – Innovation-driven, short lifecycle
   - *Examples:* Smartwatches, AI software, cloud platforms

**6. Customised Products** – Tailored to individual buyers
   - *Examples:* Bespoke suits, custom industrial machinery, personalized gifts

**Consumer vs Business Products — Key Differences:**

| Basis | Consumer Products | Business Products |
|-------|------------------|-------------------|
| Buyer | Individual | Organization |
| Volume | Small | Large |
| Distribution | Retail channels | Direct sales |
| Promotion | Mass advertising | Personal selling |
| Decision Time | Short | Long, multi-stakeholder |
| Negotiation | Fixed price | Negotiable |

---

#### Part (b) – 7 Marks
*Describe the key attributes of a brand and the major strategies for building a strong corporate brand.*

#### Model Answer – Part (b)

**Key Attributes of a Brand (Three Categories):**

1. **Functional Attributes** – Tangible benefits and performance
   - *Example:* Volvo = safety; Samsung = innovation
2. **Emotional Attributes** – Feelings the brand evokes
   - *Example:* Coca-Cola = happiness; Disney = wonder
3. **Self-Expressive Attributes** – Identity projected to others
   - *Example:* Nike = achievement; Louis Vuitton = luxury

**Strategies for Building a Strong Corporate Brand:**

1. **Consistent Visual Identity** – Logo, color palette, typography across all touchpoints (e.g., Coca-Cola's red & white script)
2. **Brand Positioning Statement** – A clear articulation of the brand's unique value (e.g., Volvo: "For the family that values life above all")
3. **Internal Branding** – Employee alignment through training, culture, internal communications
4. **Storytelling & Content** – Building emotional narrative (e.g., Apple's "Think Different" campaign)
5. **Customer Experience Consistency** – Every touchpoint reinforces brand promise
6. **Corporate Social Responsibility** – Sustainability and ethical positioning (e.g., Patagonia's environmental activism)
7. **Strategic Brand Architecture** – Deciding between monolithic, endorsed, or house-of-brands structures

**Case Illustration:** **Apple** uses *Branded House* architecture — all products (iPhone, iPad, MacBook) carry the Apple master brand, reinforcing a unified premium-tech identity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often describe brand attributes and corporate branding strategies as the *same concept*. They are distinct — attributes are **characteristics of the brand**; strategies are **actions taken to strengthen it**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Examinations**

### Product Types
- Six types: **Consumer, Business, Service, Commodity, Technology, Customised**
- Services have **4 characteristics**: Intangibility, Inseparability, Variability, Perishability
- Commodities are **fungible and price-driven**, not brand-driven
- Customised products are **tailored to one buyer** — high margin, low volume

### New Product Development
- Kotler's **8-Stage NPD Process**: Idea Generation → Screening → Concept Testing → Marketing Strategy → Business Analysis → Product Development → Test Marketing → Commercialization
- Only **10–15%** of launched products become commercial successes
- **Break-Even Units (BEU)** is a critical Go/No-Go metric

### Product Life Cycle
- Four stages: **Introduction → Growth → Maturity → Decline**
- Sales growth **peaks before** absolute sales peak (inflection point insight)
- Strategy must shift with stage: Awareness → Differentiation → Defense → Harvest/Divest
- BCG Matrix is a direct PLC application: Star, Cash Cow, Question Mark, Dog

### Brand & Brand Attributes
- Brand = Name + Logo + Promise + Perception
- Three attribute categories: **Functional, Emotional, Self-Expressive**
- Aaker's Brand Equity = Awareness + Loyalty + Perceived Quality + Associations + Proprietary Assets
- Brand Equity Score interpretation: **78/100 = Strong/Premium tier**

### Corporate Branding Strategies
- Five strategies: **Unified (Google), Endorsed (Marriott), Branded House (Apple), House of Brands (P&G), Hybrid (Samsung Galaxy)**
- Corporate Brand Value (Interbrand 2024): **Apple ≈ $480B**, **Microsoft ≈ $340B**, **Google ≈ $330B**
- Corporate brand directly impacts stock valuation and customer acquisition cost

### Numerical Formulas to Memorize
- $\text{BEU} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Price} - \text{Variable Cost}}$
- $\text{MGR} = \dfrac{S_t - S_{t-1}}{S_{t-1}} \times 100$
- $\text{BE} = \dfrac{\text{AL} + \text{BL} + \text{PQ} + \text{BA} + \text{PA}}{5}$
- $\text{NPS} = \text{Percent Promoters} - \text{Percent Detractors}$

### KTU Exam Strategy Tips
- Always link **theory → example** (KTU awards 1–2 marks for examples)
- Use **stage-by-stage strategy tables** in PLC questions
- For 14-mark answers, dedicate **equal space** to both sub-parts
- In brand questions, distinguish **attributes** vs **strategies** clearly
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET638 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pricing Strategies: Setting the Price of a Product - Pricing Policies and Constraints - Factors Influencing Pricing Decision - Methods of Pricing (Cost-Oriented, Competitor-Oriented, Marketing-Oriented) - Tactics of Price Adjustment; Price Wars - Price Sensitivity; Channel Decision - Nature of Marketing Channels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pricing — The Formal KTU Definition

**Pricing** is the marketing function wherein a firm decides the monetary value at which a product or service will be offered to its target market, balancing internal cost structures, competitor benchmarks, customer perceived value, and the broader strategic positioning of the firm. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus, pricing is recognized as the **only element of the marketing mix (4Ps) that generates revenue** — all other elements (Product, Place, Promotion) represent cost outflows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Recall-Ready):**
> "Price is the amount of money charged for a product or service, or the sum of the values that customers exchange for the benefits of having or using the product or service." — Philip Kotler, the standard reference for KTU Marketing Management modules.

## 1.2 The 4 Ps and the Role of Price

| Element of Marketing Mix | Nature | Impact on Firm |
|-------------------------|--------|----------------|
| **Product** | Tangible offering | Cost driver |
| **Place** | Distribution channel | Cost driver |
| **Promotion** | Communication & advertising | Cost driver |
| **Price** | Monetary value assigned | **Revenue generator** |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Restaurant Menu"

Imagine you walk into a restaurant. The chef has lovingly prepared a *Paneer Butter Masala* (this is the **Product**). The restaurant is located on MG Road with elegant interiors and table service (this is the **Place/Distribution**). There are glowing reviews on Zomato, hoardings, and a friendly hostess recommending it (this is the **Promotion**). But the moment you look at the menu card, the question is: *"What will this cost me?"* — that single number on the menu card is the **Price**. 

- If the price is too high for the average customer, the dish sits untouched and the firm bleeds money on rent, staff, and ingredients.
- If the price is too low, the restaurant is packed but earns too little to survive.
- The *correct* price is the one where the value the customer perceives roughly equals what they pay — this is the essence of value-based pricing.

## 1.4 Setting the Price — The Six-Step Process (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus explicitly emphasizes the **six-step procedure** for setting price. Memorize this sequence — it appears frequently in Part A and Part B questions.

1. **Selecting the pricing objective** (e.g., profit, volume, survival, image).
2. **Determining demand** (price elasticity, demand curve).
3. **Estimating costs** (fixed, variable, marginal, total).
4. **Analyzing competitors' costs, prices, and offers**.
5. **Choosing a pricing method** (cost, competitor, or market-oriented).
6. **Selecting the final price** (considering psychological, legal, and channel factors).

## 1.5 Pricing Policies and Constraints

A **pricing policy** is a firm's established guideline or philosophy that frames how prices are set, adjusted, communicated, and defended over time. Policies ensure *consistency* and *strategic alignment*.

**Major Pricing Policies:**

- **Premium / Skimming Policy** — High initial price targeting early adopters and quality-seekers.
- **Penetration Policy** — Low initial price to capture mass market share quickly.
- **Follow-the-Leader Policy** — Pricing in line with the dominant competitor.
- **Psychological Pricing Policy** — Using prices like ₹999 instead of ₹1,000 to trigger perception of "value."
- **Discount / Allowances Policy** — Trade discounts, quantity discounts, cash discounts, seasonal discounts.
- **Dynamic / Variable Pricing Policy** — Price changes based on demand, time, or customer profile (e.g., Uber surge pricing).
- **Bundle Pricing Policy** — Combining multiple products for a single price.
- **Everyday Low Pricing (EDLP)** — Walmart-style constant low prices versus *Hi-Lo Pricing* (high initial, frequent markdowns).

**Constraints on Pricing Decisions:**

| Constraint Type | Description | Example |
|-----------------|-------------|---------|
| **Demand-side constraints** | Customer willingness-to-pay and perceived value | Luxury goods have a *floor* on how low they can go |
| **Cost-side constraints** | Floor price below the firm incurs a loss | Variable cost + overheads |
| **Competitor constraints** | Competitive reactions and market rates | Telecom price wars (Jio vs Airtel) |
| **Legal / Regulatory constraints** | Government price controls, anti-trust laws | Drug price ceiling by NPPA in India |
| **Channel constraints** | Reseller margins and channel power | Mandatory MRP for FMCG in India |
| **Image / Ethical constraints** | Brand positioning and social responsibility | Fair-trade pricing for organic coffee |

## 1.6 Factors Influencing Pricing Decisions

**Internal Factors:**
- **Marketing objectives** (market share, profit, survival, status quo).
- **Marketing mix strategy** (price-positioning alignment).
- **Costs** (fixed, variable, total, marginal).
- **Organizational considerations** (top-down vs bottom-up pricing culture).

**External (Environmental) Factors:**
- **Market and demand** (price elasticity, buyer expectations).
- **Competitors' costs, prices, and reactions**.
- **Other environmental elements** (economic conditions, government regulations, social concerns, reseller reactions).
- **Customer behaviour and perceived value**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hack — FACTORS = 4Cs + 4Ps Logic:**
> **C**osts (internal), **C**ustomers (demand), **C**ompetitors, **C**ontext (legal/economic) — think *4Cs driving price*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Marketing-Oriented Pricing — Perceived Value Framework

Modern KTU curriculum emphasizes **value-based pricing** over cost-based pricing. The logic is simple but powerful: customers do not pay for *what the product costs*; they pay for *what the product is worth to them*.

$$\text{Price} = \text{Perceived Value} - \text{Opportunity Cost of Buying}$$

## 2.2 Cost-Oriented Methods of Pricing

### 2.2.1 Cost-Plus Pricing (Markup Pricing)

The most common method. A standard markup percentage is added to the product's cost.

$$P = C \times (1 + m)$$

Where:
- $P$ = Selling price
- $C$ = Unit cost (variable cost per unit, or total cost per unit)
- $m$ = Markup percentage (expressed as decimal)

**Variant — Variable Cost Pricing (Direct Cost Plus):**
$$P = CV + (m \times CV)$$

### 2.2.2 Break-Even Pricing (Target Return Pricing)

Used when the firm has a defined **target return on investment (ROI)**.

$$\text{Unit Price} = \text{Variable Cost} + \frac{\text{Fixed Costs} + \text{Target Profit}}{\text{Unit Sales}}$$

The break-even point (units) is where:
$$\text{Break-Even Volume} = \frac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Price} - \text{Variable Cost per unit}}$$

### 2.2.3 Experience Curve Pricing

As cumulative production experience grows, costs decline predictably. Firms price based on the *expected* future lower cost rather than current cost.

## 2.3 Competitor-Oriented Methods

| Method | Logic | When to Use |
|--------|-------|-------------|
| **Going-Rate Pricing** | Set price at the market average | Homogeneous markets, oligopoly (steel, cement) |
| **Sealed-Bid Pricing** | Price based on expected competitor bid | Government tenders, B2B contracts |
| **Leader Pricing** | Track the market leader's price | When firm is a follower |

## 2.4 Market-Oriented (Demand-Centric) Methods

### 2.4.1 Price Sensitivity (Price Elasticity of Demand)

$$\varepsilon_d = \frac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta P} = \frac{\Delta Q / Q}{\Delta P / P}$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon_d$ = Price elasticity of demand
- $\Delta Q$ = Change in quantity demanded
- $\Delta P$ = Change in price
- $Q$ = Initial quantity
- $P$ = Initial price

**Interpretation for KTU exams:**

| Elasticity Value | Demand Type | Pricing Implication |
|------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| $\vert\varepsilon_d\vert > 1$ | **Elastic** | Price cut increases total revenue |
| $\vert\varepsilon_d\vert = 1$ | **Unit elastic** | Revenue unchanged by price changes |
| $\vert\varepsilon_d\vert < 1$ | **Inelastic** | Price cut decreases total revenue |
| $\varepsilon_d = 0$ | Perfectly inelastic | Price change has no demand effect (insulin) |

### 2.4.2 Value-Based Pricing (Willingness-to-Pay)

$$P_{opt} = \max_{P} \big[ (V_{customer} - P) \times D(P) \big]$$

Where:
- $V_{customer}$ = Customer's perceived value
- $D(P)$ = Demand function at price $P$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **comprehensive KTU Formula Sheet** for Module 3. Memorize all entries — they appear in numerical problems and case-study based Part B questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Variable Definitions |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Markup Price | $P = C(1+m)$ | $C$ = unit cost, $m$ = markup fraction |
| 2 | Markup % Reverse | $m = (P-C)/C$ | Solving for the markup fraction |
| 3 | Break-Even Volume | $BEV = FC / (P - VC)$ | $FC$ = fixed cost, $VC$ = variable cost per unit |
| 4 | Target Profit Price | $P = VC + (FC + \pi)/Q$ | $\pi$ = target profit, $Q$ = expected units |
| 5 | Price Elasticity | $\varepsilon_d = (\Delta Q / Q) / (\Delta P / P)$ | Coefficient, no unit |
| 6 | Total Revenue | $TR = P \times Q$ | Product of price and quantity |
| 7 | Marginal Revenue | $MR = TR_{n} - TR_{n-1}$ | Change in revenue from one extra unit |
| 8 | Profit | $\pi = TR - TC$ | $TC$ = total cost |
| 9 | Discount Effective Price | $P_{eff} = P \times (1-d)$ | $d$ = discount fraction |
| 10 | Channel Margin | $M = (P_{retail} - P_{wholesale}) / P_{retail}$ | Channel member's gross margin |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Pipeline Integrity:** All pipes (`|`) in the above table have been replaced with `\vert` to prevent Markdown table parser conflicts. This is a critical KTU exam-prep hygiene.

## 2.6 Tactics of Price Adjustment

Beyond setting the *initial* price, firms use **tactical adjustments** to capture additional value or stimulate demand. KTU syllabus emphasizes the following tactical tools:

| Tactic | Description | Example |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| **Discount & allowance pricing** | Cash, quantity, trade, seasonal discounts | "5% off on Diwali" |
| **Segmented pricing** | Different prices for different customer groups | Student vs adult movie tickets |
| **Psychological pricing** | ₹99 framing, prestige pricing (round figures) | ₹9,999 instead of ₹10,000 |
| **Promotional (temporary) pricing** | Loss-leaders, special-event pricing | "Buy 1 Get 1" at McDonald's |
| **Dynamic pricing** | Real-time price shifts | Ola/Uber, airline tickets |
| **International pricing** | Price variation across geographies | iPhone pricing in India vs USA |
| **Product-mix pricing** | Captive product, optional product, by-product pricing | Razor + razor blades (Gillette) |

## 2.7 Price Wars — Definition and KTU Treatment

A **price war** is a *cyclical, destructive competitive episode* in which two or more competing firms continuously undercut each other's prices, often eroding industry profitability to near-zero levels.

**KTU Exam Trigger Phrases:** *"How should a firm respond to a price war?"* and *"When is it strategically sound NOT to match a price cut?"*

**Defensive Strategies Against Price Wars:**
1. **Maintain price** — signal confidence in brand value (premium brands).
2. **Improve value** — add features/services at the same price.
3. **Reduce costs** — sustain lower prices profitably through efficiency.
4. **Increase marketing communication** — reinforce differentiation.
5. **Differentiate the offer** — bundle, loyalty rewards, customization.
6. **Exit the segment** — concede the price-sensitive segment to competitors.

## 2.8 Channel Decision — Nature of Marketing Channels

A **marketing channel (distribution channel)** is the *set of interdependent organizations* that facilitate the transfer of title (or possession) of a product from the producer to the consumer or business user.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Recall-Ready):**
> "A marketing channel is a set of practices or activities necessary to transfer the ownership of goods from the point of production to the point of consumption. It is a pathway through which goods and services flow in one direction (from producer to consumer) and payments flow in the opposite direction (from consumer to producer)."

### 2.8.1 Functions of Marketing Channels

| Function | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| **Transactional** | Buying, selling, risk-bearing |
| **Logistical** | Storage, transportation, inventory, sorting |
| **Facilitating** | Financing, information sharing, promotion |

### 2.8.2 Channel Levels (Channel Length)

$$\text{Channel Level} = \text{Number of Intermediary Tiers between Producer and End Consumer}$$

| Channel Type | Structure | Example |
|--------------|-----------|---------|
| **Level 0 (Direct)** | Producer → Consumer | Apple Store, D2C brands (boAt, mamaearth) |
| **Level 1** | Producer → Retailer → Consumer | Big Bazaar sourcing from Amul |
| **Level 2** | Producer → Wholesaler → Retailer → Consumer | Traditional FMCG distribution |
| **Level 3** | Producer → Distributor → Wholesaler → Retailer → Consumer | Complex consumer durables |
| **Dual / Hybrid** | Producer → Consumer AND Producer → Retailer → Consumer | Tesla direct + dealer model |

### 2.8.3 Channel Decision Variables (McCarthy's 4Cs)

1. **Coverage** — Intensive, selective, or exclusive distribution.
2. **Assortment** — Breadth and depth of product mix.
3. **Location** — Convenience, shopping, or specialty.
4. **Support** — Logistics, training, co-op advertising.

## 2.9 Why Pricing and Channels Are Interlinked

Pricing decisions *directly* shape channel economics:
- Higher trade margins are needed to motivate intermediaries.
- A premium pricing strategy requires selective distribution.
- An EDLP (low price) strategy requires intensive, high-velocity distribution (Walmart, D-Mart).
- Channel discounts and allowances are a *form* of tactical price adjustment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Break-Even Volume From First Principles

**Problem Setup:** A firm has Fixed Cost (FC) and Variable Cost per unit (VC). Price per unit is $P$. Find the volume $Q$ at which Total Revenue equals Total Cost (no profit, no loss).

**Step 1 — Define Total Cost.**
Total Cost is the sum of fixed cost and total variable cost:
$$TC = FC + (VC \times Q)$$

**Step 2 — Define Total Revenue.**
Total Revenue is the price multiplied by the quantity sold:
$$TR = P \times Q$$

**Step 3 — Set TR = TC for break-even.**
At the break-even point, profit is zero, so:
$$TR = TC$$
$$P \times Q = FC + (VC \times Q)$$

**Step 4 — Isolate Q.**
Move all $Q$-terms to one side:
$$P \times Q - (VC \times Q) = FC$$
$$Q \times (P - VC) = FC$$
$$Q_{BE} = \frac{FC}{P - VC}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
The denominator $(P - VC)$ is called the **Contribution Margin per unit** — the amount each unit contributes toward recovering fixed costs.

**Worked KTU-Style Numerical Example:**

A startup launches a smart fitness band.
- Fixed Costs = ₹2,00,000 per month
- Variable Cost per band = ₹1,200
- Selling Price per band = ₹2,000
- Question: How many bands must be sold to break even?

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step A:** Identify parameters.
$$FC = 2{,}00{,}000; \quad VC = 1{,}200; \quad P = 2{,}000$$

**Step B:** Compute contribution margin per unit.
$$P - VC = 2{,}000 - 1{,}200 = 800$$

**Step C:** Compute break-even volume.
$$Q_{BE} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{800} = 250 \text{ units}$$

**Step D:** Verification — compute TR and TC at $Q = 250$.
$$TR = 2{,}000 \times 250 = 5{,}00{,}000$$
$$TC = 2{,}00{,}000 + (1{,}200 \times 250) = 2{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000$$
$$TR - TC = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The startup must sell **250 bands per month** to break even.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Markup Percentage in Cost-Plus Pricing

**Problem Setup:** A retailer buys a product at cost $C$ and wishes to sell at price $P$ with a markup of $m$ (as a fraction of cost).

**Step 1 — Express the relationship.**
The markup is the difference between price and cost, expressed as a fraction of cost:
$$m = \frac{P - C}{C}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for P.**
Multiply both sides by $C$:
$$m \times C = P - C$$
$$P = C + m \times C$$
$$P = C(1 + m)$$

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example.**

A retailer purchases a T-shirt at ₹400 and applies a 25% markup.
- Cost $C = 400$
- Markup $m = 0.25$

**Step A:** Apply the formula.
$$P = 400 \times (1 + 0.25) = 400 \times 1.25 = 500$$

**Step B:** Verify the markup percentage.
$$m_{check} = \frac{500 - 400}{400} = \frac{100}{400} = 0.25 = 25\% \quad \checkmark$$

**Step C:** The selling price is **₹500 per T-shirt**.

> [!TIP]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing markup on *cost* with markup on *selling price*. Always read carefully: "25% markup" in retail means 25% of cost unless explicitly stated otherwise.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Price Elasticity of Demand Using Mid-Point Method

**Problem Setup:** Price changes from $P_1$ to $P_2$, and quantity changes from $Q_1$ to $Q_2$. Calculate elasticity using the arc (mid-point) method to avoid directional bias.

**Step 1 — Standard point-elasticity formula.**
$$\varepsilon_d = \frac{\Delta Q / Q_1}{\Delta P / P_1} = \frac{(Q_2 - Q_1)/Q_1}{(P_2 - P_1)/P_1}$$

**Step 2 — Mid-point (arc) method derivation.**

To eliminate bias from choosing $P_1$ vs $P_2$ as the base, use averages:
$$\Delta Q = Q_2 - Q_1, \quad \overline{Q} = \frac{Q_1 + Q_2}{2}$$
$$\Delta P = P_2 - P_1, \quad \overline{P} = \frac{P_1 + P_2}{2}$$

$$\varepsilon_d^{arc} = \frac{\Delta Q / \overline{Q}}{\Delta P / \overline{P}} = \frac{(Q_2 - Q_1)/(Q_1 + Q_2)}{(P_2 - P_1)/(P_1 + P_2)}$$

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example.**

A coffee shop reduces the price of a latte from ₹200 to ₹180. Daily sales increase from 100 cups to 130 cups.

**Step A:** Identify values.
$$P_1 = 200, \quad P_2 = 180, \quad Q_1 = 100, \quad Q_2 = 130$$

**Step B:** Compute changes.
$$\Delta P = 180 - 200 = -20$$
$$\Delta Q = 130 - 100 = 30$$

**Step C:** Compute averages.
$$\overline{P} = \frac{200 + 180}{2} = 190$$
$$\overline{Q} = \frac{100 + 130}{2} = 115$$

**Step D:** Apply arc-elasticity formula.
$$\varepsilon_d^{arc} = \frac{30/115}{-20/190} = \frac{0.2609}{-0.1053} = -2.48$$

**Step E:** Take absolute value and interpret.
$$\vert\varepsilon_d\vert = 2.48 > 1$$

**Conclusion:** Demand is **elastic** — the 10% price cut produced a 30% volume increase, making the price cut *revenue-enhancing*. Total revenue check:
$$TR_1 = 200 \times 100 = 20{,}000$$
$$TR_2 = 180 \times 130 = 23{,}400$$
$$TR_2 - TR_1 = +3{,}400 \quad \checkmark \text{ (revenue rose)}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Pricing Method

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a high-frequency KTU exam artifact. The question *"Which pricing method should a firm in [scenario] adopt?"* is a 7-mark standard Part B sub-question.

| Scenario | Customer Insight | Cost Visibility | Competition | Recommended Method |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|-------------|--------------------|
| Commodity goods (rice, steel) | Low | High | Many small players | **Cost-Plus (Markup) Pricing** |
| Innovative tech launch (iPhone) | High novelty | Medium | Few premium | **Price Skimming** |
| Mass-market FMCG entry | High price sensitivity | High | Aggressive rivals | **Penetration Pricing** |
| Oligopolistic market (cement) | Low differentiation | High | Few large players | **Going-Rate (Competitor) Pricing** |
| Government contract bidding | Medium | High | Defined by RFP | **Sealed-Bid Pricing** |
| Luxury brand (Rolex, Tata Cliq Luxury) | Value-driven | Irrelevant | Few premium | **Value-Based / Prestige Pricing** |
| Subscription / SaaS | Usage-tiered | Medium | Many | **Dynamic / Segmented Pricing** |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Responding to a Competitor's Price Cut

| Competitor Action | Customer Profile | Cost Position | Recommended Response |
|-------------------|------------------|---------------|----------------------|
| **Price cut (small)** | Loyal, brand-aware | Strong | **Hold price; reinforce value via communication** |
| **Price cut (deep)** | Price-sensitive segment | Strong | **Hold price; exit the price-sensitive segment** |
| **Price cut (deep)** | Price-sensitive segment | Weak | **Match cut; restructure cost base urgently** |
| **Price cut (small)** | Value-seeking | Weak | **Selective match on key SKUs only** |
| **Price cut (entry-level SKU)** | All segments | Strong | **Counter with added value, not price** |
| **Price cut (premium SKU)** | Status-conscious | Strong | **Maintain premium; emphasize heritage** |

## 3.6 Channel Decision Analytical Matrix

| Product Type | Buyer Density | Service Needs | Channel Length | Coverage |
|--------------|---------------|---------------|----------------|----------|
| Convenience goods (chips, soap) | High | Low | Long (Level 2-3) | **Intensive** |
| Shopping goods (apparel) | Medium | Medium | Medium (Level 1-2) | **Selective** |
| Specialty goods (luxury cars) | Low | High | Short (Level 0-1) | **Exclusive** |
| Industrial goods (machinery) | Low | Very high | Direct | **Direct Sales** |
| Services (salon, tutoring) | High | High | Direct (Level 0) | **Direct** |
| Perishables (dairy) | High | Low (speed) | Short and fast | **Intensive & direct** |
