# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEMET638]**

### **Q1. Distinguish between Informative Advertising and Persuasive Advertising. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> Informative advertising is used in the **introduction stage** of the product life cycle with the objective of creating **primary demand** — i.e., making consumers aware that a new product category exists. It focuses on the *what* and *how* of the product. For example, the initial Jio 4G launch ads in 2016 were informative.
>
> In contrast, persuasive advertising is used in the **growth stage** with the objective of creating **selective demand** — i.e., making consumers prefer *one specific brand* over its competitors. It focuses on the *why* and uses emotional hooks, testimonials, and USPs. For example, Coca-Cola's "Open Happiness" campaign targeted preference over Pepsi.
>
> *(Key distinction marker: Informative → builds the **category**; Persuasive → builds the **brand**.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEMET638]**

### **Q2. What is Reinforcement Advertising? Give one example. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> Reinforcement advertising is a type of advertising aimed at the **existing customers** of a brand, with the objective of **assuring them that they have made the right purchase decision** and reducing post-purchase **cognitive dissonance**. It is typically used in the **maturity stage** of the product life cycle for high-involvement or premium products.
>
> **Example:** Apple's "Shot on iPhone" campaign — which reassures existing iPhone users of the superior camera quality of their device, thereby reinforcing brand loyalty and reducing buyer's remorse.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 4, PEMET638]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Classify the different types of advertisements based on advertising objective. Explain any two in detail with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> Advertising objectives give rise to **five primary types** of advertisements:
>
> 1. **Informative (Pioneering) Advertising** — Used in the *Introduction stage* to build *primary demand* by creating awareness of a new product. Example: The launch campaign of **Tesla Model 3 in India** focused on educating the audience about EV technology.
>
> 2. **Persuasive (Competitive) Advertising** — Used in the *Growth stage* to build *selective demand* and develop brand preference. Example: **Nike's "Just Do It"** campaign persuades athletes to prefer Nike over Adidas or Puma.
>
> 3. **Reminder Advertising** — Used in the *Maturity stage* to stimulate *repeat purchase*. Example: **Coca-Cola's summer refresher ads** remind consumers to pick up a Coke during hot weather.
>
> 4. **Reinforcement Advertising** — Used to reassure current buyers. Example: **Apple's "Switch from Android"** campaign reinforces the wisdom of being an iOS user.
>
> 5. **Comparative Advertising** — Used to directly contrast with a named competitor. Example: **Dettol's "Dettol vs. ordinary soap"** germ-protection campaigns.
>
> **Detailed explanation of Informative Advertising:**
> - **Purpose:** Educate the consumer about a *new product or category*.
> - **Stage:** Product Life Cycle – Introduction.
> - **Message content:** What the product is, what problem it solves, how to use it, where to buy.
> - **Example:** When **CRED** (the credit-card bill payment app) launched, its early ads were heavily informative — explaining the *cashback rewards* and *gamified experience*.
>
> **Detailed explanation of Persuasive Advertising:**
> - **Purpose:** Convert *awareness into brand preference*.
> - **Stage:** Product Life Cycle – Growth.
> - **Message content:** Emotional storytelling, celebrity endorsement, USPs.
> - **Example:** **Dove's "Real Beauty"** campaign used emotional persuasion to position Dove as the brand that celebrates natural beauty, differentiating it from competitors using airbrushed models.
>
> *(Validation: Mapping each ad type to the corresponding PLC stage earns the examiner's full 7 marks.)*

**(b) Discuss the various types of advertisements based on the medium used. Which medium is most suitable for a new FMCG product launch in a Tier-2 Indian city? Justify. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Medium-based types of advertisements:**
>
> 1. **Print Advertising** — Newspapers, magazines, brochures, flyers, direct mail. *Strength:* High credibility, long shelf life. *Weakness:* No audio-visual, declining reach.
> 2. **Broadcast Advertising** — Television, radio. *Strength:* Mass reach, audio-visual impact. *Weakness:* High cost, low targeting.
> 3. **Outdoor (OOH) Advertising** — Billboards, hoardings, wall paintings, digital OOH. *Strength:* 24×7 visibility. *Weakness:* Hard to measure.
> 4. **Transit Advertising** — Bus wraps, metro panels, airport displays, taxi-top ads. *Strength:* Captive audience. *Weakness:* Limited to cities.
> 5. **Digital / Online Advertising** — Search ads, display banners, YouTube pre-rolls. *Strength:* Highly targetable, measurable. *Weakness:* Ad-blockers.
> 6. **Social Media Advertising** — Facebook, Instagram, X, LinkedIn, TikTok. *Strength:* Engagement, virality. *Weakness:* Negative feedback can spread.
> 7. **Mobile Advertising** — SMS, in-app ads, WhatsApp marketing. *Strength:* Always-on, location-based. *Weakness:* Permission required.
> 8. **Point-of-Sale (POS) Advertising** — Shelf-talkers, danglers, billing-counter displays. *Strength:* Triggers last-mile impulse. *Weakness:* Retail-only.
> 9. **Word-of-Mouth / Influencer Marketing** — Local influencers, referral programs. *Strength:* Highest trust. *Weakness:* Hard to scale.
>
> **Suitability for a new FMCG launch in a Tier-2 city:**
>
> The most effective media mix would be:
>
> - **Local Cable TV + Radio** — for mass awareness across the city and surrounding rural areas.
> - **Outdoor Hoardings & Wall Paintings** — for 24×7 visibility at junctions and marketplaces.
> - **Social Media (Instagram Reels + Facebook Ads)** — to reach the *young, smartphone-savvy* population in Tier-2 India.
> - **Point-of-Sale Danglers** — at local kirana stores to trigger the impulse purchase.
> - **Influencer Marketing** — partnering with local micro-influencers (10K–50K followers) for trust.
>
> **Justification:** A Tier-2 city has a *mixed audience* — semi-urban, multilingual, and value-conscious. Pure digital advertising will miss the older, non-smartphone demographic. Pure print will miss the youth. Hence, an **integrated marketing communication (IMC) approach** combining local TV, OOH, social media, and POS is recommended. This balances *reach* (TV, OOH), *engagement* (social media, influencers), and *conversion* (POS danglers).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Compare and contrast Product Advertising and Institutional Advertising. Under what circumstances would a company prefer institutional advertising over product advertising? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Product Advertising** focuses on a *specific product, brand, or SKU* with the goal of driving direct sales. It highlights the features, benefits, and USPs of the product. Example: **Surf Excel's "Daag Acche Hain"** campaign promotes the *stain-removal product* directly.
>
> **Institutional (Corporate) Advertising** focuses on the *organisation itself* — its mission, values, culture, and contribution to society — with the goal of building long-term *corporate image* rather than immediate sales. Example: **Infosys's "Navigate Your Next"** corporate brand campaign.
>
> | **Parameter** | **Product Advertising** | **Institutional Advertising** |
> |---|---|---|
> | Focus | A specific product | The whole company |
> | Objective | Drive sales | Build reputation & goodwill |
> | Measurement | Sales lift, ROI | Brand equity, perception scores |
> | Time Horizon | Short-term | Long-term |
> | Examples | Maggi noodles, Ariel detergent | Tata Group, Google India |
>
> **When a company prefers institutional advertising:**
>
> 1. **Crisis Management** — When the brand is facing a PR crisis (e.g., **Nestlé Maggi's 2015 ban** in India — Maggi ran *institutional* ads rebuilding consumer trust in the company).
> 2. **Diversification** — When a company is entering *new categories* and wants to leverage existing corporate equity.
> 3. **B2B Trust Building** — For industrial companies like **L&T** or **Reliance Industries**, the *corporate reputation* drives B2B sales.
> 4. **M&A Activity** — Post-merger, a company runs institutional ads to communicate the *new unified identity*.
> 5. **CSR & Sustainability** — Companies like **Unilever** use institutional ads to highlight their sustainability mission, indirectly benefiting all brands in the portfolio.

**(b) "The choice of advertising type is a function of the product life cycle stage." Critically evaluate this statement with examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> The Product Life Cycle (PLC) is a powerful framework that maps the **four stages** of a product's commercial journey — Introduction, Growth, Maturity, and Decline — and each stage calls for a *distinct advertising strategy* and ad type. Let us evaluate this statement stage by stage.
>
> **1. Introduction Stage — Informative / Pioneering Ads**
> In the introduction stage, the product is new, the consumer is unaware, and competition is minimal. The advertising task is to **build primary demand** — i.e., make the category itself desirable. *Example:* When **WhatsApp Pay (UPI)** launched in India in 2018, the initial advertising was purely *informative*, educating users on how to send money via a chat interface.
>
> **2. Growth Stage — Persuasive / Comparative Ads**
> As competitors enter, the company must **build selective demand** and steal market share. *Example:* During the 2018–2020 growth of the Indian EV scooter market, **Ola Electric, Ather, and Hero Vida** ran persuasive ads comparing range, charging time, and smart features to win customers.
>
> **3. Maturity Stage — Reminder Ads**
> In maturity, the market is saturated and the product is well-known. The advertising goal is to **stimulate repeat purchase** and maintain top-of-mind awareness. *Example:* **Parle-G** runs nostalgic reminder ads every few years ("Kisne dekha Bharat ka apna...") to keep the biscuit top-of-mind for older generations.
>
> **4. Decline Stage — Reinforcement / Harvesting Ads**
> In decline, companies either *harvest* (reduce ad spend) or *reinforce loyalty* among the remaining niche. *Example:* **Black & Decker** reduced TV ads for traditional power tools in decline and shifted to *reinforcement* ads for cordless tool upgrades.
>
> **Critical Evaluation:**
> The PLC framework is useful, but in **modern digital marketing**, the PLC is often *compressed* — products move from introduction to maturity in months, not years. A startup like **boAt** (audio wearables) used **influencer marketing and social media** across all PLC stages simultaneously, blurring the traditional ad-type-to-PLC mapping. Similarly, *direct-to-consumer (D2C) brands* often skip comparative ads and rely on *brand storytelling*.
>
> **Conclusion:**
> The PLC-based ad-type mapping is a **useful heuristic** but not a rigid rule. In volatile, digitally connected markets, smart marketers use a **hybrid approach** — combining informative, persuasive, and reminder ads *concurrently* across digital channels to maximise impact.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on "Types of Advertisements" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing classification dimensions.** Students often mix "Product vs. Institutional" with "Informative vs. Persuasive" as if they were the same axis. They are NOT. One is *sponsor-based*, the other is *objective-based*. **Always state which classification axis you are answering on.**
> 2. **Forgetting the PLC link.** Writing only definitions without mapping to *Introduction / Growth / Maturity / Decline* loses **2–3 marks** per question.
> 3. **Skipping examples.** KTU valuation key requires *at least one Indian brand example per sub-type*. Use **Maggi, Amul, Tata, Jio, Flipkart, boAt** — not just Coca-Cola and Nike.
> 4. **No justification for medium choice.** When asked "Which medium is best?", students list pros and cons but never *recommend a specific medium for a specific scenario*. Always end with a **justified recommendation**.
> 5. **Missing ASCII / bullet structure in long answers.** Examiners spend 30 seconds per answer. Use **numbered points, sub-headings, and tables** to make scanning easy.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Advertisements** are classified on **four axes** — *Objective, Audience, Sponsor, Medium*.
- The **Objective-based** classification is the most-tested and consists of: **Informative, Persuasive, Reminder, Reinforcement, Comparative**.
- **Informative Ads ↔ Introduction Stage** → Build *primary demand* / category awareness.
- **Persuasive Ads ↔ Growth Stage** → Build *selective demand* / brand preference.
- **Reminder Ads ↔ Maturity Stage** → Stimulate *repeat purchase* / top-of-mind recall.
- **Reinforcement Ads ↔ Maturity → Decline** → Reduce *cognitive dissonance* / reinforce loyalty.
- **Comparative Ads** directly name or imply a *competitor brand* (subject to **ASCI Code** in India).
- **Institutional Ads** promote the *corporation itself* (used in **crisis management** and **CSR**).
- **B2B / Industrial Ads** are *rational and data-driven*; **B2C / Consumer Ads** are *emotional and lifestyle-driven*.
- **Print + Broadcast + OOH + Transit** are the **traditional** media; **Digital + Social + Mobile + Influencer** are the **modern** media.
- **Word-of-Mouth (WOM)** remains the **highest-trust** advertising form, even in the digital age.
- Always link the chosen **ad type** to the corresponding **PLC stage**, the **target audience**, and the **marketing objective (DAGMAR)** — this is the **full-marks formula** in KTU valuation.
- A **hybrid media mix** (IMC approach) is the modern industry standard — never recommend *just one* medium for a serious campaign.
- Use **Indian brand examples** (Maggi, Amul, Tata, Jio, Flipkart, boAt, CRED) — international examples alone are marked down.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact of Advertisements - Role of Media in Advertisements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET638 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Impact of Advertisements - Role of Media in Advertisements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the Impact of Advertisements?

**Advertising Impact** refers to the measurable effect that an advertising message produces on the target audience's **cognition (awareness), affect (attitude/preference), and behavior (purchase/intent)**. It is the bridge between a brand's communication spend and its commercial outcomes, evaluated across three classic response stages: **cognitive, affective, and conative (behavioral)** — collectively known as the **Lavidge-Steiner Hierarchy of Effects Model**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMET638 Module 4):**
> *"Advertising impact is the net psychological, sociological, and economic change brought about in the consumer's mind-set and marketplace behavior as a result of exposure to paid, non-personal communication through identified media."*

In simpler words, advertising impact answers the question: **"Did the ad actually work?"** — not just "Did people see it?"

## 1.2 What is the Role of Media in Advertisements?

**Media** in advertising is the **carrier or channel** that physically delivers the advertiser's message from the sender (brand) to the receiver (consumer). Without media, an ad is just a creative idea trapped inside a studio. The role of media is to:

1. **Carry the message** to the right audience,
2. **Optimize reach** (how many unique people see it),
3. **Optimize frequency** (how often they see it),
4. **Set context** (the editorial environment that shapes brand perception), and
5. **Control cost-efficiency** of the campaign.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Bulletin Board"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Imagine you bake the world's best chocolate cake. The *cake* is your **advertisement (creative)**. The *bulletin board at the supermarket, a flyer, Instagram story, TV commercial, and a newspaper ad* are the **media**. Even the most delicious cake (ad) reaches zero customers if posted on a hidden board in a desert. Choosing the right board (media) decides whether the cake is tasted, remembered, and reordered. This is precisely the **role of media** — to place the right creative in front of the right mouth at the right moment.

## 1.4 Three Pillars of Advertising Impact (KTU Classification)

| Pillar | Meaning | Example Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Cognitive Impact** | Awareness, recognition, recall | Ad Recall %, Top-of-Mind Awareness (TOMA) |
| **Affective Impact** | Attitude, preference, liking | Brand Favorability Score, Message Believability |
| **Conative Impact** | Action — inquiry, trial, purchase | Coupon Redemption Rate, Sales Lift % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Questions in Module 4 frequently test your ability to *differentiate* between these three impact types and *map a media channel* (e.g., TV vs. Newspaper) to a particular impact type.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The DAGMAR Model (Defining Advertising Goals for Measured Advertising Results)

A widely tested KTU framework for measuring advertising impact. DAGMAR specifies that advertising must move the prospect through a **defined sequence of communication states**:

1. **Unawareness → Awareness** (Cognitive)
2. **Awareness → Comprehension** (Cognitive)
3. **Comprehension → Conviction** (Affective)
4. **Conviction → Action** (Conative)

Each stage is **measurable** with a specific KPI.

## 2.2 The AIDA Model (Attention → Interest → Desire → Action)

A parallel hierarchy that maps **impact type → media suitability**:

| Stage | Impact Type | Best-Suited Media |
|---|---|---|
| Attention | Cognitive | TV, Outdoor, Display Banners |
| Interest | Cognitive/Affective | Magazines, Influencer Videos |
| Desire | Affective | Storytelling Ads, Cinema |
| Action | Conative | Direct Mail, SMS, Search Ads |

## 2.3 Role of Media — Five Functional Roles

1. **Information Disseminator** — conveys product facts, features, prices.
2. **Persuasion Engine** — builds favourable attitudes via emotional cues.
3. **Reminder Trigger** — maintains top-of-mind recall between purchases.
4. **Image Builder** — associates the brand with status, lifestyle, or values.
5. **Distribution of Demand** — smooths sales across geographies and seasons.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Media & Impact Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for ESE 2024 — questions of 7-mark weightage often use these formulas.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{CPM} = \dfrac{C}{N} \times 1000$ | C = Cost of ad insertion, N = Audience size | Cost Per Mille (thousand) — media cost efficiency |
| 2 | $\text{GRP} = R \times F$ | R = Reach %, F = Average Frequency | Gross Rating Points — total ad weight delivered |
| 3 | $\text{Reach} = \dfrac{\text{Unique Audience}}{\text{Total Target Population}} \times 100$ | % of target exposed at least once | % |
| 4 | $\text{Frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Total Impressions}}{\text{Unique Audience}}$ | Average exposures per person | Number |
| 5 | $\text{OTS} = \dfrac{\text{Impressions Delivered}}{\text{Target Population}} \times 100$ | Opportunities-To-See | % equivalent |
| 6 | $\text{ROAS} = \dfrac{\text{Revenue from Ads}}{\text{Ad Spend}} \times 100$ | Return on Ad Spend | % |
| 7 | $\text{Sales Lift} = \dfrac{S_{a} - S_{b}}{S_{b}} \times 100$ | $S_a$ = Sales with ad, $S_b$ = Baseline sales | % |
| 8 | $\text{Brand Recall} = \dfrac{\text{Aided/Unaided Recallers}}{\text{Sample Size}} \times 100$ | Cognitive impact metric | % |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Rule:** Never use the literal character `\vert` in raw form inside markdown tables of KTU board answers; if writing in plain text, prefer "abs(x)" or the word "absolute" to avoid formula corruption.

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Real-World Engineering-Marketing Crossover

Even though PEMET638 is a management course, **KTU values the linkage**: product engineers design the *what*; marketers use media to deliver the *why-it-matters* to the right *whom*. Media planning today uses **algorithmic bidding, programmatic DSPs, and AI-driven attribution** — meaning the same students who study CPM/GRPs in marketing will encounter them as **CTR, CPC, and CPA** in digital analytics tools.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Reach × Frequency Relationship (GRPs)

We begin with the most basic unit of media delivery — the **impression**.

**Step 1 — Define an Impression.**
An *impression* is one exposure of an ad to one individual at one point in time.

$$
I = R \times N
$$

where $I$ = total impressions, $R$ = Reach (in absolute number of unique people), and $N$ = total target population.

> *Text row: Reach multiplied by the universe size gives the number of people who see the ad at least once.*

**Step 2 — Express Frequency as Average Repetition.**

$$
F = \dfrac{I}{R}
$$

> *Text row: Total exposures divided by unique reach gives the average number of times each person was exposed.*

**Step 3 — Substitute $I$ from Step 1 into Step 2.**

$$
F = \dfrac{R \times N}{R} = N
$$

Wait — this holds only if Reach is expressed as a **proportion** (decimal), not as an absolute count. Correcting:

$$
F = \dfrac{I}{R} \quad \text{and} \quad I = \text{Reach}_{\%} \times \text{Target Pop} \times F
$$

So for a media plan:

$$
\text{GRP} = \text{Reach}_{\%} \times F
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-mark style).**

> A brand targets **10 lakh people** in Kerala. A TV campaign delivers **30 lakh impressions** at a cost of **₹6 lakh**.

- Reach % = (Unique Audience / Target) × 100

Assume a measured unique audience of 6 lakh:

$$
\text{Reach}_{\%} = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 60\%
$$

- Frequency = Total Impressions / Unique Audience

$$
F = \dfrac{30{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} = 5
$$

- GRP = Reach × Frequency

$$
\text{GRP} = 60 \times 5 = 300
$$

- CPM = (Cost / Impressions) × 1000

$$
\text{CPM} = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{30{,}00{,}000} \times 1000 = \text{₹ } 200
$$

> *Interpretation: A CPM of ₹200 is competitive for South Indian TV; a GRP of 300 is considered a heavy-weight campaign in Kerala's regional media planning.*

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Media to Impact Type

| Media Channel | Cognitive Impact | Affective Impact | Conative Impact | Cost Tier | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Television** | Very High | Very High | Medium | High | Mass brand launches |
| **Print (Newspaper)** | High | Medium | Medium | Medium | Local, price-led promos |
| **Radio** | Medium | High | Low | Low | In-car, regional reach |
| **Outdoor (Hoardings)** | High | Low | Low | Low | Reminder, brand visibility |
| **Cinema** | High | Very High | Low | High | Premium brand imagery |
| **Digital (Search/Social)** | High | Medium | Very High | Variable | Direct response, ROAS |
| **Direct Mail** | Medium | High | High | High per piece | Loyalty, B2B |
| **Influencer / UGC** | High | Very High | High | Variable | Trust, Gen-Z segments |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked "Which media builds *affective* impact best?", the model answer is **Television and Cinema** — because of their audio-visual storytelling capacity.

## 3.3 The Impact-Response Curve (Why More Spend ≠ More Impact)

Beyond a saturation point, additional advertising expenditure yields *diminishing marginal impact* on awareness and sales. The mathematical intuition:

$$
\dfrac{\partial (\text{Impact})}{\partial (\text{Spend})} > 0 \quad \text{but} \quad \dfrac{\partial^{2} (\text{Impact})}{\partial (\text{Spend})^{2}} < 0
$$

> *Text row: First derivative is positive — more spend raises impact. Second derivative is negative — the rate of increase slows. This is the **Law of Diminishing Returns in Advertising**, a frequently tested KTU concept.*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Advertising Impact Flow — Sender to Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Advertiser Brand] --> B[Ad Agency Creative]
    B --> C[Media Planner]
    C --> D[Media Channel Selection]
    D --> E1[TV]
    D --> E2[Print]
    D --> E3[Digital]
    D --> E4[Outdoor]
    D --> E5[Radio]
    E1 --> F[Consumer Exposure]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G1[Cognitive Impact Awareness Recall]
    F --> G2[Affective Impact Attitude Preference]
    F --> G3[Conative Impact Action Purchase]
    G1 --> H[Brand Equity Growth]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Sales and Market Share]
```

> *Diagram Reading Guide:* The flow moves from brand → creative → media planner → channel mix → consumer exposure → three impact buckets → brand equity → sales. Any **blockage at the media selection stage** collapses the entire downstream impact.

## 4.2 Media Selection Decision Tree (KTU Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Campaign Objective]) --> q1{Target Audience Geographics}
    q1 -- Urban --> q2{Message Complexity}
    q1 -- Rural --> rural[Use Radio and Outdoor and Local Print]
    q2 -- Simple --> tv[Use TV and OOH]
    q2 -- Complex --> digital[Use Long Form Digital Video]
    q2 -- Emotional --> cinema[Use Cinema and Influencer]
    rural --> budget{Budget Tier}
    tv --> budget
    digital --> budget
    cinema --> budget
    budget -- High --> mass[Mass Media Mix]
    budget -- Medium --> mix[Targeted Mix]
    budget -- Low --> low[Digital Only]
    mass --> metric[Measure via GRP and Reach]
    mix --> metric
    low --> metric
    metric --> end1[Optimize CPM and ROAS]
```

## 4.3 Three-Tier Impact Measurement Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    tier1[Tier 1 Cognitive Impact] --> m1[Ad Recall Tests]
    tier1 --> m2[Aided Recall Percentage]
    tier2[Tier 2 Affective Impact] --> m3[Brand Attitude Studies]
    tier2 --> m4[Message Believability Surveys]
    tier3[Tier 3 Conative Impact] --> m5[Coupon Redemption]
    tier3 --> m6[Sales Lift Analysis]
    tier3 --> m7[Website Traffic Attribution]
    m1 --> report[Integrated Marketing Report]
    m2 --> report
    m3 --> report
    m4 --> report
    m5 --> report
    m6 --> report
    m7 --> report
    report --> dec[Strategic Decision for Next Campaign]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper PEMET638]**
*Define advertising impact and list its three classifications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Advertising impact is the measurable effect of an advertising message on the target audience's mind and behaviour. The three classifications are:
1. **Cognitive Impact** — awareness, recognition, recall (knowledge-related),
2. **Affective Impact** — attitude, preference, emotional bonding (feeling-related), and
3. **Conative Impact** — action, trial, purchase (behaviour-related).
*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the three classifications.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary]**
*State any two functions of media in advertising.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Information Disseminator** — media transmits product facts, features, prices to the target market. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Persuasion and Image Builder** — media provides the editorial and emotional environment (music, visuals, story) that shapes brand image. *(1.5 marks)*
*(Any two of the five functional roles listed in Section 2.3 are accepted.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**
*(a)* Explain the **DAGMAR model** in detail. How does it help in measuring advertising impact? *(7 marks)*
*(b)* "Media is the lifeline of advertising." Discuss the **role of media** in modern advertising with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — DAGMAR Model (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Full Form & Origin (1 mark):**
DAGMAR stands for **D**efining **A**dvertising **G**oals for **M**easured **A**dvertising **R**esults. It was proposed by **Russell H. Colley in 1961** for the Association of National Advertisers (USA).

**Step 2 — Core Premise (1 mark):**
Advertising is a *communication task*, not a sales task. Its goal is to move the prospect through a hierarchy of well-defined communication states that are *measurable*.

**Step 3 — The Four Communication Stages (4 marks):**

$$
\text{Unawareness} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Awareness} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Comprehension} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Conviction} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Action}
$$

- **Awareness** — the prospect first knows the brand exists. Measured via *aided/unaided recall*.
- **Comprehension** — the prospect understands what the product does and its key benefit. Measured via *message recall tests*.
- **Conviction** — the prospect develops a mental preference and a positive attitude. Measured via *attitude scales*.
- **Action** — the prospect actually purchases, inquires, or visits. Measured via *coupon redemption, sales lift*.

**Step 4 — Linkage to Impact (1 mark):**
- Awareness & Comprehension → **Cognitive Impact**
- Conviction → **Affective Impact**
- Action → **Conative Impact**

**Part (b) — Role of Media (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
Media is the carrier or channel that delivers the advertiser's message to the target audience. It is the *bridge* between the ad creative and the consumer.

**Step 2 — Five Roles with Examples (5 marks, 1 mark each):**

| Role | Explanation | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| Information Disseminator | Conveys product facts, features, prices | A newspaper ad announcing a Big Bazaar sale |
| Persuasion Engine | Uses visuals, music, story to influence attitude | A Royal Enfield TVC evoking adventure and freedom |
| Reminder Trigger | Keeps brand top-of-mind between purchases | A Coca-Cola hoarding on NH-66 reminding daily commuters |
| Image Builder | Associates brand with premium status or values | A Tanishq Diwali ad positioning jewellery as modern Indian sentiment |
| Distribution of Demand | Smooths seasonal/geographic sales swings | A Flipkart Big Billion Days digital campaign driving tier-2 Kerala sales |

**Step 3 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
Without an apt media choice, even the most creative ad is dead. Media is the *operational arm* of advertising, converting creative intent into commercial impact.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Expected Pattern]**
*(a)* Differentiate between **cognitive, affective, and conative impact** of advertising with examples. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* Calculate **Reach, Frequency, GRP, and CPM** for the following case: A South Indian FMCG brand spends **₹9 lakh** on a regional TV campaign that delivers **45 lakh impressions** to a target population of **15 lakh**, of which **9 lakh** are unique viewers. Comment on the cost efficiency. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Three Impact Types (7 marks, 2 marks each + 1 conclusion):**

**1. Cognitive Impact** — *What the consumer knows.*
It relates to the mental learning of information — brand awareness, recognition, recall.
*Example:* A child identifying the Amul "Utterly Butterly Girl" hoarding = cognitive impact.

**2. Affective Impact** — *What the consumer feels.*
It relates to attitude, preference, emotional bonding, brand likability.
*Example:* A consumer feeling nostalgic and proud after the Vicks "Touch of Care" ad = affective impact.

**3. Conative Impact** — *What the consumer does.*
It relates to behavioral intent and action — inquiry, trial, purchase.
*Example:* A customer using a coupon code seen in a Zomato app ad = conative impact.

| Dimension | Cognitive | Affective | Conative |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nature | Rational | Emotional | Behavioral |
| Question Answered | "Do I know it?" | "Do I like it?" | "Did I buy it?" |
| Best Media | Outdoor, TV | Cinema, Influencer | Digital, Direct Mail |
| Metric | Ad Recall % | Brand Favorability | Sales Lift % |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** A balanced advertising campaign must engineer impact across all three dimensions, and the choice of media must be aligned to the dominant impact sought.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Total Target Population = 15,00,000
- Unique Viewers (Reach in absolute) = 9,00,000
- Total Impressions = 45,00,000
- Cost = ₹9,00,000

**Step 1 — Reach % (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Reach}_{\%} = \dfrac{9{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 60\%
$$

> *Text row: 60% of the target market in Kerala was exposed to the ad at least once.*

**Step 2 — Frequency (2 marks):**

$$
F = \dfrac{45{,}00{,}000}{9{,}00{,}000} = 5
$$

> *Text row: On average, each unique viewer saw the ad 5 times during the campaign window.*

**Step 3 — GRP (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{GRP} = 60 \times 5 = 300
$$

> *Text row: The campaign delivered 300 gross rating points, indicating heavy ad weight.*

**Step 4 — CPM (1 mark):**

$$
\text{CPM} = \dfrac{9{,}00{,}000}{45{,}00{,}000} \times 1000 = \text{₹ } 200
$$

> *Text row: Cost per thousand impressions is ₹200, which is competitive for regional South Indian TV inventory.*

**Step 5 — Cost-Efficiency Comment (0.5 mark):**
A CPM of ₹200 along with a GRP of 300 is considered *cost-efficient* for a regional FMCG launch. The plan achieves healthy reach (60%) with a strong frequency (5), satisfying the classic **3-exposure effective-frequency rule**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing Reach with Impressions.** Reach = unique audience; Impressions = total exposures. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks in numericals.
> 2. **Forgetting the × 1000 in CPM.** Most students write CPM = Cost/Impressions and lose 1 mark. Always multiply by 1000.
> 3. **Not mapping media to impact type.** When asked "which media for affective impact", students write "TV" generically. Specify *TV + Cinema* for emotional storytelling.
> 4. **Skipping the conclusion line.** KTU board examiners reserve 1 mark for an analytical/interpretive comment after any numerical solution.
> 5. **Writing GRP incorrectly.** GRP is dimensionless, but it must be expressed as *Reach (%) × Frequency* — never as an absolute number.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Advertising Impact** = Cognitive + Affective + Conative effects on the consumer.
- **Media's role** is to *carry, persuade, remind, image-build, and distribute demand* — not just deliver a message.
- **DAGMAR** = Unawareness → Awareness → Comprehension → Conviction → Action (a measurable sequence).
- **AIDA** = Attention → Interest → Desire → Action (a parallel hierarchy mapped to media types).
- **Key formulas** (must be memorized cold):
  - $\text{CPM} = \dfrac{C}{I} \times 1000$
  - $\text{GRP} = R_{\%} \times F$
  - $F = \dfrac{\text{Impressions}}{\text{Unique Audience}}$
  - $\text{ROAS} = \dfrac{\text{Revenue}}{\text{Ad Spend}} \times 100$
- **Best media for cognitive impact** = Outdoor, TV; **affective** = Cinema, Influencer; **conative** = Digital, Direct Mail.
- **Law of Diminishing Returns** applies — beyond a saturation point, more ad spend yields less incremental impact.
- **Effective Frequency Rule (Herbert Krugman, 1965)** = 3 exposures to a brand message is the minimum for impact; 3+ exposures create memorability.
- **Always end a numerical answer with a one-line interpretive comment** for the final 0.5–1 mark from the examiner.
- **Avoid the words "obviously" and "clearly"** in KTU answers — show, don't assume, the reasoning.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advertisement Restrictions & Legal Actions; Direct & Internet Marketing: Direct Marketing Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET638 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advertisement Restrictions & Legal Actions; Direct & Internet Marketing: Direct Marketing Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1. Advertisement Restrictions & Legal Actions

### Formal Definition
**Advertisement Restrictions & Legal Actions** refer to the statutory, regulatory, and self-regulatory frameworks that govern the content, form, medium, and dissemination of advertisements. In the Indian context, this encompasses the **Advertising Standards Council of India (ASCI) Code**, the **Consumer Protection Act (CPA), 2019**, the **Information Technology Act, 2000 (amended 2008)**, the **Cigarettes and Other Tobacco Products Act (COTPA), 2003**, the **Drugs and Magic Remedies (Objectionable Advertisements) Act, 1954**, the **Indian Contract Act, 1872**, and the **Intellectual Property laws**. These frameworks prohibit misleading advertisements, unsubstantiated claims, surrogate advertising, advertisements harmful to children, and unfair trade practices, while prescribing penalties, withdrawal orders, and corrective mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the Consumer Protection Act 2019, a "Misleading Advertisement" attracts a penalty of up to **₹10 lakh** for the first offence and up to **₹50 lakh** for repeat offences, with the **Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA)** empowered to issue orders for modification or withdrawal.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a marketplace where every shopkeeper screams that their product is "the best in the world." Without rules, customers would be perpetually deceived. **Advertisement Restrictions act like the "traffic police" of the marketing world** — they don't ban advertising; they simply enforce lane discipline so that no advertiser runs a red light by making false claims, exploiting children, or harming public health. **Legal Actions are the courtroom** where the violator is tried and penalized. Together, they ensure that the marketplace of ideas remains fair, truthful, and consumer-centric.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Decoded:**
> - **Surrogate Advertising:** Indirectly promoting a banned product (e.g., alcohol/tobacco) through a similar-looking brand of a permitted product (e.g., bottled water, music CDs).
> - **Substantiation:** The advertiser's legal obligation to provide scientific, technical, or factual proof for every objective claim made in an advertisement.

---

## 2. Direct & Internet Marketing

### Formal Definition
**Direct Marketing** is a promotional method in which marketers communicate directly with pre-identified individual customers or target segments through non-personal media (mail, telephone, email, catalogues) to elicit a measurable response, transaction, or visit, without the intermediary of a retail outlet or mass-media channel.

**Internet Marketing (Digital Marketing)** is the subset of direct marketing that uses internet-based digital channels — search engines, websites, social media platforms, email, mobile applications, and display networks — to deliver promotional content, build customer relationships, and convert prospects into buyers in real time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Definition (Direct Marketing Association – DMA):** *"Direct Marketing is an interactive system of marketing which uses one or more advertising media to effect a measurable response and/or transaction at any location."*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of **Traditional Marketing** as shouting through a megaphone to a crowded stadium — you reach thousands, but most don't listen, and you never know who heard you. **Direct Marketing is like slipping a personal, handwritten letter into a specific customer's mailbox** — you know exactly who received it, and they can respond directly. **Internet Marketing is the same letter, but now it's interactive, instantaneous, and trackable** — you know not only who opened it but also which line they paused on, which link they clicked, and whether they bought the product, all in real time.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 4 Pillars of Direct Marketing (Kotler's Framework):**
> 1. **An identifiable customer** — the recipient is known.
> 2. **A measurable response** — every action is trackable.
> 3. **No middleman** — the brand reaches the buyer directly.
> 4. **A call-to-action (CTA)** — the customer is invited to respond immediately.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. Advertisement Restrictions — Legal Framework Architecture

### A. Self-Regulatory Framework: ASCI (Advertising Standards Council of India)

Founded in **1985**, ASCI is a voluntary, non-statutory body that processes complaints against misleading or offensive advertisements. Its **Code of Self-Regulation** contains 11 chapters covering:

| Chapter | Scope |
|---------|-------|
| Chapter I | General Code of Conduct |
| Chapter II | Specific Guidelines (Truthfulness, Honesty) |
| Chapter III | Decency & Obscenity |
| Chapter IV | Advertisements Targeted at Children |
| Chapter V | Advertisements on Health & Fitness |
| Chapter VI | Education & Career Guidance Ads |
| Chapter VII | Financial Products & Investment Schemes |
| Chapter VIII | Real Estate Advertising |
| Chapter IX | Alcoholic Beverages |
| Chapter X | Tobacco Products |
| Chapter XI | Environmental Claims (Greenwashing) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ASCI vs. CCPA — The Hierarchy of Enforcement:**
> ASCI issues **recommendations** (moral suasion). CCPA under CPA 2019 issues **binding orders** with monetary penalties. Non-compliance with CCPA is punishable as a cognizable offence.

### B. Statutory Framework (Hard Law)

| Statute | Year | Key Provision | Penalty |
|---------|------|---------------|---------|
| Consumer Protection Act (CPA) | 2019 | Sec. 2(28): Misleading Advertisement definition; Sec. 89–94: CCPA powers | Up to **₹10 lakh** first offence; **₹50 lakh** repeat |
| Drugs and Magic Remedies Act | 1954 | Prohibits ads for magic cures for diseases | Fine up to **₹5,000**; imprisonment up to **6 months** |
| COTPA | 2003 | Bans direct/indirect tobacco advertising; mandates pictorial warnings | Fine up to **₹1,000** (first); up to **₹5,000** (repeat) |
| Information Technology Act | 2000 (2008) | Sec. 66A (struck down), Sec. 69A: Blocking of offensive content | Imprisonment up to **3 years** |
| Indian Contract Act | 1872 | Sec. 17–19: Misrepresentation as ground for voiding contract | Civil remedy |
| Cable Television Networks Act | 1995 | Rule 7: Prohibits transmission of certain ads | Suspension of broadcaster license |
| Trademarks Act | 1999 | Comparative advertising & brand disparagement | Civil suit + injunction |
| MRTP Act (repealed) → replaced by Competition Act | 2002 | Sec. 3 & 4: Anti-competitive agreements & abuse of dominance | Penalty up to **10% of turnover** |

### C. Categories of Prohibited / Restricted Advertisements

1. **Misleading Advertisements** — false claims, unproven superlatives, hidden costs, bait-and-switch.
2. **Surrogate Advertising** — using permitted products to push banned ones (e.g., "Kingfisher" water promoting "Kingfisher" beer).
3. **Comparative Advertising** — permitted *only if* the comparison is factual, verifiable, not confusing, and not disparaging.
4. **Children-Targeted Ads** — restricted under ASCI Chapter IV; cannot exploit children's credulity or lack of experience.
5. **Ads for Hazardous Products** — tobacco, liquor, gambling (state-level restrictions apply).
6. **Ads with Obscene/Offensive Content** — restricted under ASCI Chapter III and IT Act Sec. 67.
7. **Endorsements** — under **ASCI Guidelines on Influencer Advertising (2023)**, every paid endorsement must carry a "Material Connection" disclosure (e.g., "Sponsored," "Advertisement," "Paid Partnership").

---

## 2. Direct Marketing Techniques — Detailed Taxonomy

### A. Traditional Direct Marketing Techniques

| Technique | Mechanism | Key Feature |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Direct Mail (DM)** | Printed promotional material posted to a known prospect list | Highest personalisation; measurable |
| **Catalogue Marketing** | Printed or digital catalogues mailed/emailed to customers | Used by retailers (e.g., Marks & Spencer, IKEA) |
| **Telemarketing** | Outbound/inbound phone-based selling | Real-time interaction; requires Do-Not-Call compliance |
| **Direct-Response Advertising** | TV/Radio/Print ads with toll-free numbers or coupon codes | Bridges mass media and direct marketing |
| **Inserts & Flyers** | Loose sheets packaged with newspapers/magazines | Low cost per reach |
| **Door-to-Door Selling** | Personal sales calls at residences | High conversion in B2C durables |

### B. Digital / Internet Marketing Techniques

| Technique | Description | Key Metric |
|-----------|-------------|------------|
| **Email Marketing** | Permission-based promotional emails | Open Rate, CTR (Click-Through Rate) |
| **Search Engine Optimisation (SEO)** | Organic ranking of website on SERPs | Keyword ranking, Organic Traffic |
| **Search Engine Marketing (SEM)** | Paid ads on Google/Bing (PPC) | CPC, ROAS |
| **Social Media Marketing (SMM)** | Promotion on Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, X | Engagement Rate, Reach |
| **Content Marketing** | Blogs, articles, videos, infographics | Time-on-page, Backlinks |
| **Influencer Marketing** | Endorsement by online creators | Impressions, Conversions |
| **Affiliate Marketing** | Commission-based third-party promoters | CPA (Cost per Acquisition) |
| **Mobile Marketing** | SMS, MMS, in-app ads, push notifications | App installs, CTR |
| **Webinar/Online Events** | Live educational/promotional sessions | Registrations, Attendance |
| **Programmatic Advertising** | Automated, AI-driven ad buying | eCPM, Viewability |

### C. The RACE Framework for Digital Marketing

$$RACE = \text{Reach} + \text{Act} + \text{Convert} + \text{Engage}$$

1. **Reach** — Build awareness through SEO, ads, social media.
2. **Act** — Drive interaction (likes, downloads, sign-ups).
3. **Convert** — Turn visitors into buyers through landing pages, CTAs.
4. **Engage** — Retain customers through email, loyalty programs, communities.

---

## 3. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Direct Marketing Performance Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Unit |
|--------|---------|------|
| **Response Rate (RR)** | $\displaystyle RR = \frac{\text{Number of Responses}}{\text{Total Pieces Delivered}} \times 100$ | % |
| **Conversion Rate (CR)** | $\displaystyle CR = \frac{\text{Number of Purchases}}{\text{Number of Responses}} \times 100$ | % |
| **Cost per Order (CPO)** | $\displaystyle CPO = \frac{\text{Total Campaign Cost}}{\text{Number of Orders}}$ | ₹/order |
| **Cost per Acquisition (CPA)** | $\displaystyle CPA = \frac{\text{Total Marketing Spend}}{\text{Number of New Customers}}$ | ₹/customer |
| **Return on Marketing Investment (ROMI)** | $\displaystyle ROMI = \frac{\text{Revenue from Campaign} - \text{Campaign Cost}}{\text{Campaign Cost}} \times 100$ | % |
| **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | $\displaystyle CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(\text{Avg Order Value} \times \text{Purchase Frequency})}{(1 + r)^t}$ | ₹ |
| **Break-Even Response Rate (BERR)** | $\displaystyle BERR = \frac{\text{Cost per Piece Mailed}}{\text{Profit per Order}} \times 100$ | % |
| **Email Open Rate** | $\displaystyle EOR = \frac{\text{Unique Opens}}{\text{Emails Delivered}} \times 100$ | % |
| **Click-Through Rate (CTR)** | $\displaystyle CTR = \frac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 100$ | % |
| **Bounce Rate** | $\displaystyle BR = \frac{\text{Single-Page Sessions}}{\text{Total Sessions}} \times 100$ | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When solving numerical problems in marketing management, always state the formula first, substitute the values, and then provide the final numerical answer with the correct unit. Marks are awarded for each step.

### Legal Action Decision Matrix

| Trigger Event | First Step | Authority |
|---------------|-----------|-----------|
| Misleading ad aired | File complaint on **ASCI portal** | ASCI Consumer Complaints Council (CCC) |
| Misleading ad — financial loss suffered | File complaint under **Sec. 35 of CPA 2019** | District/State/National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission |
| Systemic misleading ads across industry | CCPA suo-motu action | **Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA)** |
| Defamatory comparative ad | Civil suit for injunction + damages | Civil Court / High Court |
| Online offensive content | Complaint to **Grievance Officer** of intermediary | IT Act Sec. 69A → MeitY |
| Tobacco surrogate ad | Complaint to **State Tobacco Control Cell** | COTPA enforcement |

### Real-World Utility in Marketing

The metrics above are deployed daily by marketing professionals in:
- **Budget allocation** between digital and traditional channels.
- **A/B testing** of email subject lines, landing pages, and ad creatives.
- **Lead scoring** in CRM systems like Salesforce, HubSpot.
- **Compliance auditing** by legal teams to pre-screen ads before launch.
- **Strategic planning** to optimise ROMI during festive sales (Diwali, Black Friday).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Worked Example 1: Break-Even Response Rate (BERR) for a Direct Mail Campaign

A jewellery brand plans a direct mail campaign with the following parameters:

- Cost per piece mailed (printing + postage): **₹50**
- Average profit per order generated: **₹2,500**
- Total pieces to be mailed: **20,000**

**Find:**
1. The Break-Even Response Rate (BERR).
2. The number of responses required to break even.
3. If the campaign yields 600 orders, calculate the **ROMI**.

### Step 1: Identify the Formulae

For BERR, the fundamental principle is that **total revenue must equal total cost**. Each mailed piece costs ₹50, and each response yields ₹2,500 in profit. Therefore, the minimum percentage of responses required to cover the mailing cost is:

$$BERR = \frac{\text{Cost per Piece Mailed}}{\text{Profit per Order}} \times 100$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Values

$$BERR = \frac{50}{2500} \times 100$$

$$BERR = \frac{1}{50} \times 100$$

$$BERR = 2\%$$

### Step 3: Calculate Number of Responses for Break-Even

$$\text{Break-Even Responses} = \text{Total Pieces Mailed} \times \frac{BERR}{100}$$

$$\text{Break-Even Responses} = 20{,}000 \times \frac{2}{100}$$

$$\text{Break-Even Responses} = 400 \text{ responses}$$

### Step 4: Compute ROMI When Actual Responses = 600

First, compute total campaign cost:

$$\text{Total Cost} = \text{Cost per Piece} \times \text{Total Pieces} = 50 \times 20{,}000 = ₹10{,}00{,}000$$

Next, compute total revenue from 600 orders:

$$\text{Total Revenue} = 600 \times 2500 = ₹15{,}00{,}000$$

Now apply the ROMI formula:

$$ROMI = \frac{\text{Revenue from Campaign} - \text{Campaign Cost}}{\text{Campaign Cost}} \times 100$$

$$ROMI = \frac{15{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 100$$

$$ROMI = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 100$$

$$ROMI = 50\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A 50% ROMI means that for every ₹1 spent on the campaign, the company earns ₹1.50 as net profit. Since the actual response (600) exceeds the break-even threshold (400), the campaign is **profitable by ₹5,00,000**.

---

## Worked Example 2: CPA and CLV Calculation for Internet Marketing

An ed-tech startup runs a Google Ads (SEM) campaign with the following data:

- Monthly ad spend: **₹3,00,000**
- Click-Through Rate (CTR): **5%**
- Impressions served: **2,00,000**
- Conversion Rate (website visitor to paying customer): **2%**
- Average order value (AOV): **₹8,000**
- Average customer purchases per year: **3 times**
- Customer lifespan: **4 years**
- Discount rate (r): **10% per annum**

**Find:**
1. Cost per Click (CPC).
2. Cost per Acquisition (CPA).
3. Customer Lifetime Value (CLV).
4. Whether the campaign is profitable based on CLV vs. CPA.

### Step 1: Cost per Click (CPC)

$$\text{Clicks} = \text{Impressions} \times \frac{CTR}{100} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{5}{100} = 10{,}000$$

$$CPC = \frac{\text{Total Ad Spend}}{\text{Total Clicks}} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{10{,}000} = ₹30 \text{ per click}$$

### Step 2: Cost per Acquisition (CPA)

First, compute the number of customers acquired:

$$\text{Customers Acquired} = \text{Clicks} \times \frac{\text{Conversion Rate}}{100}$$

$$\text{Customers Acquired} = 10{,}000 \times \frac{2}{100} = 200$$

$$CPA = \frac{\text{Total Ad Spend}}{\text{New Customers}} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{200} = ₹1{,}500 \text{ per customer}$$

### Step 3: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)

The simplified CLV formula for constant annual profit:

$$CLV = \text{AOV} \times \text{Purchase Frequency} \times \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{1}{(1+r)^t}$$

Compute the discount factor summation:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{4} \frac{1}{(1.10)^t} = \frac{1}{1.10} + \frac{1}{1.21} + \frac{1}{1.331} + \frac{1}{1.4641}$$

Computing each term step-by-step:

$$\frac{1}{1.10} = 0.9091$$

$$\frac{1}{1.21} = 0.8264$$

$$\frac{1}{1.331} = 0.7513$$

$$\frac{1}{1.4641} = 0.6830$$

Summing:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{4} \frac{1}{(1.10)^t} = 0.9091 + 0.8264 + 0.7513 + 0.6830 = 3.1698$$

Now substitute back:

$$CLV = 8{,}000 \times 3 \times 3.1698$$

$$CLV = 24{,}000 \times 3.1698$$

$$CLV = ₹76{,}075.20$$

### Step 4: Profitability Assessment

$$\text{Net Profit per Customer} = CLV - CPA = 76{,}075.20 - 1{,}500 = ₹74{,}575.20$$

$$\text{CLV to CPA Ratio} = \frac{76{,}075.20}{1{,}500} = 50.72$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule (KTU Standard):** A CLV:CPA ratio **≥ 3:1** is considered a healthy, profitable marketing campaign. Here, the ratio is **50.72:1**, indicating the ed-tech startup's SEM campaign is **extremely profitable** and scalable. The marketing team can confidently increase the ad budget to acquire more customers.

---

## Worked Example 3: Email Campaign Performance Audit

A SaaS company sent **50,000 emails** as part of a product launch. The analytics dashboard reports:

- Unique opens: **12,500**
- Unique clicks: **2,500**
- Unsubscribes: **150**
- Conversions (purchases): **200**
- Average order value: **₹4,500**

**Find:** Open Rate, Click-Through Rate (CTR), Click-to-Open Rate (CTOR), Conversion Rate, and Total Revenue.

### Step 1: Email Open Rate (EOR)

$$EOR = \frac{\text{Unique Opens}}{\text{Emails Delivered}} \times 100 = \frac{12{,}500}{50{,}000} \times 100 = 25\%$$

### Step 2: Click-Through Rate (CTR)

$$CTR = \frac{\text{Unique Clicks}}{\text{Emails Delivered}} \times 100 = \frac{2{,}500}{50{,}000} \times 100 = 5\%$$

### Step 3: Click-to-Open Rate (CTOR)

$$CTOR = \frac{\text{Unique Clicks}}{\text{Unique Opens}} \times 100 = \frac{2{,}500}{12{,}500} \times 100 = 20\%$$

### Step 4: Conversion Rate

$$CR = \frac{\text{Conversions}}{\text{Unique Clicks}} \times 100 = \frac{200}{2{,}500} \times 100 = 8\%$$

### Step 5: Total Revenue

$$\text{Total Revenue} = \text{Conversions} \times AOV = 200 \times 4{,}500 = ₹9{,}00{,}000$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Benchmark (KTU Reference):** Average email open rate across industries ≈ **21.33%** (2024 Statista data). Average CTR ≈ **2.62%**. Average CTOR ≈ **10.5%**. The campaign above **outperforms all benchmarks**, indicating strong subject lines, relevant content, and a well-segmented list.

---

## Analytical Matrix: Comparative Legal Frameworks

| Dimension | ASCI (Self-Regulation) | Consumer Protection Act 2019 | IT Act 2000/2008 |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------------|------------------|
| **Nature** | Voluntary | Statutory | Statutory |
| **Scope** | All advertisements | Misleading ads causing harm | Online content, cybercrime |
| **Penalty** | Withdrawal/modification (no fine) | Up to ₹50 lakh + imprisonment up to 2 years | Up to 7 years imprisonment |
| **Speed** | 30–60 days | 3–6 months | Variable |
| **Binding** | No (moral suasion) | Yes (court-enforced) | Yes |
| **Examples** | Dabur Red Paste (objectionable), Maggi (2015) | Vyapar scam, CCPA action on coaching ads | Section 144 blocking orders during COVID |

## Analytical Matrix: Direct Marketing vs. Internet Marketing

| Dimension | Direct Marketing (Traditional) | Internet Marketing (Digital) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Medium** | Mail, phone, catalogue | Web, email, mobile, social |
| **Cost per Reach** | Higher (printing, postage) | Lower (CPC, CPM) |
| **Speed** | Days to weeks | Seconds to minutes |
| **Measurability** | Moderate (coupons, codes) | High (pixel tracking, analytics) |
| **Personalisation** | Moderate (name, address) | High (dynamic content, AI-driven) |
| **Two-way Interaction** | Limited (phone, reply cards) | Real-time (chatbots, comments, DMs) |
| **Regulation** | Do-Not-Call registry (TCPA in US, TRAI in India) | GDPR, IT Act, CCPA, ASCI influencer rules |
| **Examples** | Amazon catalogue, LIC direct mail | Amazon email, Swiggy push notifications |
